elements.sent-2411-023: This dissertation explores the area of real-time IP networking for embedded devices, especially those with limited computational resources.
elements.sent-2411-024: With the increasing convergence of information and operational technologies in various industries, and the growing complexity of communication requirements in (semi-)autonomous machines, there is a need for more advanced and reliable networking solutions.
elements.sent-2411-025: This research focuses on the challenge of integrating real-time embedded devices into packet-switched networks.
elements.sent-2411-026: Through a comprehensive review of current real-time communication technologies, standards, and practices in the context of Industry 4.0, a notable gap is identified: the lack of a robust real-time communication standard tailored for wireless mobile machines, and insufficient research on real-time embedded devices in highly networked environments.
elements.sent-2411-027: The study includes detailed experimentation with commercially available off-the-shelf networked microcontrollers, revealing a priority inversion problem where network packet processing interrupts real-time tasks, potentially causing real-time violations.
elements.sent-2411-028: To address this challenge, this thesis proposes mitigation methods and system designs that include software and hardware implementations.
elements.sent-2411-029: These include a new embedded network subsystem that prioritizes packet processing based on task priority, and a real-time-aware network interface controller that moderates interrupt requests.
elements.sent-2411-030: In addition, a hybrid hardware-software co-design approach is developed to ensure predictable and reliable real-time task execution despite network congestion.
elements.sent-2411-031: Furthermore, the research extends to task offloading in wireless Industrial Internet of Things environments, presenting a system architecture and scheduler capable of maintaining real-time constraints even under heavy loads and network uncertainties.
elements.sent-2411-032: Diese Dissertation untersucht den Bereich der Echtzeit-IP-Vernetzung für eingebettete Geräte, insbesondere solche mit begrenzten Rechenressourcen.
elements.sent-2411-033: Angesichts der zunehmenden Konvergenz von Informations- und Betriebstechnologien in verschiedenen Branchen und der wachsenden Komplexität der Kommunikationsanforderungen in (halb-)autonomen Maschinen besteht ein Bedarf an fortschrittlicheren und zuverlässigeren Netzwerklösungen.
elements.sent-2411-034: Diese Forschungsarbeit konzentriert sich auf die Herausforderung der Integration von echtzeitfähigen eingebetteten Geräten in paketvermittelte Netzwerke.
elements.sent-2411-035: Durch eine umfassende Auswertung aktueller Echtzeit-Kommunikationstechnologien, Standards und Praktiken im Kontext von Industrie 4.0 wird eine wesentliche Lücke identifiziert: das Fehlen eines robusten Echtzeit-Kommunikationsstandards, der auf drahtlose mobile Maschinen zugeschnitten ist, und unzureichende Forschung zu eingebetteten Echtzeit-Geräten in stark vernetzten Umgebungen.
elements.sent-2411-036: Die Studie umfasst detaillierte Experimente mit handelsüblichen vernetzten Mikrocontrollern, die ein Problem der Prioritätsumkehrung aufzeigen, bei dem die Verarbeitung von Netzwerkpaketen Echtzeitaufgaben unterbricht, was zu dem Verpassen von Deadlines führen kann.
elements.sent-2411-037: Um dieses Problem zu lösen, werden in dieser Arbeit Methoden und Systemdesigns vorgestellt, die sowohl Software- als auch Hardware-Implementierungen umfassen.
elements.sent-2411-038: Dazu gehören ein neues eingebettetes Netzwerk-Subsystem, das die Paketverarbeitung auf der Grundlage der Aufgabenpriorität priorisiert, und ein echtzeitfähiger Netzwerkschnittstellen-Controller, der Interrupt-Anfragen mäßigt.
elements.sent-2411-039: Darüber hinaus wird ein hybrider Hardware-Software-Co-Design-Ansatz entwickelt, der eine vorhersehbare und zuverlässige Echtzeit-Task-Ausführung trotz Netzwerküberlastung gewährleistet.
elements.sent-2411-040: Weitergehend befasst sich die Forschung mit der Auslagerung von Aufgaben in drahtlosen Umgebungen des industriellen Internets der Dinge und stellt eine Systemarchitektur und einen Scheduler vor, die in der Lage sind, Echtzeitbedingungen auch bei hoher Belastung und Netzwerkunsicherheiten einzuhalten.
elements.sent-2411-041: In such scenarios, real-time systems are used to enable the digital control of physical movements.
elements.sent-2411-042: In contrast to general-purpose computing, these systems need to fulfill timing constraints in a predictable manner.
elements.sent-2411-043: Real-time systems are computing systems designed to process and respond to data with precise timing constraints, which is crucial for industrial automation and control applications.
elements.sent-2411-044: These systems ensure that critical tasks are executed within specified time intervals, guaranteeing reliability and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-045: Examples include \acp{plc in manufacturing lines, Distributed Control Systems in chemical plants, and \acp{mcu in industrial robots.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: In short, anywhere, where timely and accurate responses to external triggers are essential for operational success~\cite{wang2017real .}
elements.sent-2411-047: Machines such as robot arms, logistic robots, vehicles, and conveyor belts not only have timing requirements for propper functionality but also need to reliably guarantee control task deadlines for safety purposes.
elements.sent-2411-048: Depending on the exact use-case, reliable reaction times in the milli and nano second ranges are required to prevent catastrophic consequences~\cite{schweitzer2016millisecond, hamdan2022using, paventhan2022latency .}
elements.sent-2411-049: The advent of the Fourth Industrial Revolution (Industry 4.0), the \ac{iiot , and autonomous vehicles has ushered in a transformative era where interconnected embedded real-time systems play a pivotal role in shaping the landscape of modern industries.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Many novel applications involve deploying a large number of devices, making cost-effectiveness a vital requirement.
elements.sent-2411-051: For devices deployed in mobile machines or remote locations their power consumption also plays a significant role, leading to longer CPU cycles and slower memory access.
elements.sent-2411-052: Another factor leading to this is the oftentimes small form factor required by embedded devices.
elements.sent-2411-053: Yet, the simplicity of embedded real-time devices is also due to the safety-criticality of their applications.
elements.sent-2411-054: Traditionally, a device is not used for more than one specific control or monitoring task.
elements.sent-2411-055: This specificity leads to simpler architectures and better verifiability~\cite{yen1998performance .}
elements.sent-2411-056: Hence, even when cost or energy-efficiency are of no concern, using more complex devices with advanced parallelization or heterogeneous architectures might not be a feasible option.
elements.sent-2411-057: Therefore, the embedded devices used in real-time systems are oftentimes resource contrained and computationally weak.
elements.sent-2411-058: The availability of cost-effective microcontrollers as well as advances in communication and artificial intelligence technologies enables scenarios requiring flexible connectivity and high data rates in real-time contexts leading to a convergence between \ac{it and \ac{ot ~\cite{ehie2020understanding .}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: This convergence is driven by the increasing need for seamless communication and data exchange between traditionally isolated IT systems (handling enterprise-level data) and OT systems (managing industrial processes and control).
elements.sent-2411-060: The goal is to create a unified ecosystem that enhances operational efficiency, facilitates data-driven decision-making, and enables a more responsive and agile industrial infrastructure~\cite{aakerberg2021future .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Factors such as Industry 4.0 initiatives, the rise of smart manufacturing, and the demand for real-time insights have catalyzed the convergence of IT and OT, breaking down silos and fostering a more connected and intelligent industrial landscape.
elements.sent-2411-062: Examples for IT/OT convergence are applicable across diverse industries, such as:.
elements.sent-2411-063: This integration allows for real-time monitoring of manufacturing operations, predictive maintenance, and adaptive production planning~\cite{ehie2020understanding .}
elements.sent-2411-064: This convergence facilitates better monitoring of energy consumption, predictive maintenance of equipment, and optimization of energy efficiency~\cite{ahmad2021using .}
elements.sent-2411-065: This integration ensures secure and seamless communication between IT systems managing patient data and OT systems controlling medical equipment~\cite{kodali2015implementation .}
elements.sent-2411-066: The given examples show how new advancements in many industries lead to or are dependent on a flexible connection between real-time embedded systems and management networks.
elements.sent-2411-067: Since all bigger networks utilize the \ac{ip and are connected to the internet, this means that for most new scenarios the real-time systems of the OT-layer are also connected via IP~\cite{xu2018survey .}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Hence, packet-switched IP networking is or might be used for communciation between sensors, actuators, and controling components in safety-relevant real-time systems like Power Plants for stability control, Medical Devices for life support systems and robotic surgery, and Industrial Control Systems for remote control of moving machinery.
elements.sent-2411-069: As a packet-switched communication technology, IP is not designed to provide timing predictability.
elements.sent-2411-070: The larger and more complex a network becomes, the higher the variance of latencies gets.
elements.sent-2411-071: While new technologies enable a new scenarios, a number of challenges has to be overcome before many of the envisioned scenarios in Industry 4.0 and autonomous machinery can be achieved.
elements.sent-2411-072: Additionally, the paradigm of IT/OT convergence might introduce new safety and security risks to real-time systems.
elements.sent-2411-073: Integrating IP networking into resource-constrained real-time embedded systems introduces complexity and unpredictable communication overhead.
elements.sent-2411-074: Furthermore, the introduction of \acp{nic and IP subsystems to embedded real-time devices creates computational workload and a new input sources to secure and handle.}
elements.sent-2411-075: This thesis focuses on scientific findings and approaches in the area of IP communication in resource-constrained real-time systems.
elements.sent-2411-076: The objective of this thesis is to \begin{center "Mitigate the real-time violation of IP networking in resource-constrained embedded systems and provide methods for real-time aware networking for them." \end{center This is addressed by decomposing the objective into the following four components.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: Incoming network packets are received and processed by computationally weak embedded devices.
elements.sent-2411-078: Real-time developers and researchers must be able to test their systems and quantify packet processing workloads under arbitrary network loads.
elements.sent-2411-079: Tipping points for real-time violations must be easily identifiable.
elements.sent-2411-080: Because network packets arrive in arbitrary intervals, real-time operating systems must be able to detect and respond to dangerous packet loads during run-time.
elements.sent-2411-081: This requires the identification of appropriate detection metrics and methods to reduce the amount of packet processing without disabling network tasks required for system functionality.
elements.sent-2411-082: Existing \acp{nic and network stack implementations for embedded systems are designed to be both simple and resource efficient.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Although they are typically combined with \acp{rtos , they lack real-time coordination.}
elements.sent-2411-084: The resulting preemption of real-time tasks can lead to real-time violations.
elements.sent-2411-085: Therefore, network subsystems on embedded real-time devices need to be made real-time aware and protected against network induced overloading.. \item Distribution.
elements.sent-2411-086: To overcome the limitations of computationally weak embedded devices, it is common practice to offload tasks to more powerful local computers.
elements.sent-2411-087: However, meeting real-time requirements in distributed systems is challenging, especially given the unpredictability of latencies in wireless networks with mobile devices.
elements.sent-2411-088: Managing real-time processes in distributed systems that include embedded devices, edge servers, and cloud computing requires a holistically designed architecture.. \end{compactenum.}
elements.sent-2411-089: This thesis proposes methods and architectures to protect resource-constrained real-time systems from IP networking-caused timing violations.
elements.paragraph-2411-001: 1. Methods for the analysis of real-time performance of networked embedded systems.
elements.sent-2411-091: The effect of processing loads on real-time performance caused by packet reception is difficult to quantify due to the arbitrary nature of packet reception and largely unstudied.
elements.sent-2411-092: We present a method for research and development of networked embedded systems to analyze the timing impact of packet processing.
elements.sent-2411-093: The method is applied to a computationally weak embedded system running a real-time operating system in various experimental setups to present a quantitative correlation between packet rate, schedulable processing time, and unschedulable \ac{isr executions.}
elements.paragraph-2411-002: 2. Algorithms for the detection and mitigation of network-caused real-time violations.
elements.sent-2411-094: This thesis introduces a network overload detection method and four reactive algorithms as an initial measure towards establishing a safer connection of resource constrained real-time devices to IP networks.
elements.sent-2411-095: These algorithms adaptively limit packet processing rates to protect critical real-time tasks from missing their deadlines.
elements.paragraph-2411-003: 3. Approaches for real-time aware packet reception and processing.
elements.sent-2411-096: Many embedded system deployment scenarios require low latency and timely response to sensor or control signals.
elements.sent-2411-097: Indiscriminately limiting packet processing can contradict this requirement.
elements.sent-2411-098: We develop an embedded real-time aware network subsystem that protects task deadlines while ensuring timely processing of time-critical packets.
elements.sent-2411-099: It uses early packet demultiplexing and classification based on real-time task priorities.
elements.sent-2411-100: By inheriting the priority of the packet-associated process, the network driver is prevented from preempting higher-priority tasks.
elements.sent-2411-101: Additional filtering and discriminatory rate limiting protects the system from network processing overload.
elements.sent-2411-102: A multiqueue real-time aware \ac{nic is implemented that demultiplexes and classifies received packets before \acp{irq are invoked.}}
elements.sent-2411-103: Packets are placed into priority-specific ingress queues with queue-specific interrupt moderation parameters.
elements.sent-2411-104: Interrupt moderation reduces the number of \acp{irq that are generated while prioritizing the timeliness of critical packets.}
elements.sent-2411-105: Additional filtering of unwanted packets further reduces unnecessary \acp{irq and \ac{isr executions.}}
elements.paragraph-2411-004: 4. Approaches for distributed real-time task offloading and scheduling.
elements.sent-2411-106: Embedded real-time systems are used in mobile, semi-autonomous machines connected over wireless networks.
elements.sent-2411-107: Due to resource constraints, real-time tasks may need to be offloaded to more powerful local machines.
elements.sent-2411-108: We present a distributed system architecture that includes a partitioned \ac{edf -based real-time scheduler.}
elements.sent-2411-109: Network latencies and their uncertainties, as well as current capacity utilization, are considered in scheduling to ensure timely result delivery and task rejection, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-110: This doctoral thesis makes contributions towards a safer deployment of IP networking within real-time systems.
elements.sent-2411-112: The scenarios are classified and their real-time networking requirements are derived.
elements.sent-2411-113: A review of standardized communication technologies for industrial networks is performed and their feasibility for the scenario classes is examined.
elements.sent-2411-114: Following, recent research in the field of real-time networking are surveyed and put into perspective of the derived real-time networking requirements.
elements.sent-2411-115: In multiple experiment setups the real-time behavior of computationally weak embedded devices is evaulated.
elements.sent-2411-116: It addresses the detection of deadline-violating packet loads and their mitigation.
elements.sent-2411-117: Further, an IP subsystem for real-time aware packet reception and processing is designed and evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-118: A multiqueue \ac{nic design is provided that differentiates incoming packets by mapping IP flows to real-time processes running on the system.}
elements.sent-2411-119: A \ac{nic simulator is implemented and used to test the viability of the design.}
elements.sent-2411-120: The individual designs are modified to interlock.
elements.sent-2411-121: The co-design is further implemented on an FPGA-SoC.
elements.sent-2411-122: A scheduler based on partitioned EDF is proposed that takes network latencies and uncertainties into account and distributes the offloaded tasks to worker machines so that their deadlines can be met.
elements.sent-2411-123: The architecture is distributed to remove the single point of failure of a centralized scheduler.
elements.sent-2411-124: The focus lies on interrupt management in real-tie systems, real-time aware packet processing, and distributed real-time scheduling as well as networking in industrial settings.
elements.sent-2411-125: These components form a technological foundation of the \ac{iiot , enabling seamless communication and coordination among interconnected devices.}
elements.sent-2411-126: This chapter explores the interplay between \acp{rtos , embedded networking subsystems, and IIoT considerations.}
elements.sent-2411-127: Section~\ref{sec:rtos provides the technical background of real-time operating systems before delving into the scheduling of real-time and aperiodic tasks and interrupts.}
elements.sent-2411-128: Section~\ref{sec:rx_path explains the receive path of embedded networking subsystems, starting at the network interface and ending with embedded IP stack implementations.}
elements.sent-2411-129: Section~\ref{sec:1_2_iiot covers some of the \ac{iiot considerations relevant to this thesis.}}
elements.sent-2411-130: Embedded systems often have special timing requirements.
elements.sent-2411-131: The correctness of these so-called real-time systems depends not only on the computation results, but also on the time at which the computation is completed.
elements.sent-2411-132: Tasks running on such systems have deadlines that must be met to avoid undesirable or even catastrophic behavior.
elements.sent-2411-133: For example, it is undesirable for a system to fail to respond to user input in a timely manner, but if the airbag controller of a car misses the deadline to deploy the airbag, the consequences can be severe~\cite{distributedRTOS .}
elements.sent-2411-134: Given these special requirements, an \ac{rtos can be distinguished from general-purpose \acp{os by a number of unique characteristics~\cite{RTOScharacteristics : \begin{compactitem \item Determinism \item Responsiveness \item Reliability \item User Control \item Fail-soft Operation \end{compactitem The system must be deterministic, meaning that the execution time of operations is known and independent of external factors.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-135: Responsiveness is the ability to respond quickly to an event, such as an I/O interrupt.
elements.sent-2411-136: In real-time systems, interrupt latency, i.e. the time between the generation of the interrupt signal and the execution of the interrupt service routine, is therefore a key feature.
elements.sent-2411-137: Furthermore, the need for high reliability in \acp{rtos requires measures to avoid system failures and reboots.}
elements.sent-2411-138: In general-purpose \acp{os , user-control is very limited by the kernel, but in \acp{rtos fine-grained control over task priorities and memory management is required.}}
elements.sent-2411-139: Finally, \acp{rtos strive for so-called Fail-soft operation.}
elements.sent-2411-140: While general-purpose \acp{os typically respond to critical failures by terminating system execution, \acp{rtos should isolate the problem and minimize its effects if possible~\cite{RTOScharacteristics .}}}
elements.sent-2411-141: Ultimately, these characteristics cannot be fully satisfied, but much effort has been made to develop \acp{rtos that come close.}
elements.sent-2411-142: General purpose \acp{os usually support virtual memory, including memory protection.}
elements.sent-2411-143: Embedded and \acp{rtos do not always support these features.}
elements.sent-2411-144: Memory protection is the concept of keeping address spaces separate between different contexts, such as the user space and the kernel space, or between different users.
elements.sent-2411-145: Without memory protection, \acp{rtos often do not differentiate between user and kernel space because embedded applications must be highly trustworthy and efficient.}
elements.sent-2411-146: However, some \acp{rtos support memory protection (e.g. LynxOS) or make memory protection an option when configuring the RTOS (e.g. VxWorks)~\cite{LiuJane .}}
elements.sent-2411-147: When using virtual memory, the physical memory is divided into frames of fixed length, each of which is mapped to a virtual page.
elements.sent-2411-148: The sum of all virtual pages is the virtual memory.
elements.sent-2411-149: Applications can only access virtual addresses, which are then translated into physical addresses by specialized hardware.
elements.sent-2411-150: This introduces an abstraction between the actual layout of memory space and the application's view of it.
elements.sent-2411-151: While this allows for more efficient use of available memory, it introduces computational overhead and requires a hardware \ac{mmu .}
elements.sent-2411-152: It is therefore often not implemented in \acp{rtos ~\cite{distributedRTOS .}}
elements.sent-2411-153: In addition, caches introduce uncertainty in memory access times and are therefore either not used in hard real-time systems or the worst-case memory access time must be known~\cite{rtcache .}
elements.sent-2411-154: One of the prevalent open-source \acp{rtos available is FreeRTOS\footnote{FreeRTOS Kernel Developer Docs and Kernel Secondary Docs, \url{https://www.freertos.org/ .}}}
elements.sent-2411-155: Initially developed by Richard Barry in 2003, the system is designed for low-power embedded devices with emphasis placed on a small size as well as easy maintainability and portability.
elements.sent-2411-156: Its scheduler supports preemptive or co-operative multitasking, with determinism achieved through user-assigned task priorities.
elements.sent-2411-157: The scheduler runs on a fixed internal tick rate, relying on a tick interrupt for performing task switches, which are based on the current task states and their assigned priorities.
elements.sent-2411-158: Strictly abiding by these priorities, a running task will only be preempted by a higher-priority task in ready state.
elements.sent-2411-159: Furthermore, a task will transition to the blocked state when required to wait for a shared resource or when a blocking API function is called.
elements.sent-2411-160: These include API calls around events, semaphores, queues and notifications.
elements.sent-2411-161: In case multiple tasks with the same priority are ready to transition into the running state, the scheduler will employ a round-robin scheme to schedule each one for a certain time period.
elements.sent-2411-162: Conversely, when no task is ready, FreeRTOS will switch the idle task into running mode, which is the the default task with the lowest-priority (priority 0).
elements.sent-2411-163: Furthermore, FreeRTOS offers high flexibility in assignment and modification of task priorities, enabling changes to the priorities of tasks to be made during runtime.
elements.sent-2411-164: As is typical in scheduling theory, we classify tasks by their timing requirements into~\cite{LiuJane : \begin{compactitem \item hard real-time tasks, whose utility depends entirely on their results being delivered on time.}}
elements.sent-2411-165: They are critical to the operation of the system, and missing deadlines can have catastrophic effects.
elements.sent-2411-166: Timing is still be somewhat desirable, but only as an end in itself.
elements.sent-2411-167: In addition, we use the following terms to describe different periodicities: \begin{compactitem \item periodic tasks are executed at regular intervals, their period $P_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-168: In addition, they may have a deadline before the end of their period.
elements.sent-2411-169: As aperiodic tasks, they do not arrive at regular times.
elements.sent-2411-170: However, there is a lower bound on their inter-arrival time, the minimum interval between two occurrences.
elements.sent-2411-171: Popular scheduling algorithms are e.g. \ac{rms and \ac{edf .}}
elements.sent-2411-172: In \ac{rms tasks with a short period are assigned a high priority, while in \ac{edf the task with the earliest deadline are assigned the highest priority~\cite{rtsched .}}}
elements.sent-2411-173: In a fixed-priority scheduling strategy, each task is assigned a static or at least not frequently changing priority in a totally ordered space $P$, partitioning the set of tasks $T$ into a prioritized task set $(T_p)_{p \in P}$.
elements.sent-2411-174: At each time, the scheduler then simply selects a ready task with the highest priority to run on the \ac{cpu .}
elements.sent-2411-175: For the usually sufficient priority spaces that do not exceed the bit width of a machine word, the necessary priority queue data structure can be implemented in a way that allows deterministic constant-time access using only a few machine code instructions.
elements.sent-2411-176: As a result, fixed-priority scheduling is widely used in practice, particularly in the domain of constrained embedded devices.
elements.sent-2411-177: When a feasible schedule for a set of critical tasks is desired, \ac{rms ~\cite{liu1973scheduling provides an optimal strategy using fixed priorities: If there is a feasible schedule using fixed priorities, simply assigning priorities inversely to the task durations yields one.}}
elements.sent-2411-178: Note that these assigned priorities do not necessarily correspond to the criticalities of the tasks, since every task in the set is certain to meet its deadline as long as the periods and \ac{wcet assumptions are obeyed.}
elements.sent-2411-179: There are many theories about feasibility analysis.
elements.sent-2411-180: For example, the famous sufficient condition of Liu and Leyland~\cite{liu1973scheduling gives a lower bound of $\ln(2)$ on the compound utilization at which every possible set of tasks is schedulable.}
elements.sent-2411-181: However, an exact analysis can be tedious, especially when there are many inhomogeneous periods.
elements.sent-2411-182: For this reason, and due to the fact that the \ac{wcet analysis itself gives loose upper bounds, in practice we are usually left with a lot of unused idle time.}
elements.sent-2411-183: It is important to note that in a set of prioritized tasks, static feasibility analysis can be useful even though the task set as a whole is not schedulable.
elements.sent-2411-184: By mathematical construction, meeting the deadline requirements of a task depends only on the set of tasks with higher or equal priority.
elements.sent-2411-185: Thus, in a prioritized task set $(T_p)_{p \in P}$ there exists a bound $p_\text{feasible}$ such that the set of tasks with higher priority $\bigcup_{p \geq p_\text{feasible T_p$ has a particular schedule.}}
elements.sent-2411-186: A secondary prioritization approach suitable for the tasks below this boundary is to naively assign priorities according to task criticality.
elements.sent-2411-187: Without feasibility guarantees, this is still a legitimate approach for less critical soft real-time tasks.
elements.sent-2411-188: The higher the priority of such a task, the lower the risk that the \ac{cpu is already overbooked.}
elements.sent-2411-189: When dealing with sporadic tasks, a simple approach is to treat them as if they were regular periodic tasks, assigning their minimum inter-arrival time as their period, thus preparing for the worst case.
elements.sent-2411-190: As lower inter-arrival times converge to the characteristics of an aperiodic task, this worst-case budget increases inversely, making such treatment infeasible.
elements.sent-2411-191: While aperiodic tasks are also given deadlines in the literature \parencite{sha2004real , these are impossible to meet in the general case due to the unpredictable nature of aperiodicity.}
elements.sent-2411-192: One approach to integrating aperiodic tasks into fixed-priority scheduling is to use so-called server tasks.
elements.sent-2411-193: To the scheduler, these behave like normal prioritized tasks.
elements.sent-2411-194: Unlike other tasks, they do not have an individual goal.
elements.sent-2411-195: Instead, they use their budget to support the execution of aperiodic jobs.
elements.sent-2411-196: Because of their limited budget in each period, they can be easily included in scheduling considerations.
elements.sent-2411-197: A very simple but effective aperiodic server is the deferrable server~\cite{strosnider1995deferrable .}
elements.sent-2411-198: It has a limited \ac{cpu time budget to serve aperiodic events.}
elements.sent-2411-199: When the budget is exhausted, it pauses execution until the next renewal.
elements.sent-2411-200: At the end of each period its budget is restored to the initial amount.
elements.sent-2411-201: A big advantage is the simplicity of the mechanism and therefore of an implementation for this server scheme.
elements.sent-2411-202: Yet, the deferral of budget consumption incurs on a higher worst-case processing demand than one budget per period, due to possible back-to-back execution patterns.
elements.sent-2411-203: Let $p$ be the server period and $e$ its execution budget for a period.
elements.sent-2411-204: There may arrive jobs just before the end of a period consuming the whole capacity $e$ of the server for this period.
elements.sent-2411-205: With the start of the next period and the consequent budget replenishment, another duration $e$ may be serviced to jobs.
elements.sent-2411-206: In the worst case, we need to expect one extra execution budget.
elements.sent-2411-207: Thus, the highest possible server demand $d(\Delta)$ inside an arbitrary interval can be indicated as \begin{equation}d(\Delta) = e \cdot \left(\left\lceil \frac{\Delta}{p} \right\rceil + 1\right)\end{equation} Note that at least for a small period $p$, the CPU bandwidth $\frac{d(\Delta) {\Delta}$ still approaches the theoretical server optimum $\frac{e {p}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-208: The sporadic server tries to improve the worst-case demand-bound situation of the deferrable server~\cite{sprunt1989aperiodic .}
elements.sent-2411-209: It does this by replenishing in chunks instead of restoring the full capacity at the beginning of a period.
elements.sent-2411-210: There are different definitions in the literature that differ in the exact conditions under which replenishments are scheduled.
elements.sent-2411-211: We mainly consider the \ac{posix specification SCHED\_SPORADIC.}
elements.sent-2411-212: By replenishing chunks of the budget one period after their consumption, we can easily ensure that jobs are not served more than $e$ times per period $p$, while allowing job arrival bursts of size below $e$ to be processed immediately.
elements.sent-2411-213: The largest possible demand within an interval is then \begin{equation}d(\Delta) = e \cdot \left\lceil \frac{\Delta}{p} \right\rceil\end{equation} Usually, as in the \ac{posix variant of the sporadic server, the replenishment for work chunks is scheduled relative to the server activation time.}
elements.sent-2411-214: This is the time at which the incoming job request makes the server ready, which it could then possibly serve if there were no higher-priority ready tasks.
elements.sent-2411-215: Since a regular periodic task is allowed to run for more than $e$ times in any period $p$ after being preempted by higher priority tasks, we can mimic its behavior.
elements.sent-2411-216: This improves the productivity of a frequently preempted server while still relying on a worst-case demand equivalent to a periodic task with the same properties.
elements.sent-2411-217: Hardware interrupts are used by peripheral components for signaling the need for action of the CPU.
elements.sent-2411-218: These interrupts are not subject to scheduling and depend on external hardware, which oftentimes requires a low response time to interrupts.
elements.sent-2411-219: In addition, the required time to handle an interrupt can vary.
elements.sent-2411-220: Therefore interrupts introduce uncertainty, threatening the real-time property of the system.
elements.sent-2411-221: As a counter measure interrupt handling is separated into a short \ac{isr and a kernel task.}
elements.sent-2411-222: The \ac{isr executes only the absolutely necessary actions to acknowledge an interrupt, like saving the processor state to the stack and afterwards calling the kernel task.}
elements.sent-2411-223: During execution of the \ac{isr , further interrupts are disabled.}
elements.sent-2411-224: The subsequent service task then executes the necessary steps depending on the interrupt source and type to fully service the interrupt.
elements.sent-2411-225: This task becomes subject to scheduling and is usually preemptable~\cite{LiuJane .}
elements.sent-2411-226: For real-time scheduling theory, \acp{irq pose several challenges.}
elements.sent-2411-227: First, they can take over \ac{cpu resources at any time.}
elements.sent-2411-228: However, if they are systematically tamed to known minimum inter-arrival times and \acp{wcet , an integration into the considerations of a schedulable task set, as discussed in Section~\ref{sec:fixed , is possible again.}}
elements.sent-2411-229: More problematic is that in all common \ac{os implementations, they trigger the execution of an \ac{isr in an elevated \ac{irq context.}}}
elements.sent-2411-230: There, the execution can either be completely uninterruptible itself, or only by another higher priority \ac{irq source (\enquote{interrupt nesting ).}}
elements.sent-2411-231: Modern interrupt controllers commonly used in microcontroller designs\footnote{e.g., the Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller included in ARM Cortex-M core designs allow \ac{irq priorities to be assigned at run-time and a priority mask to be set so that only higher-priority \ac{irq are scheduled.}}}
elements.sent-2411-232: By introducing an interrupt mask register write operation to the task scheduler, it is possible to partially integrate the launching of \acp{irq into the scheduling of tasks~\cite{task_aware .}}
elements.sent-2411-233: However, once an interrupt has passed through this mask, it cannot be preempted by a recently unblocked regular task, and thus exhibits atomic execution behavior.
elements.sent-2411-234: Thus, \ac{irq and task priorities form two different priority spaces.}
elements.sent-2411-235: To minimize the worst-case latency caused by priority inversion situations between interrupts and high-priority tasks, a common programming best practice is to minimize the work done in an \ac{isr by only unblocking a deferred \ac{ist , which then does the actual processing.}}
elements.sent-2411-236: This compromises interrupt handling performance, especially for relatively short interrupt routines, for better scheduler control.
elements.sent-2411-237: Therefore, in reality, the driver developer often has to weigh how much additional latency is acceptable until an \ac{isr /\ac{ist split is introduced.}}
elements.sent-2411-238: A critical part to effectively schedule tasks with deadlines is that the task execution times must be known.
elements.sent-2411-239: Especially the longest possible time a program needs until completed is of interest here, commonly refereed to as \ac{wcet ~\cite{wcet_survey .}}
elements.sent-2411-240: In general, two approaches to finding the worst-case execution time of a program exist, which can also be combined: Static analysis and execution time measuring.
elements.sent-2411-241: Simplified, a static analysis aims to analyze an executable to find the longest control flow path of the program.
elements.sent-2411-242: For this path the number of CPU instructions are counted, and with information about the clock speed and cycles per instruction of the executing processor the maximum execution time can be calculated.
elements.sent-2411-243: The advantage of static analysis is that the actual worst-case execution time can be found.
elements.sent-2411-244: However, for complex programs it is not always feasible.
elements.sent-2411-245: The execution times can also be measured from the actual runtime of the program.
elements.sent-2411-246: For this, the program must run on the real hardware, and in a realistic environment which can trigger all possible control flows of the program.
elements.sent-2411-247: This might be more practical for complex programs, but does not guarantee that the actual worst-case execution was really found.
elements.sent-2411-248: Both approaches can be combined, by finding control flow blocks with static analysis and then measuring the execution times of these blocks.
elements.sent-2411-249: In summary, worst-case execution time analysis is a hard, but well studied problem~\cite{wcet_survey .}
elements.sent-2411-250: The \ac{nic acts as an I/O device for any networked computer.}
elements.sent-2411-251: While the sending of packets can be influenced by both the sending process and the networking subsystem of the operating system, the receiving of packets, like any other external input, is not predictable.
elements.sent-2411-252: Moreover, unlike most other external input sources, the frequency of reception is subject to an open complex network that makes prediction impossible at any time.
elements.sent-2411-253: As a result, IP packet reception occurs arbitrarily and without definitive knowledge of packet rates and the corresponding triggered workload.
elements.sent-2411-254: In order to find feasible real-time protection against unscheduled packet loads, the entire receive path must be understood.
elements.sent-2411-255: This section explains that path in detail.
elements.sent-2411-256: At a high level, the \ac{rx path is organized into subsequently executed stages as seen in Figure~\ref{fig:rx_path_overview .}}
elements.sent-2411-257: Upon packet reception, the \ac{nic transfers the packet content to a previously prepared memory location via \ac{dma , marks the corresponding \ac{bd entry and triggers an interrupt.}}}
elements.sent-2411-258: The network driver, handling the interrupt, acknowledges the \ac{dma -operation and exchanges the received frame buffer with a newly allocated one.}
elements.sent-2411-259: From here, protocol processing can commence disregarding the already finished \ac{mac -layer operations.}
elements.sent-2411-260: The different network stack implementations vary in their set of features.
elements.sent-2411-261: The lwIP network stack is widely spread among embedded applications and sets its focus on memory efficiency~\cite{lwip .}
elements.sent-2411-262: It covers the majority of commonly used and necessary protocols from Layer 2 up to Layer 4 like \ac{arp , \ac{ip , \ac{tcp , \ac{udp , \ac{dns , and \ac{dhcp .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-263: It offers different \acp{api for efficient access, multi-threading, and an implementation of the Berkley Socket \acp{api .}}
elements.sent-2411-264: Internally, lwIP does not represent a complete data frame but stores a subset of data in pbuf structures.
elements.sent-2411-265: Other stack implementations like FreeRTOS+TCP\footnote{Documentation, \url{https://www.freertos.org/FreeRTOS-Plus/FreeRTOS_Plus_TCP/ offer the complete frame and stick to the Berkeley sockets API while being thread-safe.}}
elements.sent-2411-266: Using sockets, application tasks can register to receive and transmit through their desired ports.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=.9\columnwidth]{images/2_3_software/RX_Path_Overview} \caption{\textbf{\ac{rx}-Path:} Packets are handled by OS data structures, the network driver, and the networking stack implementation before they can be accessed by the receiving process.} \label{fig:rx_path_overview}
elements.sent-2411-267: The \ac{nic facilitates communication in \acp{lan by implementing the first two layers of the OSI model.}}
elements.sent-2411-268: The physical layer and data link layer.
elements.sent-2411-269: A \ac{nic may support one or multiple transmission media, for example twisted pair copper cable, fiber optics or wireless communication as defined by the \ac{lan standard it implements.}}
elements.sent-2411-270: Hence, the technologies implemented in \acp{nic supporting distinct technologies will differ fundamentally.}
elements.sent-2411-271: This is not only due to the difference in medium on the physical layer, but also due to the requirements reliable data delivery imposes on the data link layer.
elements.sent-2411-272: For example, Wireless LAN (IEEE 802.11) has a core requirement of collision avoidance due to contention on a shared medium, which in comparison would not be required when using point-to-point communication over cable on a Token Ring (IEEE 802.5).
elements.sent-2411-273: Consolidation and abstraction of these differences is the design objective of the NIC.
elements.sent-2411-274: The most prevalent LAN technology in use is Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), with Gigabit Ethernet (1000 Mb/s baseband networks) being a prominent exponent.
elements.sent-2411-275: The standard is designed in layers, with the physical layer components (PHY) and data link layer components (\ac{mac ) connected via a Media Independent Interface (MII).}
elements.sent-2411-276: On the link layer, data is transmitted as packets (called \ac{mac frames) with a size between 64 and 1518 octets.}
elements.sent-2411-277: The integrity of the frames is guaranteed using a 4 octet Frame Check Sequence (FCS) containing a cyclic redundancy check (CRC) value.
elements.sent-2411-278: The CRC is used by the \ac{mac to detect bit errors in the transmission, subsequently dropping any corrupted frames.}
elements.sent-2411-279: The NIC's output are Ethernet Frames which contain the network-layer packets as payload.
elements.sent-2411-280: Checksums of (parts of) the frame typically occur at Layer 2 and Layer 4 (for \acs{tcp /\acs{udp ).}}
elements.sent-2411-281: For \ac{ip v4 even the header itself is checksummed again at Layer 3.}
elements.sent-2411-282: Modern \acp{nic can fill in blank checksum fields with the respectively calculated values and test incoming packet checksums for correctness, relieving the software and therefore the \ac{cpu from that task.}}
elements.sent-2411-283: A more sophisticated feature is to have the \ac{nic do \ac{tcp processing, either by managing the segmentation of larger buffers into packets ("partial \ac{tcp offloading") or also handling the management of \ac{tcp connections ("full offloading") \parencite[107]{tcpoffloadengines .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-284: These are however only used in high bandwith server systems.
elements.sent-2411-285: For our scope of embedded devices, such features are not expected to be available.
elements.sent-2411-286: To decrease the performance impact of incoming packets, high performance NICs employ interrupt moderation techniques.
elements.sent-2411-287: Instead of sending an interrupt for each received data frame, the NIC delays the delivery of an interrupt in order to receive and coalesce additional packets~\cite{makineni2006receive .}
elements.sent-2411-288: Different strategies to realize the delay exist.
elements.sent-2411-289: A simple approach is to use a packet counter that triggers an interrupt and resets once a certain number of packets have arrived.
elements.sent-2411-290: This leads to a constant and homogeneous reduction of interrupts but also introduces the possibility of starving packets and very unpredictable packet delays.
elements.sent-2411-291: To have control over the time packets reside in memory unnoticed, different types of delay timers are applied: The absolute timer begins a countdown once a packet has been received and only triggers an interrupt once reaching zero.
elements.sent-2411-292: All packets received in this time frame are announced by this interrupt and do not reset the timer.
elements.sent-2411-293: The obvious disadvantage of this approach is the high latency the first packet of each countdown experiences.
elements.sent-2411-294: In low traffic scenarios, this is highly inefficient.
elements.sent-2411-295: To this end, packet timers can be introduced.
elements.sent-2411-296: Instead of having a relatively long countdown timer to trigger an interrupt for multiple packets, the counter is a lot smaller and resets with each incoming packet.
elements.sent-2411-297: In low traffic scenarios this leads to smaller delays while interrupts can be entirely impeded under high traffic.
elements.sent-2411-298: The mostly applied solution is therefore a combination of a longer absolute timer and a shorter packet timer.
elements.sent-2411-299: The tuning of the specific parameters is highly dependent on the expected load and subject to research in high performance computing~\cite{interruptmoderation_hpc .}
elements.sent-2411-300: In order to relieve the CPU from actively pulling data from or pushing data to the NIC memory, \ac{dma has been established as a common feature in peripheral hardware.}
elements.sent-2411-301: With it, the NIC can asynchronously place received packet data into main memory at previously assigned locations and afterwards only needs to notify the CPU about the arrival of new packets via interrupt.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[scale=0.6]{images/2_3_software/BD_ring} \caption{BD Ring: The data structure allows simultaneous access from the NIC adding entries and the CPU checking off processed descriptors.} \label{fig:BD_ring}
elements.sent-2411-302: The memory assignment usually happens in so-called \ac{bd rings, as outlined in Figure~\ref{fig:BD_ring .}}
elements.sent-2411-303: In memory such a ring comprises an array of Buffer Descriptors, interpreted as a ring buffer.
elements.sent-2411-304: Each \ac{bd contains a memory pointer to the respective buffer and some metadata for cooperation.}
elements.sent-2411-305: The latter typically includes an ownership bit, indicating whether the CPU or NIC is obligated to go on with processing, and a length field indicating how far into the buffer data shall be sent or has been received respectively.
elements.sent-2411-306: This way, both actors can track their current working position(s) individually.
elements.sent-2411-307: A more sophisticated mode is Scatter/Gather \ac{dma , through which successive transfers into non-contiguous blocks of memory are realized.}
elements.sent-2411-308: This technique uses memory-resident structures called block descriptors, each holding the memory address of a write destination buffer as well as a status field.
elements.sent-2411-309: Every block descriptor also stores the address of another block descriptor, consequently forming a single-linked list of descriptors called a descriptor chain \footnote{Xilinx, \ac{axi \ac{dma LogiCORE IP Product Guide (PG021), April 2022.. .}}}
elements.sent-2411-310: When active, the \ac{dma engine traverses the chain sequentially from the first descriptor (head) to the last descriptor (tail), processing each block descriptor: \begin{compactenum \item Fetch the current block descriptor from memory \item Transfer a received block of data to the memory address pointed to by the block descriptor \item Write a completion bit into the status word of the descriptor \item Replace the address of the current descriptor with the next descriptor \end{compactenum Once the \ac{dma controller reaches the tail pointer, it halts and signals an \ac{irq .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-311: This allows the software to access the transferred data and update the descriptor chain.
elements.sent-2411-312: Since \ac{dma modifies a processing system's memory independently of software, cache coherency needs to be established.}
elements.sent-2411-313: When using \ac{dma to receive data, this is required to avoid reading stale cache data in place of the transferred data in memory.}
elements.sent-2411-314: This can be achieved by clearing the cache lines for the address range of the received data\footnote{ARM, ARM Cortex-A Series Programmer's Guide Version: 4.0, January 2014.. .}
elements.sent-2411-315: Similarly, when transferring data to the peripheral via \ac{dma , the cache lines in memory regions holding data need to be flushed beforehand.}
elements.sent-2411-316: This ensures all data intended for transfer has been flushed from cache to memory.
elements.sent-2411-317: The \acs{rx -path begins with the triggering of an \ac{irq by the \ac{nic .}}}
elements.sent-2411-318: The \ac{isr is executed and either directly or after scheduling a deferred \ac{ist , the driver does all the work necessary to have the packet data in an owned buffer, either by \begin{compactitem \item actively pulling the data from the \ac{nic via \ac{mmio and writing it into a custom buffer, or by \item acknowledging the \ac{dma -operation and exchanging the respective buffer for a freshly allocated one.. \end{compactitem.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-319: As described in Section~\ref{sec:nic the \ac{mac layer does not need to be processed again.}}

elements.sent-2411-324: The \ac{arp protocol is used to resolve network addresses to hardware (\ac{mac ) addresses in local \acs{ip v4 subnets.}}}
elements.sent-2411-325: Its packets are almost impossible to attribute to a single network application.
elements.sent-2411-326: On a well-behaved local network, they appear sporadically in small quantities.

elements.sent-2411-328: Alternatively, it could be a response containing another node's address pair that needs to be cached.
elements.sent-2411-329: For the \acs{ip layer, it must be checked whether the packet is destined for this node, based on the destination \acs{ip address .}}
elements.sent-2411-330: If not, it can act as a router and forward it through another \ac{nic .}
elements.sent-2411-331: In embedded devices that are leaves in the network topology, this step can be omitted by simply dropping misaddressed packets.
elements.sent-2411-332: The length of the payload of an \acs{ip packet is limited only by the 16 bits of the Total Length field.}
elements.sent-2411-333: Therefore, a packet can be as long as 65,535 bytes.
elements.sent-2411-334: When transmitting over a local link, the length restrictions are often much finer.
elements.sent-2411-335: For example, regular Ethernet has a maximum payload size of 1,500 bytes.
elements.sent-2411-336: Packets that exceed this limit must be broken into multiple fragments.
elements.sent-2411-337: As a stateful mechanism in an otherwise stateless \acl{ip , this introduces robustness, reliability, and even security issues \parencite{kent1987fragmentation,gilad2011fragmentation .}}
elements.sent-2411-339: Reliable transport manages its own segmentation, and even runs into transmission quality issues if it inadvertently causes \ac{ip fragmentation.}
elements.sent-2411-340: Untrustworthy datagram applications also like to handle efficient encoding of application logic data into single datagrams themselves, to increase performance and reliability.
elements.sent-2411-341: In \acs{ip v6, there is a guaranteed lower bound of 1,280 bytes for the MTU of any link, while \acs{ip v4 only requires a minimum of 68 bytes.}}
elements.sent-2411-342: When the stack supports \acs{ip fragmentation, fragmented packets must be cached by the key tuple (source \acs{ip address , identification) or reassembled with other fragments in that cache.}}
elements.sent-2411-343: Given the general discouragement of using \acs{ip fragmentation, and in order to avoid the reassembly overhead, embedded networking stacks tend to ignore fragmented packets, even though this makes them non-standard.}
elements.sent-2411-344: Then all you have to do to prove that a packet is complete is look up the Fragmentation Offset and Flags.

elements.sent-2411-346: In addition to the standard 20 bytes, there can be up to 40 bytes of additional options in the header.
elements.sent-2411-347: These are rarely used outside the Internet.
elements.sent-2411-348: In fact, packets with IP options are discriminated against by Autonomous System providers, having a higher drop rate and experiencing measurably worse latency and jitter \parencite{fonseca2005ipoptions .}

elements.sent-2411-355: Also, packets destined for unreachable hosts or ports may trigger a corresponding \ac{icmp response message.}

elements.sent-2411-359: If not already done by the \ac{nic , the correctness of the Payload Length and Checksum fields must be checked again.}
elements.sent-2411-360: Besides allowing demultiplexing similar to \ac{udp , \ac{tcp also provides a reliable communication stream over unreliable segments.}}

elements.sent-2411-363: For many years there has been the idea of custom user-level stacks that can be provided and configured by each application independently.. \parencite{honda2014userlevelstacks .}
elements.sent-2411-364: However, competitors to \ac{tcp , such as QUIC, are usually implemented on top of \ac{udp , which itself introduces little overhead over simple demultiplexing, since it is supported by virtually every \acs{tcp /\acs{ip stack and respected by all the Internet middleboxes that otherwise love to destroy end-to-end protocol agnosticity.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-365: Its header is more streamlined, omitting legacy complications such as variable header length for additional options and \ac{ip fragmentation.}
elements.sent-2411-366: These, in turn, can be included by a special extension header that sits between the main \acs{ip v6 header and higher protocols.}
