elements.sent-2411-369: Nevertheless, \acs{ip v4 is still much more widely used, and \acs{ip v6 is generally derided as a future protocol, since it has been around for 20 years and will probably take another 20 years to really replace \acs{ip v4.}}}
elements.sent-2411-370: Lightweight IP\footnote{\url{https://www.nongnu.org/lwip/2_1_x/index.html (LwIP) is an open-source implementation of the Internet Protocol Suite for embedded devices.}}
elements.sent-2411-371: It was originally developed by Adam Dunkel at the Swedish Institute of Computer Science in 2003.
elements.sent-2411-372: Having been designed especially for low-power embedded devices, its implementation places an emphasis on reduced memory usage and code size~\cite{lwip .}
elements.sent-2411-373: The IP stack takes advantage of the typically soft division between kernel and application space on embedded devices, which allows sharing of data structures and directly passing buffer between memory spaces without copy.
elements.sent-2411-374: Furthermore, LwIP does not adhere to the strict protocol layering in the TCP/IP suite.
elements.sent-2411-375: Instead, it is implemented in a flexible event-based architecture, combining data from different layers in shared data structures where advantageous and relying on callbacks for protocol processing between layers~\cite{lwip .}
elements.sent-2411-376: Multiple APIs exist for implementing access to the network stack.
elements.sent-2411-377: The low-level raw API is designed for applications in bare-metal environments, offering an event-driven workflow while requiring no separate network thread.
elements.sent-2411-378: This API provides native access to the protocol processing functionality, since it is also used internally for protocol processing between layers of the network stack.
elements.sent-2411-379: LwIP also implements more traditional higher-level sequential APIs to be used in environments with \acp{os , such as the Netconn API or Berkeley Sockets.}
elements.sent-2411-380: These are implemented with an internal user-space core process, which encapsulates all network stack data as well as the invocation of the native functions for protocol processing.
elements.sent-2411-381: Communication with the core process is implemented through a messaging bus, where the external API calls pass messages containing the internal functions as callbacks to the core process for invocation.
elements.sent-2411-382: This avoids the critical section problems inherent with multi-tasking \acp{os as well as multi-threading, making the high-level APIs thread-safe.}
elements.sent-2411-383: The messaging bus requires some platform-dependent means of inter-process communication.
elements.sent-2411-384: Furthermore, LwIP is also supported in concurrent environment without the necessity for the core process.
elements.sent-2411-385: This is realized by core locking, where a platform-defined synchronization primitive is used for mutual exclusion.
elements.sent-2411-386: The mutex guards against concurrent access to the internal LwIP core data structures and functionality.
elements.sent-2411-387: It also accommodates priority inversion required when using LwIP with a preemptive multitasking operating system.
elements.sent-2411-388: The FreeRTOS+TCP stack is developed as an open source library extension to FreeRTOS.
elements.sent-2411-389: As the naming suggests, it is designed to work with the mechanisms of FreeRTOS and is developed in a similar clean code style.
elements.sent-2411-390: The internal architecture features a monolithic \acs{ip task that handles all kinds of events, from packet reception to socket binding, and thus acts as a synchronization unit.}
elements.sent-2411-392: Support for \acs{ip v6 is still in preview.}
elements.sent-2411-393: Also, \acs{ip v4 options are not evaluated.}
elements.sent-2411-394: Depending on the preprocessor configuration, the options may be ignored or the whole packet discarded.
elements.sent-2411-395: Outgoing packets can be fragmented, but reassembly is not supported.
elements.sent-2411-396: The \ac{iiot builds on the same core ideas as the IoT and applies them to the industrial domain.}
elements.sent-2411-397: Whereas in \ac{iot , devices are equipped with sensors, computing power and, most importantly, communication capabilities, in \ac{iiot this is applied to all kinds of industrial assets.}}
elements.sent-2411-398: This enables the collection and analysis of data generated by machines and their control systems, for example to optimize process flows or detect anomalies in order to prevent malfunctions.
elements.sent-2411-399: In addition, the comprehensive networking of processing machines makes it possible to respond in real-time to external influences such as changing demand.
elements.sent-2411-400: In contrast to IoT, where the networked devices and the network itself are generally considered unreliable and volatile, the industrial domain tends to assume static networks with low mobility and high reliability.
elements.sent-2411-401: These are well structured and tailored to the expected load, since control applications, for example, require latencies in the millisecond range~\cite{iiot2 .}
elements.sent-2411-402: Although a central component of both IoT and IIoT is the connection of individual devices to the Internet and the use of cloud technology, this is not the optimal approach in every scenario.
elements.sent-2411-403: As just described, control applications in particular have very high communication latency and reliability requirements.
elements.sent-2411-404: Particularly with the large amounts of data generated in the IIoT context, the point is quickly reached where cloud-based data processing no longer meets the requirements and, for example, real-time critical processes miss their deadlines.
elements.sent-2411-405: For these reasons, the edge computing approach is becoming increasingly popular in the IIoT space.
elements.sent-2411-406: Edge computing describes a paradigm in which computing capacity in physical proximity to the data source is used instead of cloud services for data processing of specific, primarily latency-critical applications~\cite{qiu2020edge .}
elements.sent-2411-407: In the context of Chapter~\ref{cha:3_3_offloading , we assume that such an edge computing cluster exists in the immediate vicinity of the real-time actors.}
elements.sent-2411-408: Since the later chapters of this work consider network constraints when scheduling and processing real-time tasks in a distributed fashion, the exact network latency conditions are of central importance.
elements.sent-2411-409: In this thesis, a simple approach to dynamically determine the network characteristics is presented and implemented, although it is highly unlikely that this approach can be applied universally.
elements.sent-2411-410: However, the topic of network delay estimation is well researched and many approaches have been published, such as in~\cite{delay_estimation_1 ,~\cite{delay_estimation_2 and~\cite{delay_estimation_3 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-411: We therefore assume that even in scenarios where the approach to network delay estimation used in this work cannot be used, one of the published approaches is applicable and that, in principle, well-founded statements about the performance of a network, especially in the area of delay, can be profiled.
elements.sent-2411-412: Another important factor in distributed real-time systems is time synchronization.
elements.sent-2411-413: Meaningful scheduling of real-time tasks can only work if the actors generating the tasks and the scheduler have the same understanding of time.
elements.sent-2411-414: Since control applications require latencies in the millisecond range, as described earlier, and wireless networks are typically in this range, time synchronization must be in the sub-millisecond range.
elements.sent-2411-415: The IEEE 1588 standard \enquote{for a Precision Clock Synchronization Protocol for Networked Measurement and Control Systems defines a protocol that enables sub-microsecond clock synchronization in local area networks~\cite{ptp_ieee .}}
elements.sent-2411-416: Another approach with a focus on distributed real-time systems, dating back to 1987, is presented in~\cite{clock_sync_1 .}
elements.sent-2411-417: Since these approaches clearly exceed the precision requirements, we assume in the further course of the work that the clocks of all actors are sufficiently synchronized and the system is not influenced by this aspect.
elements.chapter-2411-005: Real-Time Performance of Industrial Communication Technologies
elements.sent-2411-418: This chapter performs a review of these technologies and presents recent academic works.
elements.sent-2411-419: With the growing need for automation and the ongoing merge of \ac{ot and \ac{it , industrial networks have to transport a high amount of heterogeneous data with mixed criticality such as control traffic, sensor data, and configuration messages.}}
elements.sent-2411-420: Current advances in IT technologies furthermore enable a new set of automation scenarios under the roof of Industry 4.0 and IIoT where industrial networks now have to meet new requirements in flexibility and reliability.
elements.sent-2411-421: The necessary real-time guarantees place significant demands on the networks.
elements.sent-2411-422: We present and discuss existing standards and technologies, as well as recent works related to real-time IIoT networking solutions.
elements.sent-2411-423: In doing so, we identify current IIoT scenarios addressed in research, derive their real-time networking requirements, and review established real-time networking technologies.
elements.sent-2411-424: This chapter presents the most notable recent research, and discusses the open challenges.
elements.sent-2411-425: The remainder of this chapter is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-426: Section \ref{sec:1_3_introduction introduces IT/OT convergence and motivates this review.}
elements.sent-2411-427: Section \ref{sec:1_3_scenarios identifies \ac{iiot scenario classes and derives real-time networking requirements.}}
elements.sent-2411-428: Section \ref{sec:1_3_background reviews the path of networking in industrial systems and presents existing standards designed for industrial networks.}
elements.sent-2411-429: Section \ref{sec:1_3_recent presents ongoing academic works on real-time networking.}
elements.sent-2411-430: Section \ref{sec:1_3_discussion discusses the feasibility of existing and ongoing solutions.}
elements.sent-2411-431: Section \ref{sec:1_3_conclusion concludes this chapter and indicates the research gaps the remainder of this thesis tries to partially bridge.}
elements.sent-2411-432: One of the main goals of Industry 4.0 is operational optimization.
elements.sent-2411-433: In a recent study~\cite{rwsn_industrial_AI_smart_factory_2021 regarding the usage of pervasive \ac{iiot sensor data, a large majority of the respondents expect Industry 4.0 to provide agility to scale the production to match demands, improved flexibility to customize products, and a reduced time to market for new products.}}
elements.sent-2411-434: The same participants reported that the current usage of IoT sensors in their systems was primarily used for remote monitoring of equipment, asset and material tracking, and predictive maintenance.
elements.sent-2411-435: Hence, to fully realize the potential of Industry 4.0, pervasive sensor coverage and interconnectedness are essential.
elements.sent-2411-436: To achieve this, great demands will be placed on the networks used in industrial systems.
elements.sent-2411-437: What used to work for \ac{ot systems will require a much higher degree of flexibility and scalability than what has been available in the past.}
elements.sent-2411-438: Science and technology are bridging the mechanized industry with the digital world to automate as many processes as possible by connecting operational technologies and business networks.
elements.sent-2411-439: Such ubiquitous sensor integration combined with a high degree of network connectivity, both amongst \acp{ot and between \acp{ot and back-office systems \ac{it , is often referred to as IT/OT Convergence and is the driving force behind Industry 4.0.}}}
elements.sent-2411-440: Examples of envisioned scenarios can be found in research on autonomous drones~\cite{lohan2018benefits , remote control~\cite{liu2020latency , real-time monitoring~\cite{zhan2022industrial , and predictive maintenance~\cite{nordal2021modeling .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-441: Furthermore, advancements in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) increase the real-time requirements for industrial networks.
elements.sent-2411-442: CPSs combine embedded systems with cybernetic control systems to control the physical world from the networked space.
elements.sent-2411-443: The physical processes involved often place strict real-time requirements on CPSs.
elements.sent-2411-444: Command, control, and safety considerations of different criticality levels demand constant latency and predictability, often implemented on small and restricted embedded systems.
elements.sent-2411-445: By attaching these embedded systems to larger networks and incorporating them into complex distributed systems, the same real-time requirements are extended to the network and its participants.
elements.sent-2411-446: Use cases with time-critical control tasks are spread over a network, requiring predictable and reliable packet routing through it.
elements.sent-2411-447: However, networking concepts like \ac{ip and packet routing are not created to consider real-time demands.}
elements.sent-2411-448: Packets are indiscriminately routed over a variable number of networking devices, with very limited predictability concerning latency and arrival.
elements.sent-2411-449: The jitter introduced by wireless communication complicates a number of envisioned scenarios containing mobile devices.
elements.sent-2411-450: To meet the network real-time demands introduced by envisioned \ac{iiot scenarios, established specialized protocols and standards exist.}
elements.sent-2411-451: At the same time, current works in research attend to IIoT-specific requirements such as scalability~\cite{mirani2022key , integration of business and factory networks~\cite{hicking2021collaboration , and mobile computing~\cite{ma2021tcda .}}}
elements.sent-2411-452: In this section, we perform a preliminary literature survey to identify IIoT scenarios containing real-time and network requirements.
elements.sent-2411-453: To this end, we classify the scenarios into groups as depicted in Table~\ref{tab:scenarios .}
elements.sent-2411-454: Network and real-time requirements are derived from the scenario groups to prepare the following survey on enabling technology standards and recent works.
elements.sent-2411-455: A total of 31 research papers with IIoT scenarios of interest have been reviewed.
elements.sent-2411-456: The papers and articles chosen were peer-reviewed and published in the past 10 years.
elements.sent-2411-457: The extracted use cases were either the main focus of a paper or example implementations for evaluation purposes.
elements.figure-2411-004: \caption{IIoT scenario classes} \label{tab:scenarios} \begin{adjustbox}{max width=\textwidth} \begin{tabular}{cl|c|c|c|c|c} \thead{Scenario Class} \thead{References} \thead{Predictability Requirement} \thead{Latency Requirement} \thead{Bandwidth Requirement} \thead{Wireless Communication} \textbf{I} \makecell[l]{Autonomous Vehicles \& Mobile Robots} \cite{lohan2018benefits, miljkovic_new_2013, singholi_review_2021, chavhan_smart_2021, lo_bello_perspective_2019, barzegaran_fogification_2020} high medium variable essential \textbf{II} Remote Control of Machines \cite{zhohov_real-time_2018, kayan_cybersecurity_2022, kajan_control_2013, huang_sense_2019, bock2021performance} high high low not necessary \textbf{III} \makecell[l]{Sensor Data Analysis \& Monitoring} \cite{kanawaday_machine_2017, blanes_80211n_2015, iqbal_cooperative_2017, hicking2021collaboration, salhaoui2019smart} low low high not relevant \textbf{IV} \makecell[l]{Worker Safety \& Hazard Protection} \cite{kivela2018towards, zhou2017industrial, mcninch2019leveraging, reyes2013intelligent} high high low not necessary \textbf{V} Task Offloading \cite{hong_multi-hop_2019, hossain_edge_2020, deng_intelligent_2021, xu_joint_2022, ma2022reliability} medium medium high not relevant \textbf{VI} \makecell[l]{Industrial Wearable Systems \& Augmented Reality} \cite{kong_industrial_2019, svertoka_wearables_2021, lorenz_industrial_2018, rosales_iiot_2021, batalla_analyzing_2020, li2021helically} low medium high essential % \end{tabular} \end{adjustbox}
elements.sent-2411-458: Using the identified scenario classes, we hope to get a better understanding of network requirements concerning predictability, latency, and bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-459: These requirements will then be used in the following sections to assess proposed IIoT real-time networking solutions.
elements.sent-2411-460: Furthermore, we identify relevant network components for each scenario class.
elements.sent-2411-461: Warehouses and manufacturing sites are getting under pressure to fulfill an ever-increasing demand.
elements.sent-2411-462: To this end, conventional rigid automation is being exchanged for more flexible autonomous solutions controlled via IoT-connected sensors~\cite{chavhan_smart_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-463: Autonomously moving machines such as transport robots, forklifts, and warehouse robots pose challenges yet to be solved, especially when employed at a large scale or in close proximity to human workers.
elements.sent-2411-464: Autonomous vehicles have to navigate through dynamic environments making decisions for real-time controlled electric motors~\cite{barzegaran_fogification_2020 while being dependent on communication networks to prevent collisions and improve efficiency~\cite{singholi_review_2021, lo_bello_perspective_2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-465: Converging these real-time and networking requirements still requires research, especially considering the necessity for wireless communication.
elements.sent-2411-466: Since moving, potentially dangerous machines are involved, predictability and latency requirements are high.
elements.sent-2411-467: Bandwidth requirements are implementation-specific, with the main factor being whether high-volume sensor data (e.g. video, LIDAR) is transmitted for analysis.
elements.sent-2411-468: Industrial Control Systems (ICS) can be changed to make use of the IIoT paradigm by physically shifting controlling entities away from the real-time device, possibly outside of the plant floor~\cite{bock2021performance .}
elements.sent-2411-469: To this end, the originally existing air gap between ICS and external networks has to be bridged~\cite{kayan_cybersecurity_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-470: Besides the resulting security implications, this also means opening up real-time control systems to conventional IP networks and protocols.
elements.sent-2411-471: Use-cases furthermore include the remote control of vehicles in inaccessible places~\cite{zhohov_real-time_2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-472: Again, real-time requirements are to be considered as high, since we are regarding moving machines.
elements.sent-2411-473: Some use cases furthermore need wireless communication technologies to work.
elements.sent-2411-474: Bandwidth requirements are low as only control commands need to be transmitted.
elements.sent-2411-475: With the help of sensors on factory floors, a high amount of real-time data can be used to improve business automation, monitoring, and decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-476: To this end, potentially broad data streams have to be transmitted sharing a medium with real-time control traffic~\cite{blanes_80211n_2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-477: Use cases include data aggregation by wireless sensor networks~\cite{iqbal_cooperative_2017 and predictive maintenance on IIoT sensor data~\cite{kanawaday_machine_2017 .}}
elements.sent-2411-478: IT/OT integration also plays a role in monitoring and data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-479: Real-time information from IIoT sensors can help make business decisions and enable automating certain use-cases such as active energy management~\cite{hicking2021collaboration .}
elements.sent-2411-480: This scenario class, while somewhat dependent on real-time data streams does not contain hard real-time tasks.
elements.sent-2411-481: Hence requirements in this regard are negligible.
elements.sent-2411-482: However, due to the large amount of collective data produced by the high number of sensors, bandwidth requirements are usually high.
elements.sent-2411-483: With data-intensive applications benefitting from offloading, bandwidth requirements can be considered high for many use cases.
elements.sent-2411-484: Most considered works regard the introduction of IIoT to safety critical systems as a relevant risk factor.
elements.sent-2411-485: Safety in this case means the physical protection of workers and equipment.
elements.sent-2411-486: Due to the attack vector granted by the new connectivity, safety is more conjoined with security than ever~\cite{kivela2018towards .}
elements.sent-2411-487: Yet, some works promote the use of interconnected devices for better safety in high-risk environments such as in the mining industry.
elements.sent-2411-488: IIoT technologies can be used to monitor access to hazardous areas around operating machinery, improve documentation/monitoring of maintenance that requires shutdown of the machinery, and prevent unexpected startup or movement during machine maintenance activities~\cite{mcninch2019leveraging, zhou2017industrial, reyes2013intelligent .}
elements.sent-2411-489: Safety solutions distinguish between informational and automatizing setups.
elements.sent-2411-490: Purely informational scenarios react to real-time sensor data by issuing alarms or notifying workers and human operators.
elements.sent-2411-491: Smart monitoring and data analysis in the cloud make these scenarios and their real-time requirements similar to the Data Analysis class.
elements.sent-2411-492: Systems that automatically react to hazards such as fire, toxic gases, or dangerous proximity require the highest predictability and latency.
elements.sent-2411-493: However, reactive systems should work independently, making distributed solutions unsuitable.
elements.sent-2411-494: IIoT devices, like traditional embedded systems, only have limited computing capabilities due to power and space constraints.
elements.sent-2411-495: To this end, current research, especially in the field of mobile edge computing, considers the offloading of delay-sensitive tasks to local servers.
elements.sent-2411-496: The main challenges are the guarantee of maximum delays over shared networking channels and compute resources~\cite{deng_intelligent_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-497: Subject to offloading is mostly soft real-time tasks for better Quality of Service (QoS) where a balance between latency and reliability has to be found~\cite{ma2022reliability .}
elements.sent-2411-498: Due to the nature of offloading, hard real-time tasks with high requirements in predictability are not considered.
elements.sent-2411-499: At the same time, while offloading to more powerful machines can reduce computation time, latency requirements that are too tight to accommodate network delays cannot be accommodated.
elements.sent-2411-500: Industrial wearable systems are the means of human-machine interaction (HMI) for most of the other listed scenario classes.
elements.sent-2411-501: With manufacturing, maintenance, and control still requiring human interaction, wearables present the necessary interface to embedding human workers in smart factories.
elements.sent-2411-502: Wearable systems enable remote control~\cite{li2021helically , real-time monitoring via augmented reality~\cite{batalla_analyzing_2020 , sensor data aggregation~\cite{rosales_iiot_2021 , and safety systems~\cite{svertoka_wearables_2021 while adding the requirements of energy efficiency, size (weight), and wireless networking.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-503: Examples roughly follow two streams: human interaction devices and data collection devices~\cite{kong_industrial_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-504: For this scenario class, only the network requirements specific to wearables and augmented reality are considered.
elements.sent-2411-505: If the devices are used for i.e. remote control, Scenario Class II covers the requirements.
elements.sent-2411-506: To derive network requirements for the scenario classes, we need to somewhat generalize the regarded use cases.
elements.sent-2411-507: In the end, it comes down to the specific requirements of the underlying real-time tasks and how messages (i.e. network packets) are integrated into the control flow.
elements.sent-2411-508: The requirements broadly quantified in Table~\ref{tab:scenarios therefore only give a rough overview of the focal points and limiting factors related to the classes.}
elements.sent-2411-509: The spectrum of feasible IIoT scenarios increases with the strictness of real-time guarantees networks can adhere to while not limiting their flexibility.
elements.sent-2411-510: Furthermore, wireless communication technologies that can fulfill these guarantees are the necessary enablers for many of the identified scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-511: Interfacing general IP networking into real-time systems is necessary for use cases requiring external access or cloud computing.
elements.sent-2411-512: However, for most real-time relevant scenarios IT/OT integration is not the enabler.
elements.sent-2411-513: Real-time scenarios become more complex the further away participants are, limiting real-time guarantees and criticality levels.
elements.sent-2411-514: The introduction of the first PLC, Modicon model 084 in 1969, changed how production systems were designed, implemented, operated, and maintained.
elements.sent-2411-515: Improving mechanical systems and machinery with relay-based control loops was but a small step compared to what PLCs brought to the industry.
elements.sent-2411-516: Whole cabinets of relays could be replaced with a relatively small number of compact devices saving both space and power.
elements.sent-2411-517: Where the first PLC only had 16 inputs, 16 outputs, and a meager 1 kB of memory, contemporary PLCs have I/O in the order of hundreds, abundant memory, and much higher speeds.
elements.sent-2411-518: The next sections will cover different industrial networks and common requirements.
elements.sent-2411-519: Table \ref{tbl:network_tech presents the connection between networks and use cases.}
elements.sent-2411-520: With improved industrial automation, communication became an obvious hurdle to tackle.
elements.sent-2411-521: In 1979, Modicon developed and published the serial communication protocol Modbus, an application-layer protocol.
elements.sent-2411-522: Shortly thereafter the work to standardize Profibus started in Germany and was finally published in 1989.
elements.sent-2411-523: These first communication protocols would be the inaugural \ac{ot networks.}
elements.sent-2411-524: As the first fieldbus protocols grew in popularity, so did issues concerning interoperability.
elements.sent-2411-525: The different protocols were seldom compatible, and few vendors could support multiple protocols.
elements.sent-2411-526: After a somewhat turbulent era often referred to as "the Fieldbus War"~\cite{felser_fieldbus_2002 , an agreement was made in the late 1990s to create a common standard.}
elements.sent-2411-527: When the first fieldbus standard (IEC 61158) was published, the final product was essentially a collection of all the competing standards at the time.
elements.sent-2411-528: The latest revision of IEC61158 \cite{iec61158 lists 26 different fieldbus protocols grouped into services and protocols.}
elements.sent-2411-529: Since then, it has become apparent that the industry is increasingly looking towards open standards \cite{kayan_cybersecurity_2022 to avoid vendor lock-in and a more transparent way to handle security vulnerabilities to name a few.}
elements.sent-2411-530: In this same decade, the Controller Area Network (CAN) was also standardized by the automotive industry.
elements.sent-2411-531: CAN is a serial communication protocol where the message identifier also serves as the message priority.
elements.sent-2411-532: The protocol is optimized for short messages and has the priority encoded in the message identifier.
elements.sent-2411-533: This provides a non-destructive address arbitration which enables messages to pass without any delay induced by lower priority messages.
elements.sent-2411-534: At the same time, the IT sector underwent a networking revolution of its own where Ethernet would ultimately end up as the de-facto standard.
elements.sent-2411-535: After its initial version was published in 1985, it has since moved the throttle from 10 Mbps to 400 Gbps~\cite{ieee:802.3-2022 with its sight firmly set on 800 Gbps.}
elements.sent-2411-536: Data is split into discrete packets, each identified using a 48-bit Media Access Control (MAC) address giving ample room for growth.
elements.sent-2411-537: To connect hosts, systems can be chained together to form a ring topology or by using ethernet bridges (where store-and-forward switches are the most common).
elements.sent-2411-538: Each frame is individually routed, and the network can quickly adapt to changes in topology and assign new routes to frames while in transit.
elements.sent-2411-539: In 1974, Cerf et al. published TCP/IP \cite{rfc675 which was to become the backbone of the "network of networks" lovingly called "the Internet".}
elements.sent-2411-540: TCP is a connection-oriented protocol that when traffic moves in one direction, control traffic will move both ways allowing TCP to resend lost data and adjust the rate to the slowest link along the path.
elements.sent-2411-541: UDP is the connectionless sibling of TCP without any control traffic and thus offers no indication that traffic arrives at the destination.
elements.sent-2411-542: Industrial Ethernet encompasses the usage of Ethernet in industrial settings.
elements.sent-2411-543: The target applications typically have both latency and reliability requirements, which drives the design of protocols away from traditional Ethernet approaches for collision detection and avoidance.
elements.sent-2411-544: The most common real-time Ethernet protocols are EtherNet/IP\footnote{IP is for Industrial Protocol.}
elements.sent-2411-545: PROFInet is a translation of PROFIbus to run over an Ethernet network.
elements.sent-2411-546: EtherCAT is often used in industrial control and automation due to its speed and determinism.
elements.sent-2411-547: TTEthernet is a congestion-free network based on Ethernet that provides Time Triggered service for critical traffic, Rate Constrained for event-triggered traffic as well as a best-effort service.
elements.sent-2411-548: EtherNet/IP is an adaption of DeviceNet to Ethernet, it uses the Common Industrial Protocol (CIP) over TCP and UDP.
elements.sent-2411-549: TSN is a series of IEEE Standards for switched Ethernet~\cite{ieee:802.1ba_2021, ieee:802.1Q-2018 where Commercial Off-the-Shelf (COTS) networks can be configured to give bounded latency and extremely low packet loss for critical traffic on the data link layer.}
elements.sent-2411-550: TSN removes some of the initial robustness of Ethernet by requiring static routes but gains lower jitter and less out-of-order delivery.
elements.sent-2411-551: In addition, TSN defines strict and fine-grained Quality of Service (QoS) mechanisms which are covered in more detail in Section \ref{sec:1_3_psn_qos .}
elements.sent-2411-552: Wireless sensors are cheaper, easier to install and maintain than their wired counterparts and bear the promise of an infrastructure that is vastly more scalable.
elements.sent-2411-553: In some scenarios, the cost-savings can be as high as 60-90\%~\cite{power_reduce_2009,8558500 compared to wired solutions.}
elements.sent-2411-554: It can even be the only viable option (e.g., the Tire Pressure Monitoring System (TPMS), tool deflection measurements~\cite{ostling_cutting_2018 ).}
elements.sent-2411-555: The downside of wireless networks is the shared medium; the integrity of the network is fully dependent upon cooperative participants that only transmit during allocated transmit slots.
elements.sent-2411-556: Frequency bands needed for wireless protocols are a tightly managed resource, and only a few bands are available for free use, most notably in the 2.4GHz and 5 GHz range.
elements.sent-2411-557: Where a wired network can be fairly resilient to signal interference, have high and predictable bandwidth, and require an adversary to be close to the wired infrastructure to eavesdrop, wireless systems have no such luxury.
elements.sent-2411-558: Over the years, many wireless protocols have been defined with different characteristics such as high bandwidth, low latency, long-range, a high number of addressable hosts, and robust resistance to interference.
elements.sent-2411-559: Oftentimes, these attributes will be at odds; i.e. with longer ranges come lower bandwidth and higher latency, and high bandwidth can make the traffic more susceptible to interference due to the denser encoding.
elements.sent-2411-560: Both WirelessHART and ISA 100.11a are common industrial protocols that are based on IEEE 802.15.4 and operate in the unlicensed 2.4GHz frequency band.
elements.sent-2411-561: ZigBee, while also being based on 802.15.4, can be found in some industrial settings but is primarily intended for home automation and low-criticality systems.
elements.sent-2411-562: Bluetooth is mentioned due to its pervasiveness in personal handheld devices.
elements.sent-2411-563: With its low power, it is only capable of transmitting data over a few meters and is primarily intended for short-range personal area networks.
elements.sent-2411-564: The upside is rather low power consumption ample access to complete modules, and comparably high available bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-565: In recent years, Wireless LAN (WLAN) has become a common technology in most households, but the technology has not been reliable or deterministic enough for industrial settings.
elements.sent-2411-566: With the recent WLAN6(E), this has improved markedly, and with the availability of Multiple-Input/Multiple-Output (MIMO), access is more reliable and less prone to interference from other senders.
elements.sent-2411-567: The latest version, WLAN6E has also markedly improved the clock accuracy for protocols such as PTP, which makes sensor aggregation more accurate.
elements.sent-2411-568: In addition to the rather crowded 2.4GHz band, WLAN can also operate in the 5GHz and 6GHz range allowing for higher bandwidth at the cost of higher signal attenuation from obstacles along the path.
elements.sent-2411-569: The fifth generation of mobile telephony (5G) from the 3GPP aims to cover all areas of wireless communications from cellular networks, IoT devices, and industrial networks.
elements.sent-2411-570: Interference from other networks should therefore be at a minimum.
elements.sent-2411-571: Three broad use cases have been defined for 5G: cellular data (enhanced Mobile Broadband, eMMB), IoT (massive Machine Type Communications, mMTC) and low-latency, critical traffic (Ultra-Reliable, Low Latency, URLLC).
elements.sent-2411-572: It is important to note that these use cases are not introduced in any single release, but support for each is added as increments with different capabilities in each release.
elements.sent-2411-573: With the first 5G release from 3GPP, Release 15, support for New Radio, rudimentary slicing, and the IoT profile from 4G were among the many parts included.
elements.sent-2411-574: The next release saw the introduction of improved slicing ("VLAN for wireless networks") and redundant transmission for high-reliability communications.
elements.sent-2411-575: Finally, with the latest approved release (Rel17, March 2022), improvements in backhaul networks, RAN slicing, public safety, and non-public networks (NPN) have been ratified.
elements.sent-2411-576: NPN is often called ``private 5G`` and makes it possible to run a 5G network as a standalone network as a local service rather than a public mobile operator.
elements.sent-2411-577: Running a 5G NPN is highly relevant in industrial automation as slicing, URLLC and access to licensed radio bands can greatly aid in security, reliability, and latency.
elements.sent-2411-578: Whereas eMMB is the use case most commonly supported by public providers, URLLC will be most relevant over NPN.
elements.sent-2411-579: Although the specification seems impressive, not much hard data is currently available to evaluate the performance of URLLC.
elements.sent-2411-580: The key strength of Ethernet is its ability to seamlessly accept new systems, adapt to topological changes, and handle variable amounts of traffic.
elements.sent-2411-581: For industrial networks where rigid deadlines are the norm rather than the exception, this dynamism can jeopardize transmission delays and be a major problem.
elements.sent-2411-582: First, packets can take one of multiple routes in such a network leading to different arrival orders, variance in delay, or being completely lost.
elements.sent-2411-583: Secondly, once a packet has started transmitting, no other packet, regardless of importance can be transmitted.
elements.sent-2411-584: Since most network bridges are store and forward, the entire packet must be fully received before it can be forwarded to the next stop.
elements.sent-2411-585: This requires buffer capacity before newly arrived packets can be forwarded to the next stop.
elements.sent-2411-586: During times of high traffic, these buffers can be exhausted and the bridge will have no other choice than to drop packets.
elements.sent-2411-587: QoS describes the treatment of frames belonging to a particular class (or stream) compared to other frames.
elements.sent-2411-588: In this context, a stream, or a flow, is a set of packets that logically belong together and should be treated similarly by the network.
elements.sent-2411-589: Several measures are available, yet whereas these are highly relevant to IT networks and the Internet, fewer apply to the needs of industrial networks.
elements.sent-2411-590: Differential Services (DiffServ) is a highly scalable class-based service where each stream is assigned to a particular service level and it is up to the network administrator to assign resources to each level.
elements.sent-2411-591: DiffServ is intended to be used across large networks and networks of networks where there can be more than one operator.
elements.sent-2411-592: However, the actual service provided to a class may differ between operators.
elements.sent-2411-593: What is more, there is no way to differentiate between streams within a class, meaning as the network grows, the interference from other streams increases.
elements.sent-2411-594: Integrated Services (IntServ) maintains a 1:1 mapping between streams and granted service levels.
elements.sent-2411-595: This ensures consistent QoS throughout the network.
elements.sent-2411-596: Since IntServ is stream-based, its scalability is tied directly to the number of streams.
elements.sent-2411-597: For large networks or networks of networks, the number of active streams will quickly outgrow the available resources in each bridge.
elements.sent-2411-598: For industrial control applications, the uncertainties of DiffServ may not suffice, and for large-scale sensor networks as foreseen in Industry 4.0, IntServ will have scalability problems.
elements.sent-2411-599: An effective solution demonstrated by Harju and Kivimaki~\cite{harju_co_operation_2000 is to use IntServ on the edges to shape and limit all incoming streams, and DiffServ in the core network to handle the bulk of traffic.}
elements.sent-2411-600: This hybrid approach can deliver adequate services, especially for large networks, which makes this one of the possible approaches presented by the Deterministic Networking (DetNet) group of IETF.
elements.sent-2411-601: TSN works by reserving buffer capacity for incoming streams and using different traffic queues on the egress port.
elements.sent-2411-602: An outgoing queue can have a shaper attached to it, which changes the traffic pattern to conform to the desired behavior.
elements.sent-2411-603: The Credit Based Shaper (CBS, ~\cite{ieee:802.1Qat ) is a class based shaper that will group streams for a given traffic class into a single queue.}
elements.sent-2411-604: Traffic belonging to this queue is then shaped so that it does not exceed the reserved bandwidth over a short service interval.
elements.sent-2411-605: Since every bridge in the domain is required to support these features, the edges will effectively limit the inflow, and the core bridges will work to reduce bursts.
elements.sent-2411-606: CBS is specifically designed to eliminate transient network overloads from bursty traffic and this has been proven to work using Network Calculus~\cite{azua_avb_modelling_2014, nc_intro_tsn_maile_2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-607: With the Time Aware Shaper (TAS~\cite{ieee:802.1Qbv-2015 ), TSN provides Time Division Multiplexing (TDM) by aligning transmission windows along the path of the traffic.}
elements.sent-2411-608: For scheduled traffic (ST) to work, all bridges must be part of a tightly synchronized time domain.
elements.sent-2411-609: When configured properly, TAS can yield extremely low delay variations and give an upper bound of 100$\mu$s transmission delay.
elements.sent-2411-610: However, even with minor time errors, carefully adjusted transmit windows can become desynchronized and effectively delay scheduled traffic for a complete TDM cycle.
elements.sent-2411-611: As the network complexity grows, this problem only worsens.
elements.sent-2411-612: This makes TAS a complex and difficult scheduler to operate and thus suitable for only the most critical streams.
elements.sent-2411-613: It also requires the network to be centrally managed.
elements.sent-2411-614: To allow more sporadic, yet critical traffic and reduce the operational complexity at the same time, a new urgency-based scheduler has been adopted by the working group.
elements.sent-2411-615: The Asynchronous Traffic Shaper~\cite{specht2016urgency has per-class queues and per-stream shaping and uses its internal clock and allows for mixing traffic types such that it can handle sporadic, critical events whilst enforcing rate limits to reduce the impact of bursts.}
elements.sent-2411-616: It has been shown that ATS will not increase the worst-case delay~\cite{nasrallah_ultra-low_2019 of traffic through the network.}
elements.sent-2411-617: CBS is appropriate for traffic that should be regular but occasionally bursty, whereas TAS is best suited for critical traffic that is regular and must be expedited through the network.
elements.sent-2411-618: For important, yet sporadic or aperiodic traffic (e.g. monitor alarms, events), ATS is appropriate.
elements.sent-2411-619: It is worth noting that the reservation of streams may fail if one or more bridges are unable to accommodate the requirements and that can only reliably be detected at run-time.
elements.sent-2411-620: It is possible to discover this a priori to a certain extent (Maile et al.~\cite{nc_intro_tsn_maile_2020 ), but as small changes to the network can result in new routes for traffic, previous scenarios may no longer be possible.}
elements.sent-2411-621: This makes it difficult to realize the full potential of a network using a fully distributed model where each node announces or reserves resources for a stream individually.. Instead.
elements.sent-2411-622: The Centralized User Configuration (CUC) and Centralized Network Configuration (CNC) provide single points of contact for end-stations.
elements.sent-2411-623: New streams are evaluated and by using the total overview, an optimal path can be created and forwarded to the CNC, which in turn configures all the required bridges before the end stations are notified and traffic can begin.
elements.sent-2411-624: For TAS, such a centralized controller is a requirement.
elements.subsection-2411-020: Common Network Requirements for Industrial Automation
elements.sent-2411-625: Missing in this list is the need for deterministic transport.
elements.sent-2411-626: From the discussion in Section \ref{sec:1_3_psn_qos , we can extend this list to also include wired industrial networks and include bounded latency and time synchronization as relevant metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-627: An updated requirement is thus: \begin{compactitem \item Open Standards Available standards and royalty-free technology should be the preferred approach as this will allow for better interoperability between vendors and a more open community that encourages academic research.}
elements.sent-2411-628: At the same time, a sender should expect the network to be available to accept new data within the pre-defined constraints (not overstepping BW bounds, etc.).
elements.sent-2411-629: The network should also be robust against jamming and other malicious attempts to disrupt the service~\cite{pirayesh_jamming_2022 \item Latency Especially closed control loops are sensitive to delay variations, but for any streams, expecting delivery within a certain time frame is a requirement.}
elements.sent-2411-630: The accuracy of the domain is dependent upon how well the network can forward time synchronization messages.. \end{compactitem.}

elements.sent-2411-631: In Table \ref{tbl:network_tech , the most common solutions found in industrial networks are listed.}
elements.sent-2411-632: Each is briefly evaluated along 3 common axes: \begin{compactenum \item Security, \item Determinism (i.e. jitter), and \item Bandwidth (upper limit).}
elements.sent-2411-633: Security can be further divided into \begin{compactenum \item External: the protocol relies on external security measures, \item Integrity: messages are accompanied by robust checksums to detect modification, \item Confidentiality: each message is encrypted using a secret known only to authorized parties, \item Secure Authentication and Authorization: each node on the system must be securely identified before being granted access to the network.\footnote{Both wired and wireless Ethernet can achieve better security by including standards such as Port-based authentication (802.1X) and Secure Device Identity (802.1AR).}}
elements.sent-2411-634: A (\cmark) indicates that the protocol can support it under ideal conditions, but it is not well suited.
elements.sent-2411-635: Likewise, (\xmark) indicates that the protocol could be made to work but should not.
elements.sent-2411-636: When it comes to the inherent predictability requirements of real-time applications, packet-switched networking alone is not adequate.
elements.sent-2411-637: This is because the load on individual network hardware in larger networks is unpredictable, and packet transmission times (as well as routes) are variable.
elements.sent-2411-638: In addition, the packet loss rates of wireless transmissions are always difficult to account for.
elements.sent-2411-639: As described in the previous section, there are industry standards that address these issues, either by sticking to packet-less bus communication or by adding specific real-time capabilities to Ethernet.
elements.sent-2411-640: However, with the convergence of IT and OT networks, as well as the highly mobile nature of the IIoT scenarios mentioned above, further real-time networking solutions need to be found.
elements.sent-2411-641: In this section, we present recent work on real-time IIoT networking.
elements.sent-2411-642: We consider peer-reviewed articles and papers from the last 5 years.
elements.sent-2411-643: The works can be either novel techniques, adaptations, or evaluations of existing standards optimized for real-time IIoT scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-644: The reviewed works are divided into subsections of equal levels of abstraction and underlying technologies.
elements.sent-2411-645: Table~\ref{tab:comparison_recent compares the works qualitatively concerning their scope and applicability to our scenario classes.}
elements.sent-2411-646: Entries marked with \cmark or \xmark ~determine whether the work is feasible for the scenario class.
elements.sent-2411-647: Entries marked with \omark ~are works that do not directly facilitate a scenario class but also do not prohibit it.
elements.sent-2411-648: It also indicates the type of source institution (academic or industry) and whether the work supports wireless communication.
