elements.sent-2411-649: The field of research with the broadest scope regards network architectures for real-time IIoT environments.
elements.sent-2411-650: Next to architectures realized by SDN, this segment also includes research on IT/OT integration and OPC UA.
elements.sent-2411-651: In~\cite{ishtaique_ul_huque_system_2019 Ishtaique ul Huque et al. present a system architecture for time-sensitive heterogeneous wireless distributed software-defined networks.}
elements.sent-2411-652: They derive this system architecture from enabling state-of-the-art technologies and their requirements.
elements.sent-2411-653: Besides the manageability of parameters in heterogeneous networks, the authors hope to facilitate TSN integration with legacy networks.
elements.sent-2411-654: Kiangala and Wang combine zero-loss redundancy protocols, TSN, and edge computing concepts to realize an intra-domain network architecture attending to the reliability and predictability needs of time-critical IIoT applications~\cite{kiangala_effective_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-655: Their solution is entirely based on IP networking.
elements.sent-2411-656: Instead of manageability via SDN they focus on technological implementations for high reliability and determinism.
elements.sent-2411-657: Network architectures have been a focus in recent works primarily due to the IT/OT convergence requirements of Industry 4.0 and IIoT applications.
elements.sent-2411-658: Foschini et al. present an SDN-enabled architecture for this and analyze its behavior during DDoS attacks~\cite{foschini_sdn-enabled_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-659: With a multi-layered network architecture, they aim to provide layer-specific security and real-time properties while making data from IIoT devices usable inside the business network of an industrial plant with autonomous vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-660: Within their DDoS attack, they showed that mere changes to the network architecture cannot secure the highly critical lower levels of the network (i.e. physical machines).
elements.sent-2411-661: The Open Platform Communication Unified Architecture (OPC UA) was introduced in 2008 as an interoperability standard for reliable information exchange and has gained momentum with discussions on IIoT networks.
elements.sent-2411-662: In recent years Li et al. presented an OPC UA architecture with TSN capabilities for the IIoT~\cite{li_practical_2020 and Morato et al. published a general assessment of OPC UA implementations under current Industry 4.0 requirements~\cite{morato_assessment_2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-663: Both works showed that OPC UA can be used to integrate real-time communication requirements into Ethernet-based networks in the manufacturing industry.
elements.sent-2411-664: When industrial networks become more complex and contain mixed computing systems and real-time requirements Software-Defined Networking can help with managing the resulting heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-665: Wang and Li present an SDN-based solution for task offloading in IIoT environments \cite{wang_adaptive_2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-666: A computing mode selector is implemented in the SDN controller and offloaded tasks are given priority based on real-time parameters.
elements.sent-2411-667: The network transmits offloaded tasks to fog computing resources in order of this priority.
elements.sent-2411-668: Another solution that addresses mixed timing criticality in large networks is proposed by Lin et al. \cite{lin_dte-sdn_2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-669: SDN is used to implement a traffic engineering approach to schedule the transfer of delay-sensitive traffic.
elements.sent-2411-670: This works using real-time analysis of the network and monitoring the QoS metrics of participating links.
elements.sent-2411-671: Based on these metrics a dynamic scheduler with multi-path routing capabilities manages the flows.
elements.sent-2411-672: Zeng et al. address flaws of Industrial Ethernet, namely poor scalability, insufficient self-configuration capabilities, and increased costs due to the use of proprietary hardware and present a time-slotted software defined Industrial Ethernet \cite{zeng2019time .}
elements.sent-2411-673: The approach contains a time synchronization mechanism based on PTP and a system architecture for time slot-based industrial switches.
elements.sent-2411-674: Using SDN, the implementation becomes scalable, and reconfigurable, and is not dependent on frequent infrastructure changes.
elements.sent-2411-675: In recent years, researchers have addressed various shortcomings of TSN by extending, improving, and integrating it with other systems and frameworks.
elements.sent-2411-676: Schriegel and Jasperneite worked on a bridging mode called Time-Aware Forwarding (TAF) \cite{schriegel_migration_2021 to increase the flexibility of TSN and thereby accelerate the migration from Profinet to TSN in industrial communication.}
elements.sent-2411-677: With the approach, already existing networking hardware can be made compatible with TSN.
elements.sent-2411-678: Tackling the scheduling of time-triggered traffic in TSN, Bujosa et al. present Hermes, a heuristic multi-queue scheduler \cite{bujosa2022hermes .}
elements.sent-2411-679: With this improvement to the synthesis algorithm of gate control lists in TSN networks, they increase network scalability by reducing its scheduling complexity.
elements.sent-2411-680: To control network jitter, incoming packets are buffered along the routing path in a TSN network.
elements.sent-2411-681: Chaine et al. propose a solution Egress TT that performs this buffering only at the final network node \cite{chaine2022egress and optimizes it for network scalability.}
elements.sent-2411-682: The solution allows to use of non-TSN networking on the routing path and reduces computational complexity while somewhat increasing latencies.
elements.sent-2411-683: In~\cite{bruckner_introduction_2019 , Bruckner et al. showed how the ubiquitous OPC-UA protocol benefits from using TSN.}
elements.sent-2411-684: Similarly, in~\cite{8823854 Agarwal et al. demonstrated improved reliability and reduced delay for Data Distribution Services (DDS) when TSN was used to protect and expedite the traffic through the network.}
elements.sent-2411-685: The need for reliable and deterministic wireless communication can be derived from most scenarios presented in Section \ref{sec:1_3_scenarios .}
elements.sent-2411-686: The following works from the past years attend to real-time wireless communication.
elements.sent-2411-687: A seemingly natural step is to transfer TSN methods to work with wireless technologies.
elements.sent-2411-688: The concomitant challenges and research objectives have been thoroughly investigated by Cavalcanti et al.~\cite{cavalcanti2019extending .}
elements.sent-2411-689: Next to a comprehensive overview of state-of-the-art wireless technologies and IEEE 802.1 TSN standards, they discuss new approaches on top of next-generation wireless technologies (e.g. WiFi 6, WigGig, and 5G) to overcome the radio-inherent challenges concerning packet loss and jitter.
elements.sent-2411-690: Sudhakran et al. build on top of this work and present a methodology to map application layer timing requirements in a collaborative robot application to the link layer, based on wireless TSN over WiFi~\cite{sudhakaran_enabling_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-691: Rather than going further into the TSN standards, they address how to classify traffic, extract time-critical flow parameters, and define an efficient schedule for the end-to-end QoS approach.
elements.sent-2411-692: Yun et al. recognize the older real-time aware WiFi (RT-WiFi~\cite{wei2013rt ) technology which is implemented on common off-the-shelf hardware and hence hard to update and maintain.}
elements.sent-2411-693: In their work, they propose a software-defined radio approach of RT-WiFi implemented on an FPGA called SRT-WiFi ~\cite{yun_rt-wifi_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-694: They provide a fully open system architecture and perform extensive evaluations on a multi-cluster SRT-WiFi testbed.
elements.sent-2411-695: The fifth generation of mobile networking (5G) promises several improvements and enabling technologies for Industry 4.0 and smart factory applications.
elements.sent-2411-696: Among them are network slicing, private management, and ultra-low latencies.
elements.sent-2411-697: Network slicing allows for logically separated virtual networks over the same physical 5G network.
elements.sent-2411-698: Both, the integration of different business layers as well as connection-specific latency control could be realized with this.
elements.sent-2411-699: Walia et al. studied the usage of network slicing in a smart factory and developed a management model for 5G slicing in the domain~\cite{walia_5g_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-700: As the effective usage of 5G for Industry 4.0 environments requires self-managed or "private" 5G networks, some recent works have focused on their implementation and feasibility.
elements.sent-2411-701: Aijaz et al. present an overview of the motivation behind and functions of private 5G networks~\cite{aijaz_private_2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-702: They present a large number of potential use cases and benefits for the IIoT and portray private 5G networking as the future of industrial networks.
elements.sent-2411-703: Wen et al. come to a similar conclusion while pointing out the early stage at which private 5G networking currently is~\cite{wen_private_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-704: Actual implementation and management are currently only feasible for large companies with a networking background.
elements.sent-2411-705: In their paper covering critical mMTC, Pokhrel et al.~\cite{pokhrel_towards_2020 , argue that although URLLC is capable of providing excellent real-time behavior, the cost of doing so means relatively few devices can be connected in such a fashion.}
elements.sent-2411-706: Similarly, where mMTC is capable of connecting a plethora of devices, the individual QoS is not compatible with sensor networks.
elements.sent-2411-707: To be able to meet the need for a large, wireless sensor network, they propose ``critical mMTC``.
elements.sent-2411-708: Only a few works have considered making improvements to the lowest layer in IIoT networks.
elements.sent-2411-709: The following research papers are concerned with securing embedded devices that are (somewhat newly) connected to large networks and responsible to execute real-time tasks.
elements.sent-2411-710: Johansson et al. use MAC layer filtering to mitigate the effects of best-effort traffic reception on real-time tasks \cite{johansson2022priority .}
elements.sent-2411-711: They extend the network driver of VxWorks to support multiple receive queues and an interface that supports the configuration of the Ethernet Controller's MAC filter.
elements.sent-2411-712: This way, packets are filtered and enqueued based on their priority in hardware, making it possible to treat incoming traffic based on real-time considerations.
elements.sent-2411-713: In~\cite{austad_ftc , TSN was used to extend Timed C, an extension to the C programming language, and include network channels as a primitive in the language.}
elements.sent-2411-714: When faced with large network loads, the critical traffic was reliably forwarded with minimal delay and jitter demonstrating the usefulness of TSN in distributed real-time systems.
elements.sent-2411-715: Traditionally, industrial networks have been isolated, mostly homogeneous, and designed with dedicated protocols and hardware to meet stringent reliability and real-time requirements.
elements.sent-2411-716: With the coming transformation of Industry 4.0 and IT/OT convergence, industrial networks need to become more configurable and flexible, enabling a wider range of applications than before.
elements.sent-2411-717: This, of course, requires tighter connectivity between networked systems, as well as a higher degree of dynamism for nodes joining and leaving the network.
elements.subsection-2411-027: Bridging the Gap between IIoT Scenarios and Network Requirements
elements.sent-2411-718: Looking at Table \ref{tbl:network_tech , the wireless networking requirements of Scenario Classes I and VI appear to be the most difficult to meet.}
elements.sent-2411-719: While several wireless communication standards exist, they either cannot meet the strict latency predictability requirements or cannot meet the bandwidth requirements of multimedia data.
elements.sent-2411-720: Once high reliability and latency are taken into account, current wireless networks do not scale sufficiently, i.e., a research gap exists where critical real-time requirements meet a need for wireless communication.
elements.sent-2411-721: Current wireless real-time protocols are generally unable to scale to the required size and the aggregated bandwidth expected in the coming years.
elements.sent-2411-722: Similarly, WLAN, and especially WLAN6E, can be seen as an attractive alternative with high bandwidth, low latency, improved timing accuracy, and readily available COTS hardware.
elements.sent-2411-723: Comparing this to Table \ref{tab:comparison_recent , it can be seen that this is where wireless TSN technologies will play a significant role in enablement.}
elements.sent-2411-724: The main gap-filler distilled from this work is wireless technologies that can provide a measure of predictability.
elements.sent-2411-725: However, the overall feasibility of this must be viewed with caution.
elements.sent-2411-726: While latency guarantees can be improved by real-time aware wireless packet switching, the inherent unpredictability of radio must not be overlooked.
elements.sent-2411-727: While privately managed 5G networks seem to be a part of the solution, the high complexity and cost make it impractical for many companies and use cases.
elements.sent-2411-728: The presented works regarding wired networks show that ``real-time networks`` are more static and carefully planned than traditional IT networks tend to be.
elements.sent-2411-729: Even though technologies such as TSN help bring determinism to COTS Ethernet, achieving high flexibility and reconfigurability is a largely unsolved challenge.
elements.sent-2411-730: Combining TSN with its centralized controllers (CNC, CUC) and SDN is one promising avenue that would extend to both WLAN and 5G solutions.
elements.sent-2411-731: The downside of such a solution is the vast complexity of all systems; needless to say, the factory network of the future will be almost unrecognizable from today's, and be a major asset of its own.
elements.sent-2411-732: Standardization and holistic large-scale implementations also are open challenges to the remaining subfields.
elements.sent-2411-733: Even where research papers are feasible for specific scenario class requirements these only focus on individual aspects.
elements.sent-2411-734: The feasibility of the presented approaches still has to be validated in complex engineered systems.
elements.sent-2411-735: Industry 4.0 is still missing a fixed set of standards and technologies which will be important for trust in safety and reliability.
elements.sent-2411-736: The approaches from the works presented have to be merged and implemented in holistic system architectures including all actors of the network, from embedded real-time devices, network devices, and compute nodes such as edge servers and cloud systems.
elements.sent-2411-737: Real-time demands on networks in industrial environments have risen with the advent of the IoT paradigm and Industry 4.0.
elements.sent-2411-738: In this chapter, we listed common and anticipated use cases for industrial networks and identified the criteria needed to meet the requirements of each.
elements.sent-2411-739: We reviewed established industrial networking technologies and protocols and presented current academic works regarding the topic.
elements.sent-2411-740: By comparing different networking technologies to these criteria and use cases, we give an overview of challenges and solutions concerning IIoT scenarios from a real-time networking perspective.
elements.sent-2411-741: The remainder of this thesis provides contributions to real-time communication research, not only for industrial use-cases, but real-time embedded systems in general.
elements.sent-2411-742: In particular, embedded system networking subsystems are analyzed and extended to meet the demands of modern use case scenarios from classes I, II, \& IV.
elements.sent-2411-743: In Chapter \ref{cha:2_3_software we present a real-time aware IP networking stack for IoT devices \cite{blumschein2022differentiating .}}
elements.sent-2411-744: By classifying packets as early as possible and inheriting real-time task priorities to the networking task, they prevent priority inversions between a high-priority task and the processing of lower-priority packets.
elements.sent-2411-745: Additionally, incoming packet rates are limited to prevent a system overload from incoming packets.
elements.sent-2411-746: In Chapter \ref{cha:2_4_hardware a hardware-based approach with similar goals is presented.}
elements.sent-2411-747: Using a multi-queue NIC design, packets are reordered and filtered based on the priority of the receiving real-time task.
elements.sent-2411-748: Furthermore, real-time aware interrupt moderation is proposed that reduces the number of interrupts generated by incoming traffic while guaranteeing low latencies for high-priority packets.
elements.sent-2411-749: A SW/HW co-design for real-time aware packet reception \cite{behnke2023towards is presented in Chapter \ref{cha:2_5_codesign .}}
elements.sent-2411-750: The challenge of task offloading in modern industrial systems with wireless mobile machines as identified in Scenarios I \& V is considered in Chapter \ref{cha:3_3_offloading .}
elements.sent-2411-751: We present a system architecture and distributed scheduler for the offloading of real-time tasks to local edge servers.
elements.chapter-2411-006: Connecting Real-Time Devices to IP Networks
elements.sent-2411-752: To this end, we first introduce the issue of \acp{irq in real-time systems and further specify the role of network generated interrupts.}
elements.sent-2411-753: In Section~\ref{sec:2_2_methodology we present the evaluation methodology of our analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-754: We then evaluate the timing measurements of critical tasks on microcontrollers running vendor-supplied \ac{rtos , network drivers, and network stack tasks under various network-triggered \ac{irq loads.}}
elements.sent-2411-755: To uncover any existing mitigations in the hardware and closed-source drivers, a pseudo-network driver is designed to serve as a second \ac{irq source.}
elements.sent-2411-756: Thus, we performed experiments on two setups: One that treats NICs and drivers as black boxes, and one that simulates network interrupts on the machines.
elements.sent-2411-757: Measurements are taken and compared between real and pseudo network packet processing.
elements.sent-2411-758: Section~\ref{sec:2_2_evaluation presents the empirical results and Section~\ref{sec:2_2_discussion discusses them.}}
elements.sent-2411-759: In the final section of this chapter, we present a testbed that facilitates on-device research on the impact of packet reception on real-time performance and helps developers test their applications accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-760: Parts of this chapter have been peer-reviewed and pre-published in two conference papers~\cite{behnke2020interrupting,bender2021pieres .}
elements.sent-2411-761: As motivated in Chapter~\ref{cha:1_1_introduction , embedded devices that control machines in the physical world have been part of industrial processes as well as home and automotive appliances for decades.}
elements.sent-2411-762: Unlike general-purpose computers, these devices must meet timing constraints.
elements.sent-2411-763: To this end, \acp{rtos are used, which are lightweight and provide guarantees towards the timing predictability of tasks~\cite{rtos .}}
elements.sent-2411-764: Typically, a preemptive task scheduler allows the configuration of different priorities for concurrent tasks, so that the most time-critical tasks always precede less critical ones.
elements.sent-2411-765: While implementation flaws in \acp{rtos and their tasks still occasionally occur, decades of research and development in this area have mitigated many reliability and security issues.}
elements.sent-2411-766: At the same time, they introduce a degree of unpredictability into the process.
elements.sent-2411-767: Since the corresponding \acp{isr are handled by the processor, the scheduler of an \ac{os has no control over their execution.}}
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{2_2_problem/priority_inversion.pdf} \caption{Priority inversion: Illustration of priority inversions caused by network packet \acp{irq} and processing.} \label{fig:prio_inv}
elements.sent-2411-768: A common example of an unwanted effect of this is priority inversion, where the execution of a high-priority task is preempted by an \acp{isr belonging to a lower-priority task.}
elements.sent-2411-769: However, by keeping \acp{isr execution times to a minimum and considering worst-case scenarios during design, most traditional embedded systems can handle \acp{irq without missing deadlines.}}
elements.sent-2411-770: However, in the past, the environment controlled by embedded systems tended to be self-contained, the number of environment-triggered \acp{irq was typically small, and their impact therefore predictable.}
elements.sent-2411-771: With the advent of \ac{iot in industrial applications, this premise has changed.}
elements.sent-2411-772: This is especially dangerous for critical real-time tasks on networked embedded microcontrollers: Embedded systems have to handle the additional resource consumption of non-critical networking tasks, and the required \ac{nic introduces a new source of unpredictability, as incoming packets trigger \acp{irq that disrupt the flow of scheduled tasks~\cite{hip .}}}
elements.sent-2411-773: This can lead to a critical load of interrupts and triggered network tasks in the \ac{rtos , invalidating real-time guarantees and thereby reducing system reliability.}
elements.sent-2411-774: Figure~\ref{fig:prio_inv illustrates the resulting priority inversion.}
elements.sent-2411-775: In this example, an embedded system controlling a machine is connected to a network for control and updating purposes.
elements.sent-2411-776: A low-priority update task sends a request to a remote server before being preempted by a monitoring task.
elements.sent-2411-777: While this task is running, a network packet is received that triggers an \ac{irq (1).}
elements.sent-2411-778: Since the network processing task has a higher priority, it preempts the monitoring task and processes the packet, which turns out to be a machine control instruction.
elements.sent-2411-779: The payload is forwarded to the critical control task, which executes the command.
elements.sent-2411-780: Monitoring is then preempted again for the same reason, with the same result (2).
elements.sent-2411-781: However, now the packets requested by the low-priority updater task begin to arrive, each triggering an \ac{irq that preempts the critical control task.}
elements.sent-2411-782: Since it has a higher priority than the network driver, the processing of these packets is postponed until the control task has finished (4).
elements.sent-2411-783: Before the control task can resume, the update packets are processed and forwarded to the updater, which, as the lowest priority process, finishes last.
elements.sent-2411-784: In this example, we observe two instances of priority inversion.
elements.sent-2411-785: First, at (3), when the low-priority packet reception takes precedence over the critical control task, and second, at (4), when the processing of the low-priority packets takes precedence over the monitoring task.
elements.sent-2411-786: In the first case, the resulting time shift of the high-priority task per packet is equal to the execution time of the \ac{isr and context switches (in red).}
elements.sent-2411-787: The upper half of the protocol processing in the network driver can be expected to be longer, but somewhat manageable, since this task is subject to OS scheduling.
elements.sent-2411-788: In the following, this chapter analyzes the timing requirements of both situations.
elements.sent-2411-789: As motivated, the inclusion of network controllers in embedded real-time systems introduces an unpredictable source of interrupts.
elements.sent-2411-790: The goal of this chapter is to provide methods for the analysis of the interrupt impact in IoT environments.
elements.sent-2411-791: This includes the execution of ISRs, network drivers, and network stack tasks, as well as the robustness of the overall system under high network loads.
elements.sent-2411-792: This section outlines the challenges and assumptions for the analysis.
elements.sent-2411-793: Available IoT devices are microcontrollers with the means to wirelessly connect to networks and handle a variety of different network protocols, comparable to network stack implementations in general computing.
elements.sent-2411-794: At the same time, these systems are designed to be deployed in untrusted environments, more or less directly connected to the Internet~\cite{iotnetstack .}
elements.sent-2411-795: Packet floods caused by failures and security breaches also expose potentially critical systems deployed in remote networks.
elements.sent-2411-796: IPv6, whose large address space is seen as an enabler for the deployment of the large number of IoT devices~\cite{ipv6 , may also lead to the abandonment of the obfuscating properties of Network Address Translation (NAT) required in IPv4.}
elements.sent-2411-797: Due to these circumstances, we consider networked embedded systems of increased vulnerability while controlling critical systems with real-time constraints.
elements.sent-2411-798: The popularity of the IoT has led to the mass production of IP-enabled (mostly WiFi) devices that are cheaply available.
elements.sent-2411-799: To make the somewhat complex programming of embedded systems more accessible in the IoT context, devices come with programming frameworks that hide low-level software such as networking stacks and drivers.
elements.sent-2411-800: Examples include Espressif's IoT Development Framework, Arduino libraries, and Particle's DeviceOS.
elements.sent-2411-801: While it is technically possible to program the examined modules without their respective frameworks, the immense workload involved in implementing and integrating the network drivers and stacks makes this infeasible in most cases.
elements.sent-2411-802: An integration of the embedded WiFi chips into fully manageable and transparent real-time systems is hindered by the unavailability of the driver source code.
elements.sent-2411-803: When designing and developing real-time systems, the developer is responsible for ensuring that all guarantees (deadlines) are met in the worst-case scenario.
elements.sent-2411-804: In the case of externally generated interrupts, it is therefore necessary for the developer to consider the maximum frequency of interrupts and their effects in the design of the system.
elements.sent-2411-805: Network generated interrupts, on the other hand, leave the designer with few options, such as disabling interrupts or reserving a physical core for network tasks~\cite{hermes .}
elements.sent-2411-806: While both actions may solve the problem of interrupt flooding over the network, the loss of a core (if even available) and remote access to the embedded system may not be feasible.
elements.sent-2411-807: In addition, limited access to low-level functions results in a loss of control over the real-time system, which should generally be designed holistically.
elements.sent-2411-808: Changing network task priorities, choosing which parts of the network stack to run, and which protocols to support are all taken away from the developer in these frameworks.
elements.sent-2411-809: Concluding, we make the following assumptions for our analysis.
elements.sent-2411-810: WiFi connections belong to the most commonly used interfaces, since no modifications to available hardware has to be made and the technology is mature.
elements.sent-2411-811: A certain impact of networking tasks and interrupts on \ac{mcu utilization and timing predictability in real-time embedded systems seems to be inherent.}
elements.sent-2411-812: With the experimental methodology and setup, we aim to analyze this impact on two current \acp{mcu used in state-of-the-art \ac{cots IoT devices.}}
elements.sent-2411-813: For the quantitative analysis, we have designed two sets of experiments that observe the timing metrics of a periodic task.
elements.sent-2411-814: The experiments are run under different network loads with two considered interrupt setups (see Section~\ref{sec:intrsetups ).}
elements.sent-2411-815: The experiment permutations were additionally adjusted to account for differences in priorities of pre-existing network tasks.
elements.sent-2411-816: We defined two sets of experiments observing different metrics under changing network loads.
elements.sent-2411-817: In preemptive real-time systems, tasks are brought into the active state by interrupts, timers, or synchronization calls from other tasks.
elements.sent-2411-818: The critical task we observe is periodically called by a timer with period p and deadline d.
elements.sent-2411-819: We define lateness l as the time the task takes longer to finish than its deadline allows, hence $ l = t_{end - d $, where $t_{end}$ is the time at which one process cycle of the task has finished, as shown in the Figure~\ref{fig:lateness .}}
elements.sent-2411-820: To see the relationship between the network load and the delay caused, the task does not miss its deadline without any external influence.
elements.sent-2411-821: Once we reach a load where the task misses its deadline, the lateness starts to accumulate over iterations.
elements.sent-2411-822: We plot the accumulated lateness per second.
elements.sent-2411-823: In the second set of experiments, the observed task runs in a closed loop for a duration of $\Delta t$ in an interval i.
elements.sent-2411-824: By measuring the number of cycles the critical task completes in a $\Delta t$ under different network loads, we can determine the resource utilization of network operations as a function of the number of packets received.
elements.sent-2411-825: The experiments were performed on two widely used IoT development boards in a similar performance and price range.
elements.sent-2411-826: One is equipped with a dual-core ESP32 chip running at 160~MHz, the other with a single-core Particle Photon (P0) ARM Cortex M3 running at 120~MHz.
elements.sent-2411-827: While both devices have ports of the FreeRTOS operating system, the vendor-provided programming frameworks differ significantly in terms of programmability and port-specific implementations.
elements.sent-2411-828: Both devices are programmed using their respective frameworks\footnote{Particle DeviceOS, \url{https://www.particle.io/device-os/ }\footnote{ESP-IDF, \url{https://docs.espressif.com/projects/esp-idf/en/latest/esp32/ .}}}
elements.sent-2411-829: While the frameworks themselves and the operating systems are open source, some low-level software, such as WiFi drivers, is only available as binary objects.
elements.sent-2411-830: While the differences between the frameworks and the processing chips used limit the degree of direct comparability, they do allow us to evaluate the possibilities of development within the constraints of the frameworks.
elements.sent-2411-831: In this way, all results are realistic for the systems tested within their established workflows.
elements.sent-2411-832: To keep connections alive, driver and network tasks must be kept running on the devices.
elements.sent-2411-833: These are called by NIC-triggered ISRs, but can be preempted by a higher priority task on the same core.
elements.sent-2411-834: To evaluate the different shares of delay caused by ISRs and networking tasks, experiments are repeated with observed task priorities chosen above, equal to, and below the driver.
elements.sent-2411-835: The default network task and driver priorities are set by the frameworks as shown in Table~\ref{tab:priorities .}
elements.sent-2411-836: The WiFi driver priorities cannot be changed.

elements.sent-2411-837: To analyze the impact on latencies and compute resource utilization, the devices under test are subjected to different network loads.
elements.sent-2411-838: One of the main difficulties in studying the process flow triggered by a received packet is the unavailability of large portions of the driver source code.
elements.sent-2411-839: A quantitative analysis of the number of times an ISR is actually called is therefore difficult to perform.
elements.sent-2411-840: To compare the relative impact of ISRs and low-level packet processing, two interrupt setups are run on the devices.
elements.sent-2411-841: In the first setup, network packets are sent to the devices over a WiFi connection and handled by the driver and networking tasks provided by the framework (Figure~\ref{fig:setup_net ).}
elements.sent-2411-842: We use the second core of the ESP32 to run a minimally configured UDP server that binds a port.
elements.sent-2411-843: While the UDP server task does not directly affect our observed task, the impact of packets received on used ports differs from the impact generated by unused ports.
elements.sent-2411-844: Since P0 does not have a dual-core processor, no UDP server was running and interrupts were generated externally.
elements.sent-2411-845: Next, we implemented an analogous task flow to perform WiFi driver-independent experiments (Figure~\ref{fig:setup_sim ).}
elements.sent-2411-846: A network task simulation performs much of the same actions as a packet received over a network: Upon registering the interrupt, which in this case is triggered by an input pin, a short ISR is called that preempts the currently running process to copy a packet descriptor to a FreeRTOS queue.
elements.sent-2411-847: A pseudo driver task under the control of the OS scheduler waits for packets in this queue and unblocks it if it is not empty to process the entries.

elements.sent-2411-848: Changes in network load directly affect the number of ISR, driver, and network stack calls in the operating system.
elements.sent-2411-849: Depending on the system and interrupt type, network loads were increased in steps of 10, 100, or 1,000 packets per second and held for measurement.
elements.sent-2411-850: In addition, experiments were conducted with traffic bursts of 120,000 packets per second for one second.
elements.sent-2411-851: Given the limited resources of the target devices, this can be considered a fault injection experiment.
elements.sent-2411-852: The priority of the observed critical task is modified with respect to the priority of the WiFi driver between different runs as described in Section~\ref{sec:priorities .}
elements.sent-2411-853: This section presents our preliminary empirical results of how interrupt loads impact the lateness of critical tasks and utilization of CPUs.
elements.sent-2411-854: The first set of experiments was conducted to measure the lateness of the observed task under increasing packet loads.
elements.sent-2411-855: Figures~\ref{fig:plot1 and~\ref{fig:plot5 show the lateness results under simulated and real network traffic on the ESP32.}}
elements.sent-2411-856: Both show a linear increase in lateness with increasing packet load once lateness occurs for priorities chosen below the driver priority, with real IP packet impact reaching 50\% lateness increase per packet per second.
elements.sent-2411-857: While the absolute values depend on the priority, the relative increase is universal to the system.
elements.sent-2411-858: The differences between the priorities correspond to the priority of the fixed network tasks when the \acp{mcu are fully utilized.}
elements.sent-2411-859: If the monitored task has a lower priority than the network tasks, it will starve as soon as too many packets arrive.
elements.sent-2411-860: If the monitored task has a critical priority, it will starve the network tasks.
elements.sent-2411-861: As can be seen, this results in no impact, suggesting that no ISRs are triggered by the NIC in this case.
elements.sent-2411-862: Figure~\ref{fig:plot5 also shows that the impact of incoming packets is much higher when addressed to a port that is not open.}
elements.sent-2411-863: This may be due to the partial deactivation of network tasks when UDP buffers in the network stack are full.
elements.sent-2411-864: The lateness results under bursts of traffic do not differ from those under continuous load, and no other effects on the systems were observed.
elements.sent-2411-865: Further burst results are therefore omitted from this work, as this was the case for all burst experiments.

elements.sent-2411-880: With real network packets, the system crashes at 980 packets per second when the observed task is given a lower priority than the WiFi driver, and at 2,000 packets per second with the same priority.
elements.sent-2411-881: The performance of the task decreases by 2\% per packet per second until it reaches equal resource utilization with the driver (same priority) or is gradually displaced by it (lower priority).
elements.sent-2411-882: This section discusses the findings from the experiments and the feasibility of IP networking on \acp{mcu .}
elements.sent-2411-883: The evaluation results show that the performance and the realtime performance are directly dependent on the incoming IP network load.
elements.sent-2411-884: The overhead of receiving packets is high for both continuous floods and short bursts.
elements.sent-2411-885: The WiFi driver and the network stack use all the computational resources they need to handle packets unless they are preempted.
elements.sent-2411-886: However, the results also show that NIC-triggered ISRs have no impact on the observed systems.
elements.sent-2411-887: This observation is made when incoming packets are not handled because the driver is preempted by a task of critical priority.
elements.sent-2411-888: In the case of P0, it was not possible to assign a higher priority to a task than the priority of the WiFi driver.
elements.sent-2411-889: Therefore, it was not possible to measure NIC-generated ISR durations.
elements.sent-2411-890: To mitigate the observed real-time guarantee violations on the tested devices, developers still have some options.
elements.sent-2411-891: The ESP IDF provides a priority level above the WiFi task's priority.
elements.sent-2411-892: Running a critical task here will preempt the driver.
elements.sent-2411-893: For critical code snippets, it is also possible to disable all or only the WiFi interrupt.
elements.sent-2411-894: This is the only option under Particle's DeviceOS.
elements.sent-2411-895: The key takeaway is that all mission-critical operations must be incorporated into a critical task that is independent of any signals received over the network.
elements.sent-2411-896: Therefore, running command and control operations over the network introduces a high safety risk to hard real-time systems.
elements.sent-2411-897: In systems where the only way to disable network interrupts is to disable all interrupts, the critical task must be completely independent of any I/O signals.
elements.sent-2411-898: However, this invalidates most real-time embedded system use cases.
elements.sent-2411-899: Network driver tasks, which are responsible for much of the impact on timing predictability and thus latency, have very high priority in the frameworks of the systems tested.
elements.sent-2411-900: There is no obvious way to change their priorities.
elements.sent-2411-901: This severely limits the leeway that critical tasks have.
elements.sent-2411-902: While short and independent tasks and code blocks can be executed in a safe manner, this is also where it ends.
elements.sent-2411-903: This leads to the conclusion that network driver tasks are currently given a priority level in RTOSs that is not suitable for critical real-time systems.
elements.sent-2411-904: Using network connectivity only for monitoring largely timing independent tasks may still be an option if one is willing to provide the resources and prioritize the necessary tasks appropriately.
elements.sent-2411-905: As discussed, current programming frameworks for IoT devices seem to be designed for certain connectivity guarantees rather than for the real-time of critical applications.
elements.sent-2411-906: However, this does not apply to the evaluated systems either, as can be seen from the results.
elements.sent-2411-907: Under the (rather general) conditions of the test setup, even low traffic loads lead to WiFi driver crashes and, depending on the framework configuration, even to total system failures.
elements.sent-2411-908: This could be reliably reproduced on P0 (see Figure~\ref{fig:plot11 ), where the presence of a task with critical priority also prevented the driver from reconnecting after a connection loss.}
elements.sent-2411-909: Similar driver crashes were observed in some iterations of the tests on the ESP32.
elements.sent-2411-910: While implementing the system for our analysis, we recognized a need to make it easier to run experiments for specific real-time applications on connected embedded systems.
elements.sent-2411-911: For future research on the identified interrupt issues, we have designed a testbed.
elements.sent-2411-912: Its goal is to allow researchers to perform experiments and engineers to test their code with respect to network interrupts.
elements.sent-2411-913: The testbed should fulfill the following requirements.
elements.sent-2411-914: It has to \begin{compactitem \item run on real IoT hardware, \item be capable of simulating multiple NIC implementations, \item be able to simulate multiple network traffic scenarios, \item be easily configurable, and \item have minimal performance impact on the tested process.. \end{compactitem.}}
elements.sent-2411-915: The operation of the testbed is shown in Figure~\ref{process .}
elements.sent-2411-916: The testbed is set up on a microcontroller.
elements.sent-2411-917: The user then inserts the code of the critical process under test and selects either a uniform load configuration, a random load configuration, or parses a recorded network trace.
elements.sent-2411-918: The user then configures the NIC and flashes the testbed to the device.
elements.sent-2411-919: Measured metrics can be configured by the user and range from execution time and number of interrupts to more complex metrics such as the ratio between interrupt sources (see Section~\ref{sec:2_2_experiments ).}
elements.sent-2411-920: The testbed needs to model different \acp{nic that can be configured by the user.}
elements.sent-2411-921: The simplest NIC would notify the \ac{mcu of the arrival of each packet by throwing an interrupt for each one.}
elements.sent-2411-922: Alternatively, some modern NICs offer interrupt moderation.
elements.sent-2411-923: To incorporate this into the testbed, we offer a choice between a simple NIC model without interrupt moderation and several smarter NIC implementations.
elements.sent-2411-924: For the simple NIC, the duration $d(l)$ of an interrupt depends on the packet length $l$ and is simply modeled as a length-dependent delay $d_l$ that is invoked $l$ times plus a constant length-independent delay $d_c$ that is an overhead that is invoked for each interrupt: \begin{equation}d(l) = d_l \cdot l + d_c\end{equation} Different simple NIC implementations can be characterized by the user by setting the values for the length dependent delay $d_l$ and the length independent delay $d_c$.
elements.sent-2411-925: For more sophisticated NIC simulations with interrupt moderation (cf.
elements.sent-2411-926: Section~\ref{sec:interrupt_moderation ), we support out-of-the-box definition of NICs with a counter mode, a timer mode, or a combination.}
elements.sent-2411-927: In this case, each part of the interrupt duration model, the packet length and the corresponding dependent and independent delay, is modeled twice, once for the simulated ISR and once for a simulated receiver task.
elements.sent-2411-928: A counter mode NIC does not generate an interrupt for every packet received, but instead counts the incoming packets, stores them in a buffer, and, after a certain number of packets, generates an interrupt for all of them.
elements.sent-2411-929: The counter and buffer are then reset.
elements.sent-2411-930: Another option is a NIC with timer mode.
elements.sent-2411-931: In this case, a delay timer of a specified duration is set for an incoming packet.
elements.sent-2411-932: When the timer expires, an interrupt is generated.
elements.sent-2411-933: If more packets arrive before the timer expires, the timer is reset without triggering an interrupt.
elements.sent-2411-934: However, problems may arise if the timer is constantly reset by incoming packets, never allowing an interrupt to be generated.
elements.sent-2411-935: The combination of both modes avoids this problem.
elements.sent-2411-936: The arrival of packets with corresponding timestamps over an observed period of time constitutes a load scenario.
elements.sent-2411-937: Since we want to simulate different scenarios, the testbed offers uniform loads, random loads, and user-defined/recorded loads.
elements.sent-2411-938: The uniform loads have a constant receive frequency.
elements.sent-2411-939: Random loads use a Poisson distribution to model the arrival of new packets.
elements.sent-2411-940: The Poisson distribution is obtained by inverse transform sampling with a uniform distribution.
elements.sent-2411-941: Assuming that the number of incoming packets per interval $p_i$ is Poisson distributed, the inter-arrival time $d_i$ is exponentially distributed: \begin{equation}\begin{split} p_i \sim Poisson(\lambda), \; d_i = p_{i+1} - p_i \Rightarrow d_i \sim Exp(\lambda) \hat{d}_i = F^{-1}(u_i) = - \frac{1}{\lambda}\ln(1-u_i) \hat{=} -\frac{1}{\lambda}\ln(u_i). \end{split}\end{equation} Using inverse transform sampling (as described in~\cite{Murphy2012 Section 23.2), we determine the empirical delays between packets $\hat{d _i$ by sampling $u_i \sim U(0,1)$ and calculating $\hat{d _i$.}}}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation} p_i \sim Poisson(\lambda), \; d_i = p_{i+1} - p_i \Rightarrow d_i \sim Exp(\lambda) \hat{d}_i = F^{-1}(u_i) = - \frac{1}{\lambda}\ln(1-u_i) \hat{=} -\frac{1}{\lambda}\ln(u_i). \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-942: By setting the parameter $\lambda$, different randomized Poisson loads can be specified.
elements.sent-2411-943: As a third option, the testbed allows you to replay recorded network scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-944: Two validation and two demonstration experiments were performed using a large summation (in a loop) with a conditional statement at each step of the iteration as the user code.
elements.sent-2411-945: The testbed is implemented on an ESP32.
elements.sent-2411-946: The parsing script for the recorded network scenarios (PCAPs) is written in Python and generates C++ code.
elements.sent-2411-947: The last requirement of the testbed is met by using both cores to separate the computational load of the testbed code from the tested user code.
elements.sent-2411-948: All validation tests were performed with a Poisson distributed random load.
elements.sent-2411-949: The following results show only a selection of the experiments performed on the testbed.

Right: Comparison of the execution time of three Poisson-distributed random network load scenarios for different $\lambda$ in dependence of the counter threshold.}

elements.sent-2411-951: This test was performed using the combined interrupt moderation mode.
elements.sent-2411-952: Both the execution time and the number of interrupts decrease as the packet delay timer increases.
elements.sent-2411-953: Since the number of packets is constant, the execution time is reduced by using interrupt moderation.
elements.sent-2411-954: This gain from increasing the packet delay timer comes with the caveat of higher packet latency.

elements.sent-2411-956: This test was performed in counter mode.
elements.sent-2411-957: A higher parameter value corresponds to a higher load.

elements.figure-2411-015: \includegraphics[width=0.6\textwidth]{images/2_2_problem/diag7} \caption{Mixed interrupt moderation: Ratio of the reasons for triggering interrupts, shown in relation to counter threshold, timer delay and execution time when using the NIC with mixed mode moderation.} \label{prac2}
elements.sent-2411-962: In the first practical example, the causes of interrupts were investigated using the combined interrupt moderation mode of the smart NIC model, as shown in Figure~\ref{prac2 .}
elements.sent-2411-963: A Poisson distributed random load was used for this test.

elements.sent-2411-966: The area extends in both the counter threshold and timer delay directions, but drifts in the direction of the counter threshold axis, indicating that as the counter threshold increases, it plays less of a role in causing interrupts than does the timer delay.
elements.sent-2411-967: In the second practical example, we take a look at recorded loads.
elements.sent-2411-968: We compare the execution time of user code using a mixed-mode NIC with a Spotify network load to a Zoom conference load that has been pre-recorded.

elements.figure-2411-016: \includegraphics[width=0.6\textwidth]{images/2_2_problem/diag9} \caption{Execution time results: Run time of the test code in two replayed network scenarios from prerecorded PCAPs is shown for diverse delay timer thresholds.} \label{pcapDiag}
elements.sent-2411-972: Figure~\ref{pcapDiag is a combination of two plots: the lines show the execution time for different delay timer thresholds, while the filled area is a histogram showing the intervals at which packets arrive in the two load scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-973: We can see that the execution time in the Spotify scenario benefits more from relaxing the timeout delay, while the behavior in the Zoom scenario more closely resembles that of a Poisson load.
elements.sent-2411-974: Comparing the results of the two scenarios, it becomes clearer that the expected load behavior can be used to find appropriate interrupt mitigation parameters.
elements.sent-2411-975: In this chapter, we presented an analysis of the impact of IP networking (or more specifically, packet reception) on computationally weak embedded real-time devices.
elements.sent-2411-976: We have also presented a testbed for experiments in this context.
elements.sent-2411-977: The following points can be deduced: \begin{compactitem \item Without explicit care, networking has a huge impact on the real-time performance of embedded systems, making real-time guarantees invalid.}
elements.sent-2411-978: However, the development effort increases significantly when WiFi and IP networking functionality is manually added to a bare-metal design.
elements.sent-2411-979: Yet, cyber-physical systems that could pose a threat to their environment would justify this.
elements.sent-2411-980: At the same time, much of the problem lies in the general interdependency between real-time processes and the networking subsystem.
elements.sent-2411-981: Any real-time task that is, for example, waiting for IP-delivered control commands cannot be effectively isolated from the network, as isolation would render the function useless.
elements.sent-2411-982: In the next three chapters, we present several algorithms and system designs to mitigate these problems.
elements.sent-2411-983: However, simultaneous guarantees for both real-time deadlines and continuous operation are not possible when deploying devices in (wireless) IP networks.
elements.sent-2411-984: It has been shown that an unmodified embedded \ac{ip subsystem can have disastrous effects on the timing characteristics of real-time tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-985: Under high traffic, almost arbitrary delays can be induced, up to the point where the system is busy processing \ac{ip packets.}
elements.sent-2411-986: Therefore, connecting embedded systems to \ac{ip networks puts real-time properties at risk.}
elements.sent-2411-987: However, embedded devices are increasingly connected to \ac{ip networks for remote control, measurement data reporting, software maintenance, and diagnostics~\cite{jazdi2014cyber .}}
elements.sent-2411-988: In order to secure critical device functions, typical \acp{rtos provide the option of critical code sections where all interrupts are disabled.}
elements.sent-2411-989: Depending on the remaining functions of the device, these sections can vary in size.
elements.sent-2411-990: The problem with turning off interrupt handling is that any function that depends on external input is disabled for the time being.
elements.sent-2411-991: Unfortunately, this is the default for embedded control systems, i.e., for remote control over IP, neither the network subsystem nor the relevant interrupt sources can be turned off, even though the associated physical motion is time-critical.
elements.sent-2411-992: The same issue exists for other traditional network security methods such as firewalls.
elements.sent-2411-993: As long as critical aspects of its functionality depend on receiving network packets, the \ac{rtos must both provide connectivity and predictably process packets and forward them to the receiving critical real-time task.}
elements.sent-2411-994: A firewall cannot do this in a prioritizing manner.
elements.sent-2411-995: As for mitigating the contention problem from the software side (the operating system), we cannot influence or moderate the hardware interrupts except by rejecting them altogether.
elements.sent-2411-996: Hardware solutions range from dedicating another processor core to network tasks~\cite{hermes to sophisticated \acs{nic offloading~\cite{nics, behnke2022priority and \enquote{smart \acs{nic s equipped with their own fully featured processing system~\cite{smartnics, humphries2019mind .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-997: In addition, advanced interrupt controllers also address priority space unification~\cite{task_aware .}
elements.sent-2411-998: Best-effort IP networks with wireless links that implement specialized real-time network technologies (such as \ac{tsn as proposed in~\cite{8412458 ) are still an active research topic~\cite{bruckner_introduction_2019 (cf.. Chapter~\ref{cha:1_3_survey ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-999: How interrupt moderation on the hardware side (the \ac{nic ) can help us with this is discussed in Chapter~\ref{cha:2_4_hardware .}}
elements.sent-2411-1000: In this chapter, we examine and discuss how we can use the tools provided by an \ac{rtos to both identify congestion situations and mitigate their effects.}
elements.sent-2411-1001: Mitigating the effects of network-generated interrupts from software in a real-time operating system is challenging because the timing impact of the mitigation techniques themselves must be kept to a minimum.
elements.sent-2411-1002: To address this issue, this chapter presents the following contributions that have been peer-reviewed for three publications~\cite{danicki2021detecting, blumschein2022differentiating, behnke2023towards .}
elements.sent-2411-1003: Section~\ref{sec:2_3_mitigation presents the metric-based detection and mitigation algorithms, followed by their evaluation and discussion in Section~\ref{sec:2_3_mitigation_evaluation .}}
elements.sent-2411-1004: Section~\ref{sec:2_3_differentiating presents our priority-differentiating network driver extension followed by its evaluation in Section~\ref{sec:2_3_ipstack_eval .}}
elements.sent-2411-1006: For the purposes of the remainder of this thesis we consider devices to be embedded systems running an \ac{rtos and a small number of processes with fixed priorities managed by a preemptive scheduler.}
elements.sent-2411-1007: The embedded device is a constrained node in an IP network serving real-time applications as well as best-effort networking.
elements.sent-2411-1008: In the network stack, a driver controls \ac{dma transfers, establishes cache coherency and passes packet buffers to a networking task by means of a queue.}
elements.sent-2411-1009: The device contains an embedded \ac{nic used to connect to an IP network which itself might be connected to the Internet.}
elements.sent-2411-1010: The proposed approaches make use of IP flow information for real-time aware packet prioritization.
elements.sent-2411-1011: Packets are received by real-time processes on the embedded system.
elements.sent-2411-1012: An IP flow $f \in \mathcal{F}$ is a sequence of IP packets arriving at the regarded device and for the purposes of this thesis characterized by the tuple $(Src, Dst_{port , P, t_P )$.}
elements.sent-2411-1013: The period of a flow depends on the sender, application, and network infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-1014: Each task listens on a separate socket for messages.
elements.sent-2411-1015: Each message is delivered using one IP packet.
elements.sent-2411-1016: The tasks process the message and produce an output or control a physical actuator.

elements.sent-2411-1037: To summarize the key takeaway: To successfully save \ac{lp -flow packet processing time, we need to start very early in the driver and develop a strategy to defer some per-packet driver handling, such as cache invalidation in our case.}
elements.sent-2411-1038: This is discussed in more detail in Section~\ref{sec:2_3_differentiating after our metric-based detection and mitigation algorithms are introduced.}
elements.sent-2411-1039: This section presents detection techniques and mitigation algorithms to handle network-generated interrupt floods in real-time systems.
elements.sent-2411-1040: The metrics used to achieve this are defined before the algorithms are discussed and evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-1041: To prevent a critical task from being drowned by network interrupts, we first need to detect such a situation.
elements.sent-2411-1042: There are several different explicit and implicit metrics we can use to do that.
elements.sent-2411-1043: The most explicit metric is the real-time task's early- or lateness.
elements.sent-2411-1044: In many real-time systems, a process periodically performs a critical computation, targeting to finish it within a fixed duration.
elements.sent-2411-1045: When the critical task completes this computation in time (i.e. in less time than the target duration), we have positive earliness, defined as the target duration minus the actual time spent.
elements.sent-2411-1046: When modeling real-time tasks, this is considered the task laxity.
elements.sent-2411-1047: When, however, the critical task exceeds its target duration, it incurs lateness, defined as how much longer it took than targeted.
elements.sent-2411-1048: We will express lateness as a ratio of the critical task's target duration, e.. g.
elements.sent-2411-1049: This metric directly corresponds to what we are trying to detect but also has drawbacks: Firstly, its measurement introduces latency as processes can only report earliness/lateness once per task cycle.
elements.sent-2411-1050: Thus, mitigation techniques might need to be overly cautious because otherwise, it will react too late.
elements.sent-2411-1051: Additionally, there is the more practical concern that the metric may be hard to come by in real systems since somehow, the metric must be reported.
elements.sent-2411-1052: We thus call mitigation techniques relying on this cooperative.
elements.sent-2411-1053: A less direct metric is the number of incoming interrupts, discretized by dividing time into fixed time slices and identify the number of network interrupts in them.
elements.sent-2411-1054: Since many network interrupts occur per time slice, the metric's resolution is equally high.
elements.sent-2411-1055: Timing precision is not crucial because misattributing the first few packets to the passed time slice does not introduce significant errors.
elements.sent-2411-1056: The drawback of this metric is that it only correlates with the situations we are trying to prevent if certain preconditions are true: The network interrupt count shows approximately how much workload the interrupts are putting on a CPU.
elements.sent-2411-1057: We can thus estimate overall system resource usage if the resource requirements of the critical task stay approximately the same for each of its cycles.
elements.sent-2411-1058: The first precondition, therefore, is that the critical task has to require the same amount of resources at all times --- mitigation techniques relying on this can not react elastically to load change.
elements.sent-2411-1059: Furthermore, this metric assumes that the processing of each packet takes about the same time.
elements.sent-2411-1060: Mitigation techniques using this metric can only be effective if the ensemble of incoming packets is homogeneous enough such that the assumption that each packet takes approximately the same time to process is either true or practical because of the regular distribution of packet response time.
elements.sent-2411-1061: A third possible metric is detecting the queue fill state of received packets to be processed by the network driver.
elements.sent-2411-1062: The queue fill state shows whether the network driver can keep pace with the incoming packets.
elements.sent-2411-1063: If more packets arrive than the network driver can handle, the queue will fill up until it reaches maximum capacity; it would empty in the opposite case.
elements.sent-2411-1064: Of course, the queue's capacity impacts the quality of this metric.
elements.sent-2411-1065: With a queue capacity that is too low, the metric will show saturation even for short bursts of packets that are not representative of the overall traffic pace.
elements.sent-2411-1066: Low queue sizes will also lead to saturation if the scheduler did not wake up the network driver for some time.
elements.sent-2411-1067: If, conversely, the queue is too large it acts as a cushion and will delay alerting by filling up, allowing the network routines to stay activated for longer than is prudent.
elements.sent-2411-1068: The metric scales well in terms of packet response time deviation and elastic critical loads because it directly mirrors the ability of the network driver to process incoming traffic.
elements.sent-2411-1069: It has to be coupled with a mechanism like scheduler priority that moderates network driver execution such that an appropriate resource ceiling is found for the tasks.
elements.sent-2411-1070: In the following, we present four different mitigation techniques.
elements.sent-2411-1071: The goal of these methods is to protect one or more time-critical tasks from interrupt overloads.
elements.sent-2411-1072: With the Burst Mitigation, we suggest a technique that prevents unresponsiveness in case of short packet bursts.
elements.sent-2411-1073: The Hysteresis Mitigation controls the networking tasks based on the early- or lateness of the critical task.
elements.sent-2411-1074: This idea taken further, the novel Budget Mitigation attempts to balance out the networking part and the critical task by assigning a networking budget to the driver that is derived from the critical task's earliness.
elements.sent-2411-1075: Finally, the novel Queue Mitigation takes a step back and explores a different way to detect high load, enabling a simple yet effective mitigation technique.
elements.sent-2411-1076: Burst Mitigation is a simple approach to deal with very high packet loads in short periods.
elements.sent-2411-1077: Conceptually, time is split into fixed time slices, each having a fixed maximum packet capacity.
elements.sent-2411-1078: The network ISR tracks these time slices by querying the operating system for the tick count each time it is invoked, starting a new slice if enough time has passed since the start of the last one.
elements.sent-2411-1079: In each slice, the number of packets is counted.
elements.sent-2411-1080: If this packet count surpasses the fixed capacity, network interrupts are disabled until the start of the next time slice.
elements.sent-2411-1081: We visualize this in Figure~\ref{fig:burst : The length of each time slice is $20ms$ and the curve plots the number of received packets in each slice.}
elements.sent-2411-1082: The red zone on top is off-limits --- when $600$ packets are reached, interrupts are disabled.
elements.sent-2411-1083: When the network load exceeds the trigger capacity for the Burst Mitigation by a small amount, network interrupt activation migh oscillate.
elements.sent-2411-1084: We used the number of packets per time frame to detect whether we had to act to maintain the local real-time guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-1085: This, however, is just an imperfect proxy for the metric we are actually interested in: The time-critical task's real-time performance, i.. e.
elements.sent-2411-1086: Therefore, we based this mitigation on it.
elements.figure-2411-022: \includegraphics[width=.8\columnwidth]{images/2_3_software/hysteresis.pdf} \caption{Hysteresis Mitigation: Late- / earliness on a scale with the block and unblock thresholds.} \label{fig:hysteresis-explain}
elements.sent-2411-1087: Hysteresis is a popular technique for controlling processes~\cite{krasnoselskiiStaticHysteron1989 .}
elements.sent-2411-1088: It defines two thresholds: A maximum allowable threshold for a metric above which whatever action contributing to the rise of that metric will be ceased and a minimum threshold below which the action will be re-started.
elements.sent-2411-1089: We use the lateness/earliness metric reported by the critical task, i.e. how much time is left in the time slice when the task has been accomplished as the hysteresis control metric.
elements.sent-2411-1090: Once the earliness falls below the minimum allowable value, we stop processing new packets in the network driver, deactivate interrupts from there and wait (in a loop that sleeps for some time in every iteration) for the critical task to report an earliness higher than the threshold.
elements.sent-2411-1091: This mitigation technique ties the earliness metric more closely to the amount of work that is permissible within the network subsystems.
elements.sent-2411-1092: For example, there could be a local critical task load that uses up to $90\%$ of computation time per cycle.
elements.sent-2411-1093: Even a minuscule network load could then lead to the Hysteresis Mitigation permanently disabling the network.
elements.sent-2411-1094: Furthermore, we wanted to use the late-/earliness metric to react more responsively to elastic loads.
elements.figure-2411-023: \includegraphics[width=.9\columnwidth]{images/2_3_software/budget.pdf} \caption{Budget Mitigation: Timelines of tasks on CPU. The network driver can preempt the critical task until its budget is depleted.} \label{fig:budget-explain}
elements.sent-2411-1095: Budget Mitigation is another cooperative approach.
elements.sent-2411-1096: The critical task reports its earliness to the network driver after each completed cycle of computation.
elements.sent-2411-1097: This earliness is interpreted as the time budget of the network driver.
elements.sent-2411-1098: The driver will measure the time of its operations and subtract that from the latest budget.
elements.sent-2411-1099: Once the budget is depleted, the network subsystem (including the interrupts) is suspended until a new earliness notification is issued.
elements.sent-2411-1100: For this mitigation to work, we set both the critical and the network driver task to equal priorities such that the network driver has a chance to deplete its budget after which it will actively yield to the critical task.
elements.sent-2411-1101: This is an important tweak since the Budget Mitigation acts as a specialized scheduler by deciding when the network subsystem has to cease to operate.
elements.sent-2411-1102: It would otherwise be defeated by the operating system scheduler's time slice logic.
elements.sent-2411-1103: We looked at three mitigation techniques so far ---~a very simple approach that requires a manual definition of a capacity limit and two cooperative ones that require communication with the critical task.
elements.sent-2411-1104: With Queue Mitigation, we propose a more universal, yet effective non-cooperative mitigation.
elements.figure-2411-024: \includegraphics[width=.9\columnwidth]{images/2_3_software/queue.pdf} \caption{Queue Mitigation: After the packet queue fills up, all incoming packets are dropped until it is empty again.} \label{fig:queue-explain}
elements.sent-2411-1105: This mitigation technique is based on a simple observation concerning the network queue we made in Section~\ref{sec:queue-fill-metric : The network driver can keep up with the traffic when the packet queue is not full.}
elements.sent-2411-1106: Thus, with queue mitigation, we simply disable network interrupts if they fail to put a packet into the queue (because it was already full) and only re-enable the interrupts from the driver once it has processed all queued packets.
elements.sent-2411-1107: When using this mitigation technique, we assign a higher priority to the critical task.
elements.sent-2411-1108: As a result, the network driver is the first process running out of time when CPU resources get scarce or the interrupt frequency rises.
elements.sent-2411-1109: The direct effect is that the queue fills up.
elements.sent-2411-1110: With a reasonable queue size, the networking is then disabled before the critical task is too stressed, thus protecting the critical task from interrupt overload.
elements.sent-2411-1111: To investigate the effectiveness of the presented metrics and techniques, we test on the ESP32 microcontroller.
elements.sent-2411-1112: The controller is a common ARM-based development board with two CPU cores and the lightweight FreeRTOS operating system.
elements.sent-2411-1113: FreeRTOS provides basic task scheduler and interrupt management as well as some data structures for our application.
elements.sent-2411-1114: The multi-core platform allows us to separate the traffic generator from the traffic consumer by placing each on one of the two cores.
elements.sent-2411-1115: A local task simulating time-critical computation is additionally placed on the consuming core such that both compete for CPU time.
elements.sent-2411-1116: In our test setup, the interrupt handler fills up a FreeRTOS queue while the network driver empties it, thus processing the incoming data.
elements.sent-2411-1117: This allows us to analyze the effect of both, the interrupts and the driver on the critical computation load.
elements.figure-2411-025: \includegraphics[scale=0.9]{images/2_3_software/system.pdf} \caption{Test setup: Processes used to simulate different interrupt load scenarios on the ESP32 SoC.} \label{fig:overview}
elements.sent-2411-1118: The traffic generator running on the second core generates network loads with a pyramid-shaped load pattern.
elements.sent-2411-1119: It sets a GPIO pin to high for each received packet, triggering a GPIO interrupt on the other core over a GPIO bridge.
elements.sent-2411-1120: There, the network simulator synthesizes a TCP SYN packet and places it into a queue for consumption by the network driver which acknowledges the packet's arrival.
elements.sent-2411-1121: Meanwhile, the observed critical task (also running on the first core) calculates an ascending series of binomial coefficients to generate an equal work load for each task cycle.
elements.sent-2411-1122: Its goal is to reach a target $(n, k)$ in a small time frame of $10ms$.
elements.sent-2411-1123: If the target is reached before the permissible time expired, the critical task will sleep the remaining time and start over in the next period.
elements.sent-2411-1124: If, however, the critical task fails to reach its target in time, it will accumulate lateness, i.e. continue until it reached the target values and then start its new period immediately.
elements.sent-2411-1125: Figure~\ref{fig:overview provides a graphical overview of this setup.}
elements.sent-2411-1126: The amount of \begin{compactitem \item interrupts triggered, \item \acp{isr executed, \item packets processed in the network driver, \item critical task cycles, and \item accumulated critical task latenesses \end{compactitem are saved into atomic variables of a monitoring routine running on core 1.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1127: Every second, the controller reports these variables via the serial interface and then clears the counters.
