elements.section-2411-022: Differentiating Network Flows for Priority-Aware Packet Processing
elements.sent-2411-1185: As shown in the previous section and in~\cite{niedermaier_you_2018 , a first approach to prevent congestion caused by received packets is to introduce budget enforcement on the entire \ac{rx path.}}
elements.sent-2411-1186: However, when limiting the reception of incoming packets, this affects every communication of the device, regardless of its importance.
elements.sent-2411-1187: Yet, devices might be running multiple processes, each with different criticality levels for both their timely termination and their communication.
elements.sent-2411-1188: One might include soft real-time traffic that, while not critical to the integrity of the controlled cyber-physical system, is relevant for basic functionality or monitoring.
elements.sent-2411-1189: Note that we expect multiple IP flows with different levels of timeliness requirements and packet frequencies, allowing for a different view of the problem.
elements.sent-2411-1190: We observe a problem of incorrect priority enforcement in the receive path of the network subsystem.
elements.sent-2411-1191: In general, an incoming packet is used by (at most) one specific task.
elements.sent-2411-1192: However, as long as its purpose is unknown, we need to schedule the processing of each packet equally.
elements.sent-2411-1193: For the practical implementation of the network subsystem in an RTOS, this means assigning a fixed priority to the protocol processing server task, which inevitably creates a priority inversion situation: \begin{compactitem \item Using a high priority, as is common in current embedded frameworks, can starve high-priority tasks in favor of low-priority packets.}
elements.sent-2411-1194: This is observed as network latency.
elements.sent-2411-1195: On the one hand, we do not want to spend computing resources before we know if it is worth it given the current scheduling situation.
elements.sent-2411-1196: On the other hand, incorporating the use of a particular packet requires prior protocol handling.
elements.sent-2411-1197: The goal of this section is to present a network driver extension that distinguishes between different IP flows and their priorities in order to always prioritize the communication necessary for critical tasks and to apply the actual process priorities of real-time tasks to the processing of their packets.
elements.sent-2411-1198: Thus, we aim to solve the problem of priority inversion in packet reception.
elements.sent-2411-1199: In addition, the proposed architecture aims to protect against network congestion and improve overall network performance.
elements.sent-2411-1200: Past work has already proposed to do packet classification in the networking driver as early as possible~\cite{druschel1996lazy , then apply a priority for each \acs{udp -packet by receiving process and defer the subsequent packet processing~\cite{lee_interrupt_2010, lee_priority-based_2015 based on its assigned priority.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1201: Others have analyzed and compared various network task architectures for enabling protocol processing prioritization with nicely integrating scheduling-properties that avoid priority inversion situations~\cite{mercer1991evaluation .}
elements.sent-2411-1202: In this section, we propose a packet reception architecture for typical embedded \acp{rtos and \acs{ip stacks that facilitates the deployment of IoT hardware in real-time scenarios without the need for specialized hardware.}}
elements.sent-2411-1203: More formally, the architecture aims to combine the following properties: \begin{compactitem \item Protection against network-induced system overloads, facilitating real-time systems.}
elements.sent-2411-1204: Namely, we evaluate \begin{compactenum \item the usefulness of a software-only approach in terms of CPU efficiency, \item the suitability for constrained scenarios, regarding possible overhead in CPU time and memory consumption, and \item improvements regarding real-time schedulability.. \end{compactenum.}}
elements.sent-2411-1205: A modification of the packet reception subsystem for real-time IoT devices should satisfy the following requirements.
elements.sent-2411-1206: Packets of high-priority flows must be processed and delivered before those of low-priority flows.. \item[1.2] Overload Protection.
elements.sent-2411-1207: Packet floods must not lead to system overloads.
elements.sent-2411-1208: In case of a packet flood, low-priority flows must be throttled in favor of high-priority processes.
elements.sent-2411-1209: A general rate limitation must protect the real-time properties of running tasks.. \item[1.3] Performance Retention.
elements.sent-2411-1210: The approach should not introduce a longer packet processing delay.
elements.sent-2411-1211: However, predictability and overload protection must be prioritized.. \end{compactenum.}
elements.sent-2411-1212: The proposed architecture is designed around a data structure of differentiated flow queues, which replaces the simple frame queue.
elements.sent-2411-1213: Each flow defines a priority and a period, to affect the further processing of its packets.
elements.sent-2411-1214: For the prioritization of processing, we add a priority manipulation mechanism to the protocol processing task.
elements.sent-2411-1215: In order to gain a maximal advantage from scheduling the subsequent processing stage, the driver is modified to do only the necessary work of classifying incoming packets to flows by their header entries.
elements.sent-2411-1216: The remaining activity is then executed on packet retrieval by the scheduled protocol processing task as presented in Figure~\ref{fig:approach_overview .}
elements.figure-2411-029: \includegraphics[width=.9\columnwidth]{images/2_3_software/Approach} \caption{Architecture overview: Packets are classified early and enqueued by their flow, with individual periodic capacity restrictions applied. Further processing is scheduled by inherited priority.} \label{fig:approach_overview}
elements.sent-2411-1217: The proposed architecture combines three concepts: \begin{compactenum \item Soft Early Demultiplexing into receiver-centric flows is necessary for differentiating flows on an End-to-End basis, without reliance on network \ac{qos and as a result satisfy Requirement 1.1.}}
elements.sent-2411-1218: In the following subsections we introduce the concept of each of the three basic building blocks of our architecture and discuss relevant implementation aspects.
elements.sent-2411-1219: In order to minimize the effort spent until after classification, we employ Early Demultiplexing~\cite{druschel1996lazy .}
elements.sent-2411-1220: By peeking into key header entries, a packet is assigned to its eventual receiver process.
elements.figure-2411-030: \includegraphics[scale=.8]{images/2_3_software/Driver_Buffer_Flow} \caption[Receive activity in the original driver]{Receive activity in the original driver: Once an interrupt occurs, the driver code moves a packet buffer from the \ac{bd} ring to a simple queue, and fills the vacant position with a newly allocated one. Due to cache coherency requirements of the memory system, the buffer caches have to be invalidated for both the retrieved and the replacement buffer.} \label{fig:driver_buffer_flow}
elements.sent-2411-1221: The benefit of demultiplexing performed in software depends heavily on the amount of work that can be saved by mere demultiplexing compared to full protocol processing.
elements.sent-2411-1222: Since the packet scheduling in our architecture can only influence the processing that follows after Early Demultiplexing, the achievable degree of partial network liveness in overload scenarios depends on its quick execution.
elements.sent-2411-1223: Starting from the existing driver receive path, depicted in Figure~\ref{fig:driver_buffer_flow , we introduce two changes: Packet classification and lazy cache invalidation.}
elements.sent-2411-1224: The classification differentiates incoming packets into flows defined by the protocols ARP, ICMP, TCP and UDP.
elements.sent-2411-1225: While the former two form a single flow, the latter are further differentiated by local port numbers in order to implement the receiver task association.
elements.sent-2411-1226: Depending on the used network stack, the lookup from the port to a flow may either be performed using the existent network stack's list of bound socket control blocks, or else requires an additional data structure managed by the driver.
elements.sent-2411-1227: In our prototype based on FreeRTOS+TCP, the socket managing code in the original network stack can easily be locked in a critical section, leaving the \ac{isr safe to access it.}
elements.sent-2411-1228: If a scenario requires anticipating a large number of bound sockets, a sophisticated data structure with better complexity should be employed.
elements.sent-2411-1229: However, with only a few sockets bound at any particular point in time, a linear linked list lookup as found in typical embedded network stacks suffices.
elements.sent-2411-1230: Instead of enqueueing every received packet to the same \ac{rx frame queue, each packet is inserted into a specific queue according to the result of the classification.}
elements.sent-2411-1231: Because the packets do not necessarily get processed in bounded time, the network subsystem might experience buffer starvation.

elements.sent-2411-1348: When comparing the approach to a simple mitigating stack as a baseline, it becomes clear that it cannot help with processing higher rates of important packets.
elements.sent-2411-1349: Instead, supported by fast Early Demultiplexing and individual prioritization against the remaining tasks, it allows to postpone an overall limitation.
elements.sent-2411-1350: This way, a system can sustain a much higher load of less important packets before real-time disturbing effects start to occur.
elements.sent-2411-1351: This chapter presents several software-based approaches to detect and mitigate the real-time violating effects of incoming IP packets.
elements.sent-2411-1352: The first half analyzes the network interrupt count and queue fill state, as well as the lateness of a critical task running on an embedded SoC while it is flooded with network traffic.
elements.sent-2411-1353: Four mitigation strategies were developed to maintain smooth operation and timeliness of a critical process using signals derived from the data obtained during the analysis: Burst Mitigation, Hysteresis Mitigation, Budget Mitigation, and Queue Mitigation.
elements.sent-2411-1354: The quality of the different mitigation strategies was measured through the lateness of a critical task that runs concurrently to a network simulation triggering a high amount of interrupts.
elements.sent-2411-1355: The evaluation indicates that Queue Mitigation is the most effective method as it processes the highest number of network packets while also safeguarding critical tasks from delays.
elements.sent-2411-1356: To further incorporate real-time task priority information on mixed-criticality devices, we presented an \ac{ip stack design to individually schedule packet processing for differently prioritized \ac{ip flows after early demultiplexing.}}
elements.sent-2411-1357: The problem of expensive processing in the network driver is addressed by incorporating the option of deferred buffer processing into our architecture.
elements.sent-2411-1358: Established embedded IP stacks, such as FreeRTOS+TCP and lwIP, can be adjusted to fit the proposed design with minimal modifications.
elements.sent-2411-1359: On our test system, the CPU load caused by low-priority packets in an already busy system is reduced by 86\% (a 7x speedup), even when dealing with packet buffers that travel through CPU caches.
elements.sent-2411-1360: Our approach enables system designers to anticipate packet rates of certain soft real-time flows, including those not belonging to any single receiver task, and derive an estimation for the respective \ac{wcet through limitation parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-1361: Compared to simpler overall mitigation algorithms, our design provides better isolation of processing time allocations for each flow, ensuring that important flows remain connected even when other flows exceed their rate limitation.
elements.sent-2411-1362: By allocating the same CPU resources to the processing of incoming packets, the networking subsystem can handle packets of a high-priority flow with up to 600\% higher overall traffic loads.

elements.section-2411-026: A Multiqueue NIC for Real-Time-aware Packet Reception

elements.sent-2411-1402: To accommodate incoming packets belonging to different real-time processes, the receive buffer holding packet descriptors of the NIC is divided into multiple queues realized as ring buffers.
elements.sent-2411-1403: This way, packet descriptors are assigned to different queues depending on their destination process and its priority, accounting for Requirement 2.2.
elements.sent-2411-1404: The metadata of validated packets is compared to a list of registered ports residing in a distribution map on the NIC.
elements.sent-2411-1405: Here, packets are assigned to queues which hold packets of one IP flow each.
elements.sent-2411-1406: According to the process priority and expected packet load, different interrupt moderation configurations (e.g. delay timers and counter threshold) are applied to them through the operating system.
elements.sent-2411-1407: This way, packets for time-critical processes trigger interrupts immediately upon reception while less important packets (packets with low priority receiving tasks) are coalesced before one interrupt is triggered for all packets in the respective queue, indicated by the millisecond specifications in Figure~\ref{fig:hwdesign .}
elements.sent-2411-1408: Packets with no associated process can be dropped before an interrupt is triggered since these packets would be dropped by the operating system at a later point in any case, but after generating unnecessary ISR and network stack work.
elements.sent-2411-1409: This is especially important under high unanticipated traffic loads targeting the device and potentially leading to a denial of service.
elements.sent-2411-1410: Finding appropriate queue configurations for different process priorities is part of the design process of the embedded real-time system as introduced delays need to be part of scenario modeling.
elements.sent-2411-1411: Coalescing packets in the NIC reduces the number of interrupts triggered, ISRs executed and context switches performed.
elements.sent-2411-1412: However, the amount of data to be processed by the network stack remains unchanged for packets registered for one of the running processes.
elements.sent-2411-1413: Depending on the number of packets coalesced, interrupt moderation might lead to an accumulation of network stack workload into bursts.
elements.sent-2411-1414: The necessary runtime to process an incoming packet is a lot smaller than the delay introduced by coalescing packets.
elements.sent-2411-1415: A meaningful delay through these bursts can hence only happen under very high packet rates.
elements.sent-2411-1416: We consider this when choosing the queue parameters as follows.
elements.sent-2411-1417: The multiqueue NIC introduces four parameters affecting packet delays and resource utilization as posed in Requirement 2.3: \begin{compactitem \item Number of queues $m$.}
elements.sent-2411-1418: The number of queues the receive buffer is divided into depends on the number of currently active processes accepting packets and supported protocols.
elements.sent-2411-1419: The number of elements of a queue $q$ corresponds to its expected packet load, available memory, and moderation parameters.
elements.sent-2411-1420: Periodic duration until an interrupt is triggered by the queue $q$.
elements.sent-2411-1421: Amount of time after a packet is received by the queue $q$ that triggers an interrupt if not reset by another incoming packet.
elements.sent-2411-1422: The maximum expected packet rate of the flow corresponding to a queue $q$.
elements.sent-2411-1424: Depending on the packet rate, a variable number of packets is then coalesced to be announced by one interrupt.
elements.sent-2411-1425: As these parameters have a high impact on the timeliness of incoming traffic and generated workload on the real-time device, the accuracy of their configuration is of high importance.
elements.sent-2411-1426: While the number of queues is directly dependent on the current number of active (i.e. socket binding) processes, timer values and queue sizes have to be cautiously chosen.
elements.sent-2411-1427: To be able to sensibly choose the parameters knowledge about expected packet rates and slack times of real-time processes is necessary.
elements.sent-2411-1428: The added packet processing time needs to be accounted for when developing an IP-connected real-time system.
elements.sent-2411-1429: Process deadlines must allow for enough slack time for the system to handle concurrent packet reception.
elements.sent-2411-1430: The higher the slack times are, the more packets can be processed without resulting in deadline misses.
elements.sent-2411-1431: The worst-case scenario is subject to high interrupt rates affecting the process with the smallest slack time.
elements.sent-2411-1432: For the calculation of appropriate queue parameters this value has to be factored in.
elements.sent-2411-1433: The parameters must be chosen respecting the following considerations.
elements.sent-2411-1434: Choosing an appropriate queue size affects memory consumption as well as the maximum number of packets that can be coalesced in one interrupt.
elements.sent-2411-1435: Applying interrupt moderation generally means holding more unprocessed packets in memory ultimately increasing the demand for the whole system.
elements.sent-2411-1436: Memory implications for queue structures behave analogous but in a much smaller scale as only descriptors are held.
elements.sent-2411-1437: The more packets can be held by one queue, the bigger the packet burst to be processed by the IP stack may become.
elements.sent-2411-1438: Hence, this parameter also enforces an upper limit for the incoming packet rate per queue as elements are dropped when new packets arrive at a full queue.
elements.sent-2411-1439: Additionally, this value has to be kept small enough for the IP stack processing time to be smaller than the minimum slack time when all queues generate a burst at the same time.
elements.sent-2411-1440: The resulting delay corresponds to the Worst-Case Packet Processing Delay (WCPD) which depends on the per packet processing time $t_{netstack}$. \begin{equation}WCPD = t_{netstack} \sum_{q=0}^{m-1} n_q\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1441: The absolute queue timer realizes the upper latency bound of the interrupt rate window.
elements.sent-2411-1442: To minimize the number of interrupts, this parameter needs to be maximized.
elements.sent-2411-1443: At the same time, a higher absolute timer value also leads to a higher added latency an incoming packet might experience.
elements.sent-2411-1444: Hence, the absolute timer value is limited by the maximum additional delay the underlying process can handle while still meeting its deadline.
elements.sent-2411-1445: As a high value increases the burst of packets to be processed under high loads, the chance of the queue filling up increases, leading to packet loss.
elements.sent-2411-1446: The maximum expected packet rate $R_{max}$ per queue $q$ needs to be factored in.
elements.sent-2411-1447: The packet timer realizes the lower latency bound of the interrupt rate window.
elements.sent-2411-1448: Increasing this value generally decreases the number of interrupts as there is more time available for a new packet to arrive and reset the timer.
elements.sent-2411-1449: This also means, that this value directly influences the minimum additional latency an incoming packet experiences.
elements.sent-2411-1450: At the same time the amount of interrupts is highly dependent on the incoming traffic shape.
elements.sent-2411-1451: The worst case in terms of interrupts generated is a packet rate corresponding to the packet timer value (one interrupt per packet).
elements.sent-2411-1452: Hence, the available slack time needs to suffice to handle the number of interrupts generated by all packet timers combined when every timer iteration of each queue triggers an interrupt.
elements.sent-2411-1453: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:hwdesign , two interfaces are used for the NIC configurations.}
elements.sent-2411-1454: One for setting the queuing parameters mentioned earlier and a second to write process-to-IP flow mappings to the distribution map.
elements.sent-2411-1455: Both configurations are performed when a socket is bound using the network stack API (see Section~\ref{sec:rx_path ).}
elements.sent-2411-1456: For this purpose, the socket API is extended with driver calls that make the specific changes.
elements.sent-2411-1457: Whenever a new process registers or releases a socket, the operating system transparently adjusts the number of queues and their parameters.
elements.sent-2411-1458: The delay times and the size of the queues must be set for specific scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-1459: The required information is passed to the driver as socket binding parameters.
elements.sent-2411-1460: As stated in Requirement 2.4, the system must be dynamically tunable at runtime to facilitate changes in processes or IP flows.
elements.sent-2411-1461: Since the configuration process is linked to the socket API, the required tuning parameters can be passed at any time by the registering process.
elements.sent-2411-1462: In the same way, NIC queues are released when a socket is no longer bound.
elements.sent-2411-1463: Since a network packet does not contain explicit information about the receiving process, a mapping must be made between the packet metadata and the processes.
elements.sent-2411-1464: For this purpose, a mapping between IP flows and processes is created and placed on the NIC.
elements.sent-2411-1465: In this design, the destination port is used to map a packet to a process.
elements.sent-2411-1466: However, it could also be extended to match specific tags.
elements.sent-2411-1467: This is be useful when all components of the distributed system are under the control of the developer to add security measures.
elements.sent-2411-1468: The map must be on the NIC itself to cause minimal additional distribution delay, and still be configurable by the operating system to reflect the current set of existing processes.
elements.sent-2411-1469: The presented approach has implications for memory usage on two levels: Firstly, the network buffer on the host system needs to be able to hold packet contents until they are processed, even when multiple packets are coalesced.
elements.sent-2411-1470: Depending on the timer values and packet rate this might be a multiple of the usually necessary space.
elements.sent-2411-1471: The network buffer resides on system RAM and is accessed by \ac{dma .}
elements.sent-2411-1472: The second level is the required memory on the \ac{nic .}
elements.sent-2411-1473: In an example implementation with 32~bit addresses, 32~bit timers, and a conservatively chosen maximum packet queue length of $65,536 KB$ the on-NIC memory necessary for one table entry is 30 Bytes as broken down in Table~\ref{tab:memory .}
elements.figure-2411-038: \caption{Required register memory per queue on the NIC for an example implementation.} \begin{tabular}{l|c} Component Size port\_id 16 bit base\_address 32 bit buffer\_size 16 bit offset 16 bit next\_base\_address 32 bit packet\_timer 32 bit absolute\_timer 32 bit packet\_timer\_expiration 32 bit queue\_timer\_expiration 32 bit \end{tabular} \label{tab:memory}
elements.sent-2411-1474: The base\_address field contains the RAM address of the beginning the the queue packet memory.
elements.sent-2411-1475: The offset is incremented for each incoming packet by its size.
elements.sent-2411-1476: The next\_base\_address is switched for the base\_address when an interrupt for the queue occurs to be able to receive new packets while the buffered ones are processed.
elements.sent-2411-1477: When reserving one queue for non-transport layer protocols such as ARP (not requiring interrupt moderation fields and hence being 14 Bytes broad), the total memory requirement is \begin{equation}m \cdot 30B + 14B\end{equation} for $m$ table entries on the NIC.
elements.sent-2411-1478: The proposed NIC extension reduces the number and frequency of interrupts caused by incoming packets.
elements.sent-2411-1479: Yet, as packets that belong to registered processes are not dropped, driver and network stack workloads remain in a time shifted manner.
elements.sent-2411-1480: We evaluate the resulting timing implications by conducting three sets of experiments: The first explores the ability to reduce interrupts.
elements.sent-2411-1481: The second compares the robustness of the real-time system against high traffic loads.
elements.sent-2411-1482: The third analyzes the effects of different queue configurations under expected loads.
elements.sent-2411-1483: As the design proposes changes to hardware and an evaluation on a real embedded device is necessary for plausibility, the evaluation platform comprises of two layers.
elements.sent-2411-1484: One layer assuring plausibility by providing a device running real-time processes (process layer) and one allowing hardware and configuration changes to the NIC (NIC layer).
elements.sent-2411-1485: The evaluation setup is depicted in Figure~\ref{fig:setup .}
elements.sent-2411-1486: To evaluate the real-time behavior of a running system, we used an ESP32\footnote{\url{https://www.espressif.com/en/products/socs/esp32 microcontroller for the process layer.}}
elements.sent-2411-1487: It is equipped with a dual-core CPU and widely used for IoT tasks that involve communication via WiFi and Bluetooth.
elements.sent-2411-1488: The two cores of the device permit a separation between observed processes and testing system.
elements.sent-2411-1489: The observed processes run on the real-time operating system FreeRTOS where scheduling is performed preemptively on basis of process priorities.
elements.sent-2411-1490: To generate realistic network loads, traffic traces from common industrial control systems are used.

elements.sent-2411-1497: The interrupt traces generated by the simulator are applied to the processing layer by an interrupt generator implemented on the ESP32.

elements.sent-2411-1499: The NIC interrupt service routine (NetRx ISR in Figure~\ref{fig:setup ) receives this batch of packet descriptors and appends them to an operating system queue to be fetched by the network driver.}
elements.sent-2411-1500: From here, each packet is processed by the network stack task.
elements.sent-2411-1501: If the packet destination port has a socket registered to it, the packet descriptor is forwarded to the socket mailbox and the associated task is notified.
elements.sent-2411-1503: The receiving real-time worker processes get access to sockets through the socket API.
elements.sent-2411-1504: Using a receive function, the processes can then read data from the socket mailbox.
elements.sent-2411-1505: This approach implementation of Berkeley sockets corresponds to the mapping of IP flows to processes.
elements.sent-2411-1506: The receiving processes are workers of different priority.
elements.sent-2411-1507: Each incoming packet is tracked from its time of arrival at the NIC until processing in its worker process where it triggers the task workload.
elements.sent-2411-1508: In all experiments, the IoT device runs four worker processes of different priority.
elements.sent-2411-1509: The processes receive traffic using the widely used industrial communication protocol MODBUS/TCP\footnote{MODBUS/TCP traces provided by~\cite{frazao2018denial .. .}}

elements.sent-2411-1530: The absolute moderation timer is an effective tool to moderate the high load as more packets are coalesced into interrupts while the critical task is unaffected.

elements.sent-2411-1531: Next, we observe the interrupt-induced runtime increase of the critical process.

elements.sent-2411-1537: By increasing the delay parameters, more packets are coalesced for each interrupt, meaning that the networking tasks are confronted with larger bursts of packets per notification.
elements.sent-2411-1538: This has negative effects on CPU load starting at a critical packet count.
elements.sent-2411-1539: For the maximum depicted packet load of 5000 packets per second the additional runtime could be decreased by 80\,\% (or 12 percentage points) resulting from the prevention of 93\,\% of interrupts.
elements.sent-2411-1540: Since the NIC retains packet notifications for low priority tasks, it causes an additional interrupt delay depending on the delay timer configurations.
elements.sent-2411-1541: To investigate this delay, the second set of experiments was performed on a stable system with no unexpected traffic floods.

elements.sent-2411-1544: In this experiment, the four processes receive IP flows ranging from about 50 (for queue 0) to 200 (for queue 3) packets per second.
