elements.sent-2411-1548: The results, depicted in Figure~\ref{fig:nodos , show that the critical process (queue 0) does not suffer from any additional delay.}
elements.sent-2411-1549: The delay added to processes of less priority is directly dependent on the chosen moderation parameters and process-specific load and can be chosen to fit the requirements of each process before or during runtime.
elements.sent-2411-1550: At the same time, the delay window prevents interrupt frequencies to climb to a critical level.
elements.sent-2411-1551: An increase in the OS-induced delay can be observed when too many packets are coalesced to one interrupt as the resulting bursts in packet processing increase the processing time for individual packets in the networking and worker processes.
elements.sent-2411-1552: While the queue moderation can effectively reduce the impact of high packet loads, the tuning of moderation parameters requires care.
elements.sent-2411-1553: Task deadlines, as well as packet loads and latencies for each process should be identified to reduce the impact of interrupt moderation under normal conditions while at the same time ensuring operability for critical tasks under unexpectedly high loads.
elements.sent-2411-1554: Unexpected floods of network traffic can delay process flows in real-time systems, putting critical real-time requirements at risk.
elements.sent-2411-1555: Specialized networking hardware for real-time embedded devices can mitigate the risk.
elements.sent-2411-1556: This chapter presented a multiqueue \ac{nic design shifting the early demultiplexing discussed in Chapter~\ref{cha:2_3_software from the lower part of the driver to hardware.}}
elements.sent-2411-1557: This way, the number of network-triggered IRQs can be moderated depending on packet priorities and load.
elements.sent-2411-1558: By configuring the extended NIC via an adapted Berkley socket API, currently active processes waiting for packets can register their priorities with the hardware, effectively mapping IP flows to processes when a socket is bound and changing queue parameters accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-1559: We evaluated the hardware design using a \ac{nic simulation and an IoT device running a real-time operating system.}
elements.sent-2411-1560: The results of these experiments show that our approach significantly reduces the impact of traffic floods on critical process runtimes by saving 93\,\% of interrupts and 80\,\% of processing delay under packet rates of 5000 per second while the configuration of multiqueue parameters requires knowledge about expected network traffic and real-time requirements.
elements.chapter-2411-009: A Hardware/Software Co-Design for Real-Time Aware Packet Processing
elements.sent-2411-1561: In an effort to mitigate the problems introduced into the real-time system by IP networking, they each developed distinct advantageous approaches to different parts of the issue.
elements.sent-2411-1562: However, as the problem involves the \ac{nic as receive hardware in conjunction with the network driver and \ac{rtos task prioritization, a unified design combining the advantages of both approaches is warranted.}}
elements.sent-2411-1563: Such a design is introducted and implemented in this chapter.
elements.sent-2411-1564: Section~\ref{sec:2_5_considerations presents the added considerations for the combined design.}
elements.sent-2411-1567: Section~\ref{sec:2_5_evaluation presents an evaluation of the implementation and Section~\ref{sec:2_5_conclusion concludes this chapter.}}
elements.sent-2411-1568: A unified design can combine the advantages of the two presented designs while also mitigate some of the individual disadvantages.
elements.sent-2411-1569: These shortcomings can be summarized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-1570: Once packets are received by the OS queue, no reordering can happen and packet processing can still lead to an inversion of priorities.
elements.sent-2411-1571: This design is subject to the same assumptions and requirements as the individual approaches.
elements.sent-2411-1572: The preliminary considerations of the two designs remain relevant here and can be compiled as follows to act as a guideline for the design stage.
elements.sent-2411-1573: Packets need to be sorted into recipient-associated flows early in hardware.
elements.sent-2411-1574: Every flow should adapt the receiver task priority to extend the task priority space into the receive path.
elements.sent-2411-1575: This early separation into flows is required to avoid resource contention in packet reception.
elements.sent-2411-1576: Moreover, packet filtering at reception allows for fast rejection of irrelevant packages, thereby reducing the load on protocol processing in software.
elements.sent-2411-1577: Whenever packets of multiple flows await processing, all packets of a lower-priority flow must wait for any packets of higher-priority flows to be processed and delivered.
elements.sent-2411-1578: This avoids priority inversion, as high-priority recipient tasks will not be blocked by lower-priority recipients serviced earlier.
elements.sent-2411-1579: To this end, the network task processing queued packets in the IP stack needs to always dequeue the high-priority packets available and change its task priority according to the flow.
elements.sent-2411-1580: Network-induced packet floods should never cause system overloads.
elements.sent-2411-1581: Hence, the effects of unpredictable packet delivery events on the real-time system need to be mitigated.
elements.sent-2411-1582: This entails decoupling the strict consecution of packet reception and \ac{dma transfer from the subsequent processing of the packet.. \item[3.4] Extensive Configurability.}
elements.sent-2411-1583: In order to configure the modified network solution to integrate it effectively into the real-time application, the moderation parameters for all packet flows are required to be configurable from software.
elements.sent-2411-1584: They need to be adjustable by the user in a similar way the real-time parameters for the \ac{rtos are.. \end{compactenum.}}
elements.sent-2411-1585: The combined design unifies the priority spaces of real-time tasks, packet processing, and network-generated interrupts.
elements.sent-2411-1586: An abstract representation of the design can be seen in Figure~\ref{fig:arch .}
elements.figure-2411-044: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{2_5_codesign/architecture.pdf} \caption{Co-Design: Schematic of the unified design displays how hardware and software components are co-integrated to form a holistic solution for the receive path. Three flows (orange, green and blue) associated with recipient processes (nos. 1 -- 3) are displayed, assuming their priorities for packet reception and processing. Each flow is configured with a different timer and queue size configuration.} \label{fig:arch}
elements.sent-2411-1587: The design extends from hardware to software, demultiplexing the packets received from the \ac{mac into application-centric flows early in hardware and processing them in their flows until they are delivered to the destination application.}
elements.sent-2411-1588: Depending on developer-defined tasks and their priorities, incoming packets can be dropped and coalesced to fewer \acp{irq before any workload emerges in the \ac{rtos .}}
elements.sent-2411-1589: Every packet received by the \ac{nic is classified based on its metadata and filtered according to the configuration stored inside the filter table.}
elements.sent-2411-1590: Matched packets will be transferred into the corresponding packet queue buffers in \ac{ram via \ac{dma , with every queue buffer holding the packets for a specific flow (designated front buffer).}}
elements.sent-2411-1591: A queue buffer will accumulate packets until expiration of the queue is indicated by associated timers managed in the \ac{nic .}
elements.sent-2411-1592: At this point, the front buffer is replaced by a new queue buffer (back buffer) as target for \ac{dma transactions and the software is informed of a packet queue ready to be processed.}
elements.sent-2411-1593: The software side of the design implements the network driver and is split into two segments.
elements.sent-2411-1594: In its design, the same structural approach was used as is proposed in Chapter~\ref{cha:2_3_software , since partitioning the driver into an eager and a deferred part allows the workload in the \ac{isr to be kept small by offloading the IP stack processing into a separate network task.}}
elements.sent-2411-1595: Similarly, using multiple differentiated flow queues was adopted to implement packet processing by flow priority inside the deferred driver task.
elements.sent-2411-1596: When a packet queue is ready to be processed, an IRQ is raised.
elements.sent-2411-1597: The subsequently executed \ac{isr inside the minimal network driver extracts all packets stored in the buffer and appends them to the associated flow queue.}
elements.sent-2411-1598: Once packets are available, the network task in the deferred driver is responsible for IP processing, ultimately submitting them to the sockets of the receiver processes.
elements.sent-2411-1599: Flow configuration and back buffer resupply for the \ac{nic are performed through the socket \ac{api and network driver, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-1600: When a socket is bound, a flow inheriting the priority of the responsible process is established, with the driver supplying initial configuration to the \ac{nic .}
elements.sent-2411-1601: Configuration of interrupt moderation parameters such as packet queue buffer size or interrupt timers may be performed via socket options in the user processes.
elements.sent-2411-1602: This allows the user to adjust these parameters to the requirements of their real-time software, as per Design Requirement 3.4.
elements.sent-2411-1603: To accommodate the combined design the individual implementations have to be adapted.
elements.sent-2411-1604: This section discusses the challenges of a new design and presents necessary changes.
elements.paragraph-2411-051: Demultiplexing Packets using the Filter Table
elements.sent-2411-1605: Every entry in the filter table is associated with a packet flow, mapping the packet metadata employed in classification to the flow distribution parameters.
elements.sent-2411-1606: This metadata will typically include the protocol and transport layer destination port number and is used for matching incoming packets to a flow.
elements.sent-2411-1607: The distribution parameters constitute a memory address identifying the next available slot inside the front packet queue buffer.
elements.sent-2411-1608: In every filter table entry two packet queue buffers are referenced (designated front and back).
elements.sent-2411-1609: Using a back buffer facilitates fast buffer swapping on timer expiration, since a replacement for the expired front buffer is available immediately.
elements.sent-2411-1610: Thus, the technique prevents packets from being dropped for the duration of the queue swap.
elements.sent-2411-1611: Although the proposed solution focuses on transport layer packets, lower-layer packets like ARP, ICMP or IGMP need to be addressed as well.
elements.sent-2411-1612: Since these are not associated with any sockets, a default flow is proposed to accommodate them.
elements.sent-2411-1613: It should be handled like any other flows inside the hardware, with the associated task priority being that of the network driver process.
elements.sent-2411-1614: Demultiplexing packets early in hardware offers performance advantages: since non-matching packets would otherwise be discarded later on in the IP stack anyway, dropping them in hardware reduces the packet processing overhead in software.
elements.sent-2411-1615: Furthermore, it mitigates the danger of packet flooding with unanticipated packets, therefore meeting Design Requirement 3.1.
elements.sent-2411-1616: As previously established, the generation of individual receive interrupts on a per-packet basis is detrimental to the real-time capability of a system.
elements.sent-2411-1617: The solution proposed in Chapter~\ref{cha:2_4_hardware stipulates generating interrupts using timers.}
elements.sent-2411-1618: Since this approach has been proven effective at interrupt moderation, the idea will be adopted in this design.
elements.sent-2411-1619: Using timers for IRQ generation effectively coalesces all interrupts generated for received packets into a single interrupt triggered when the timer is due.
elements.sent-2411-1620: This approach couples the receive interrupt execution only loosely to packet reception, with any danger of network induced overloads mitigated, thus realizing Design Requirement 3.3.
elements.sent-2411-1621: As in the original \ac*{nic desgin, two timers are proposed for each flow.}
elements.sent-2411-1622: The packet timer and the queue timer.
elements.sent-2411-1623: Both timers dictate the minimum and maximum possible duration of the time frame in which interrupts may be raised.
elements.sent-2411-1624: The packet timer is started when the first packet is filtered into a flow and reset on every subsequently filtered packet.
elements.sent-2411-1625: Thus, it dictates the lower bound of the time frame, which is the minimum time a packet will remain queued.
elements.sent-2411-1626: The queue timer on the other hand dictates the maximum duration packets may stay queued before being passed to software.
elements.sent-2411-1627: Through configuration of these timers, the additional delay incurred for the received packets may be adjusted.
elements.sent-2411-1628: Furthermore, they may also be tuned to the packet reception rates expected by the corresponding processes running on the \ac{rtos .}
elements.sent-2411-1629: For example, a high-priority task awaiting steady rates of real-time data over the network may configure both timers to a very short duration or even zero to force immediate packet reception.
elements.sent-2411-1630: On the other hand, a low-priority process awaiting reception of periodic telemetry information may configure the timer much more relaxed.
elements.sent-2411-1631: The driver only has to be changed minimaly compared to the design from Chapter~\ref{cha:2_3_software .}
elements.sent-2411-1632: The following two paragraphs descibe the elements of the driver necessary to fulfil the requirements stated above.
elements.sent-2411-1633: The minimal network driver is responsible for extracting all packets from the available packet queue buffers and moving them to the flow queues.
elements.sent-2411-1634: Processing steps like cache invalidation or validation of offloaded checksums are also performed in the \ac{isr .}
elements.sent-2411-1635: However, cache invalidation is not required for the complete buffer at this point, since only the metadata such as packet size (and optionally the checksum) need to be extracted from the packet queues buffers.
elements.sent-2411-1636: As explained in Section~\ref{sec:2_3_approach , it is sufficient in this stage to only invalidate the parts of the cache where the data resides.}
elements.sent-2411-1637: The invalidation of the remaining cache lines is delayed for the deferred driver to handle, therefore reducing the workload to be performed in the \ac{isr (i.e. lazy cache invalidation).}
elements.sent-2411-1638: The beneficial effects of lazy cache invalidation on the run time of the \ac{isr are estimated to be even greater in this design.}
elements.sent-2411-1639: Since the packet queue buffers usually contain multiple packets, cache invalidation for the complete buffer range will demand much more run time than merely invalidating small ranges for each of the packets in the buffer.
elements.sent-2411-1640: The deferred driver will fetch the packets received from the flow queues, processing them in the IP stack.
elements.sent-2411-1641: Packets are always read from the populated queue of highest priority, adhering to the strict flow prioritization to accommodate Design Requirement 3.2.
elements.sent-2411-1642: As concomitant measure, the network process changes its own task priority to the priority of the flow queue being processed, hence mitigating the possibility of priority inversion as has been described in Section~\ref{sec:prioritedprotocolhandling .}
elements.sent-2411-1643: This section describes implementation details for the previously introduced design.
elements.sent-2411-1644: The referenced implementation is a working prototype developed for this thesis.
elements.sent-2411-1645: The prototype was implemented on a Xilinx Zynq \ac{soc , which features a \ac{fpga integrated with an ARM Cortex-A9 processor as \ac{ps .}}}
elements.sent-2411-1646: An out-of-the-box Ethernet \ac{nic design on the \ac{fpga was used as the basis for implementing the modifications to the network interface controller.}}
elements.sent-2411-1647: The standard design features an Ethernet subsystem and an \ac{axi \ac{dma \ac{ip-core which connects the Ethernet Subsystem to a peripheral high-performance \ac{axi bus interface of the processing system.}}}}
elements.figure-2411-045: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{2_5_codesign/module_hierarchy.pdf} \caption{Module hierarchy: Hardware implementation of the RX Path depicting the hierarchy and interconnection of all modules. Modules shown in blue are parameterized macros or instantiated IP-Cores distributed by Xilinx.} \label{fig:hw-arch}
elements.sent-2411-1648: The module hierarchy of the hardware design for the custom \ac{nic is displayed in Figure~\ref{fig:hw-arch .}}
elements.sent-2411-1649: All modifications to the existing hardware are encapsulated in an additional \ac{ip-core designated the \ac{mq-dsppe .}}
elements.sent-2411-1650: This \ac{ip-core is connected directly to the output of the Ethernet subsystem, receiving status information on the the \ac{rxs and the actual frame payload on the \ac{rxd .}}}
elements.sent-2411-1651: The buses are connected directly to the \ac{axi Stream Packet Buffer module, described in more detail in the following subsection.}
elements.sent-2411-1652: Since the proposed design requires unique \ac{dma functionality, a custom-built \ac{dma controller is included in the \ac{ip-core .}}}
elements.sent-2411-1654: Therefore, the receive functionality of the \ac{axi \ac{dma \ac{ip-core is not required in the custom \ac{nic implementation and the custom \ac{dma controller module is instead connected to the peripheral bus interface of the \ac{ps .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1655: All modules directly involved in processing and forwarding the received Ethernet frames are encapsulated in the Downstream module.
elements.sent-2411-1656: The \ac{axi Stream Packet Buffer module receives the frame data as well as additional status information directly from the Ethernet Subsystem.}
elements.sent-2411-1657: It scans the frames and extracts all relevant information required for filtering the packets.
elements.sent-2411-1658: Once it has latched all relevant data from the Ethernet frame stream, it sends a lookup query to the Filter Table module, receiving information on whether the packet may be forwarded to the \ac{dma controller or dropped.}
elements.sent-2411-1659: Additionally, parts of the data looked up in the filter table are passed to the \ac{dma controller.}
elements.sent-2411-1660: This module transfers the Ethernet frame received to the selected frame queue buffer resident in the \ac{ps \ac{ram .}}
elements.sent-2411-1661: The frames are stored in series inside the buffer, with one word of metadata such as frame length and raw checksum placed before each frame.
elements.sent-2411-1662: During operations, the Downstream module also increments statistics timers, collecting information on the number of packets forwarded or dropped and the number of \ac{dma errors encountered in transmission.}
elements.subsubsection-2411-024: The AXI Stream Packet Buffer Module
elements.sent-2411-1663: This module is responsible for scanning and classifying the frames received from the Ethernet subsystem.
elements.sent-2411-1664: The parameters extracted from the received streams are the frame length as well as the port number in transport layer packets.
elements.sent-2411-1665: Scanning the Ethernet frames while they are being transmitted word for word on the \ac{axi stream bus is realized using a \ac{fsm and stream word counters.}}
elements.sent-2411-1666: The counters are incremented for every word received on the bus, enabling the \ac{fsm to identify relevant fields by their byte offsets inside the nested packet headers.}
elements.sent-2411-1667: Since all packet headers need to be scanned, a portion of the frame needs to be received before deciding whether to forward or drop the frame.
elements.sent-2411-1668: Thus, it cannot be forwarded to the \ac{dma controller as of yet and needs to be buffered inside this module.}
elements.sent-2411-1669: An \ac{axi Stream FIFO\footnote{\url{https://docs.xilinx.com/r/2022.1-English/ug953-vivado-7series-libraries/XPM_FIFO_AXIS is utilized for this purpose, allowing the frameto be forwarded or dropped when sequentially shifting out the frame on the bus interface connected to the \ac{dma controller.}}}}
elements.figure-2411-046: \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{2_5_codesign/fsm_packet_buffer.pdf} \caption{Frame classification: Simplified state diagram of the FSM controlling the scanning process of the frame.} \label{fig:eth_fsm}
elements.sent-2411-1670: A state diagram for the \ac{fsm is provided in Figure~\ref{fig:eth_fsm .}}
elements.sent-2411-1671: The IDLE state is the default state, where the module awaits receiving frame data.
elements.sent-2411-1672: Status information about a received frame is the first thing the Ethernet subsystem transfers, causing the \ac{fsm to switch to state STS on the status stream bus, providing information such as the total frame length and the EtherType from the frame header.}
elements.sent-2411-1673: Based on the value stored, the \ac{fsm switches into states IPv4 or IPv6, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-1674: In case of any other EtherType, the packet is not relevant and the \ac{fsm switches into state OTHER, ignoring the remainder of the frame transmission.}
elements.sent-2411-1675: By the time the \ac{fsm has switched into the network layer state, the actual frame data is being transmitted on the \ac{rxd .}}
elements.sent-2411-1676: Dependent on the network layer protocol, the IP header fields Protocol (Ipv4) or Next Header (IPv6) are inspected to determine the transport layer protocol (\ac{tcp or \ac{udp ), again switching into state OTHER when any other values than the expected ones are encountered.}}
elements.sent-2411-1677: Once the headers are transmitted, the port numbers are latched and the module is able to post its query to the filter table, providing the port number and frame length.
elements.sent-2411-1678: In case the \ac{fsm is in state OTHER, a query is still sent but containing the value zero instead of a port number.}
elements.sent-2411-1679: The \ac{fsm then either returns to state IDLE in case no further frame transmission was initiated or it switches into state STS in case the status stream information on the next Ethernet frame is already pending.}
elements.sent-2411-1680: This module stores the information determining where frames with a certain id are transferred to in memory via \ac{dma .}
elements.sent-2411-1681: Every entry stored inside the table has the following fields: \begin{compactitem \item id stores the port number (16 bit) \item buffer length holds the total length of the packet queue buffer (configurable, default 16 bit) \item front buffer address stores the memory address of the front buffer (configurable, default 32 bit) \item back buffer address stores the memory address of the back buffer (configurable, default 32 bit) \item offset pointer holds the number of bytes already stored inside the table (configurable, default 16 bit) \end{compactitem The offset pointer points indicates the next free byte in the buffer and is always incremented by the length of the frame transferred.}}
elements.sent-2411-1682: Thus, the actual address to which a frame will be transferred into memory is calculated as the sum of the front buffer address and the offset pointer.
elements.sent-2411-1683: If the buffer length minus the sum is less than the frame length, the frame will dropped since there is not sufficient memory left in the buffer to store the complete frame.
elements.sent-2411-1684: This module implements the \ac{dma functionality required for transferring the received packets to memory.}
elements.sent-2411-1685: For the actual data movement, the module uses an \ac{axi DataMover\footnote{\url{https://docs.xilinx.com/r/en-US/pg022_axi_datamover .}}}
elements.sent-2411-1686: This module is capable of transferring data received on an \ac{axi Stream bus into \ac{ps Memory.}}
elements.sent-2411-1687: Since the \ac{dma engine itself buffers transfer requests, care was taken to track all pending \ac{dma operations to prevent the hardware from releasing a filled front packet queue buffer to software while \ac{dma transfers into the buffer are still in progress.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1688: The software solution was implemented into the Xilinx port of LwIP 2.1.1.
elements.sent-2411-1689: Primarily modifying the vendor-specific lower-level part of the network stack as well as the socket module.
elements.sent-2411-1690: The minimal driver is implemented in an \ac{isr , which is registered with the processor's interrupt controller to be executed when the Ethernet \ac*{mq-dsppe \ac{ip-core raises an \ac{irq .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1691: The deferred driver functionality was implemented into the vendor-specific network interface code of the network stack, superimposed over a task responsible for moving packets from the frame queue to the LwIP stack.
elements.sent-2411-1692: Furthermore, the single frame queue was replace with a data structure, holding the frame queues for all flows as well as additional information on the associated task priorities.
elements.sent-2411-1693: Much care was taken to avoid concurrency issues such as race conditions or deadlocks between tasks.
elements.sent-2411-1694: Mutexes were used for locking access to the central data structure holding the queue information.
elements.sent-2411-1695: This was necessary, as this data structure is accessed from the deferred driver task, the \ac{isr and any user task via the Berkeley socket \ac{api .}}
elements.sent-2411-1696: To avoid race conditions with the \ac{isr , interrupts are deactivated in critical sections where a task accesses driver-related memory which the \ac{isr shares access to.}}
elements.sent-2411-1697: Inside LwIP, the socket \ac{api was modified to allow interrupt moderation configuration to be performed via the socktop() function, which is part of the Berkeley Socket \ac{api .}}
elements.sent-2411-1698: Moreover, core locking was enabled for LwIP.
elements.sent-2411-1699: This configuration option ensures all accesses to the IP stack are thread safe by using a mutex to lock the central data structure.
elements.sent-2411-1700: The positive corollary of using core locking is the effective elimination of a user-space worker task (designated tcpip), which ordinarily is employed in handling packet input.
elements.sent-2411-1701: Since the proposed design featured an adaptive-priority task in the deferred driver, which already handles packet input to the IP stack, eliminating the packet reception capability of the tcpip thread was necessary.
elements.sent-2411-1702: In this section the implementation of the custom hardware-software solution is evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-1703: For this purpose, the design was implemented on an \ac{fpga evaluation board and tested in a scenario using real network traffic.}
elements.sent-2411-1704: The empirical evidence provided in this section will substantiate the design choices made and prove the effectiveness of the developed approach.
elements.sent-2411-1705: The experiment was performed on an Alinx AX7Z035 evaluation board featuring the Xilinx XC7Z7035 Zynq \ac{soc , which merges a dual-core ARM Cortex-A9 with a Kintex-7 \ac{fpga .}}
elements.sent-2411-1706: The evaluation board features a Micrel KSZ9013 Gigabit Ethernet PHY connected to the \ac{fpga fabric on which the custom hardware design is implemented.}
elements.sent-2411-1707: To simulate a low-power embedded device as typically employed in IoT environments, the ARM Cortex was configured to operate in single-core mode with a clock rate of 300 MHz.
elements.sent-2411-1708: To collect precise information on the amount of processing time utilized by each task, FreeRTOS was compiled with run time statistics enabled.
elements.sent-2411-1709: Additionally, the statistics collection tick rate in FreeRTOS was increased 100-fold to enhance the accuracy of the measurements.
elements.sent-2411-1710: In the experiment, the impact of high rates of low-priority packets on a high-priority network-dependent task were evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-1711: For this scenario, two worker tasks were defined, a low-priority task (LP) and a high-priority task (HP).
elements.sent-2411-1712: Each task held an open socket, anticipating \ac{udp network traffic.}
elements.sent-2411-1713: To simulate the packet processing delay for the worker tasks, the task would engage in busy-waiting for 5 ms after every packet received on the socket.
elements.sent-2411-1714: The experiment was performed for 3 setups, once for the custom implementation and twice for the baseline implementation.
elements.sent-2411-1715: Two configurations were tested on the baseline implementation, one with a default frame queue size of 32 packets and another with an infinite frame queue size.
elements.sent-2411-1716: This was done to examine the effect a limited frame queue size has on packet reception.
elements.sent-2411-1717: Measurements were taken for a time period of 70 seconds per run, with packets sent for 60 of those seconds.
elements.sent-2411-1718: During the experiment, a constant rates of zero-length \ac{udp packets was sent to the high-priority and low-priority tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-1719: Throughout the 7 runs performed, the rate of packets sent to the high-priority task was held constant at 100 packets per second.
elements.sent-2411-1720: The rate packets sent to the low-priority task was doubled on every run.
elements.sent-2411-1721: Thus, the packet rates sent to the task were 0, 100, 200, 400, 800, 1600 and 3200 packets per second (pps).
elements.sent-2411-1722: The processing time every task was scheduled as running was measured as well as the number packets received be the high-priory task.
elements.sent-2411-1723: The results of the measurements are shown in Figures~\ref{fig:diag1 and~\ref{fig:diag2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-1724: They clearly establish the custom implementation in the \ac{nic to be the only system where the high-priority task is not affected by the increasingly higher rates of low-priority packets sent.}
elements.sent-2411-1725: In comparison, the high priority task in both baseline setups is allotted increasingly less running time by the scheduler.
elements.sent-2411-1726: This is likely due to priority inversion blocking the high-priority task, while the low-priority task is able to keep running due to large amount of low-priority packets received.
elements.sent-2411-1727: Furthermore, it may be ascertained that a limited queue size degrades the performance of the high-priority task even more.
elements.sent-2411-1728: Thus, the empirical findings establish the effectiveness of the design proposed in this thesis and support the implementation choices.
elements.figure-2411-048: \begin{tikzpicture} \begin{axis}[ xlabel=Number of LP packets sent, ylabel=Number of HP packets received, xmin=-100, xmax=3500, ymin=0, ymax=7990, legend columns=-1, legend style={ column sep=1pt, /tikz/every even column/.append style={column sep=4pt} }, ] \pgfplotsset{width=0.6\textwidth, height=0.45\textwidth} \addplot[smooth,mark=*,LinePetrol] plot coordinates { (0, 5772) (100, 5775) (200, 4019) (400, 1742) (800, 1151) (1600, 555) (3200, 505) }; \addlegendentry{baseline} \addplot[smooth,mark=*,LineGreen] plot coordinates { (0, 5769) (100, 5897) (200, 4558) (400, 2785) (800, 2009) (1600, 1103) (3200, 817) }; \addlegendentry{baseline-uq} \addplot[smooth,mark=*,LineRed] plot coordinates { (0, 5714) (100, 5802) (200, 5903) (400, 5794) (800, 5835) (1600, 5692) (3200, 5723) }; \addlegendentry{custom} \end{axis} \end{tikzpicture} \caption{Responsiveness: Number of packets received by the high-priority task in relation to the number low-priority packets sent to the system.} \label{fig:diag2}
elements.sent-2411-1729: This chapter presented a hardware/software co-design mitigating the negative effects of IP packet reception and ensuring the real-time performance of time-critical tasks running on an embedded device.
elements.sent-2411-1730: Devised as a unified hardware-software solution, the design combines aspects of the exclusively software-based approach presented in Chapter~\ref{cha:2_3_software with those of and the exclusively hardware-based approach presented in Chapter~\ref{cha:2_4_hardware .}}
elements.sent-2411-1731: To this end, the two established proposed approaches were reviewed and their shortcomings ascertained.
elements.sent-2411-1732: The combined design was developed building on individual ideas from the approaches and adapting them.
elements.sent-2411-1733: The design features a network interface driver which employs early packet demultiplexing into prioritized packet flows.
elements.sent-2411-1734: Packets are scanned and their metadata is compared with the configurations of all flows to realize sorting them into prioritized packet queues in memory.
elements.sent-2411-1735: Interrupt generation for processing the queue buffers is timer-based.
elements.sent-2411-1736: Two timers are used, defining the time frame for how long packet will stay queued before the interrupt service routine fetches the buffer and passes the packets along to the network driver.
elements.sent-2411-1737: The design has been implemented in a working prototype.
elements.sent-2411-1738: The implementation required the development of a custom hardware design on an \ac{fpga -\ac{soc and adaptation of an existing IP Stack.}}
elements.sent-2411-1739: The hardware design is based on a standard Ethernet configuration.
elements.sent-2411-1740: The novel functionality in the network interface controller was implemented inside a custom \ac{ip-core integrated into the existing base design.}
elements.sent-2411-1741: The implementation of the software involved the creation of a driver for interaction with the custom \ac{ip-core .}
elements.sent-2411-1742: Furthermore the IP stack was modified, with the vendor-specific network interface portion substantially changed and the socket \ac{api of LwIP adjusted.}
elements.sent-2411-1743: A test scenario was chosen to contrast the custom implementation with a standard Ethernet configuration as baseline in a realistic test scenario.
elements.sent-2411-1744: The results show that the combined design is able to protect time critical tasks from the real-time violating effects of high lower priority packet loads.
elements.sent-2411-1745: Moreover, the reception of high priority packets is not hindered by low priority packet floods, showing that the previously existing priority inversion (cf.
elements.chapter-2411-010: Offloading Real-Time Tasks in Distributed Systems
elements.sent-2411-1747: In the previous approaches, we aimed to introduce IP networking to embedded real-time systems in a safe(r) manner.
elements.sent-2411-1748: In the following, we put these devices and their connectivity to use, by sharing their real-time tasks with more powerful resources in the vicinity.
elements.sent-2411-1749: We present an architecture for scalable real-time task offloading as well as a distributed real-time scheduler for this purpose.
elements.sent-2411-1750: The integration of real-time tasks in distributed systems introduces challenges that impact system performance, reliability, and predictability~\cite{yen1998performance, zhou2020dependable, akesson2020empirical .}
elements.sent-2411-1751: Achieving precise synchronization across distributed nodes, accounting for network latency and communication overhead, ensuring fault tolerance and reliability, and optimizing resource allocation are among the key challenges associated with real-time task execution.
elements.sent-2411-1752: Furthermore, scalability issues may arise as the number of nodes increases.
elements.sent-2411-1753: Ongoing research efforts in these areas, along with the exploration of novel algorithms and architectures, hold the potential to advance the integration of real-time tasks in distributed systems, making them more robust, scalable, and capable of supporting mission-critical applications across diverse domains.
elements.sent-2411-1754: To make a contribution to these efforts, we identify the tradeoffs and implications of offloading real-time tasks to local edge resources connected by wireless networks.
elements.sent-2411-1755: For this purpose, a system architecture is designed and a simulator implemented to test the scenario with various parameters related to the properties of the network of actors and tasks.
elements.sent-2411-1756: The basic architecture includes mobile embedded devices acting as clients that offload real-time tasks, a cluster of worker nodes computing these tasks, and a scheduler distributing the offloaded tasks incorporating task deadlines and network latencies into its decision making.
elements.sent-2411-1757: Further, the architecture is extended with a distributed scheduler design eliminating the single point of failure a single scheduler node constitutes.
elements.sent-2411-1758: With this system, different scenarios will be run in order to test the possibilities and limits and finally to evaluate the circumstances under which the real-time conditions can still be guaranteed.
elements.sent-2411-1759: The focus of this work lies on the incorporation of network uncertainties.
elements.sent-2411-1760: The first section of this chapter introduces the motivation and the specific challenges concerning the offloading of real-time tasks in IP networks.
elements.sent-2411-1761: Section~\ref{sec:3_3_sysarch explains the system architecture with which we aim to meet these challenges.}
elements.sent-2411-1762: Section~\ref{sec:3_3_scheduler proposes a scheduler to perform latency-aware offloading.}
elements.sent-2411-1763: Section~\ref{sec:3_3_evaluation evaluates different system configurations to quantify trade-offs between performance and reliability.}
elements.sent-2411-1764: In Section~\ref{sec:3_3_tolerance we discuss fault-tolerance in such systems and introduce a distributed scheduler.}
elements.sent-2411-1766: Parts of this chapter have been peer-reviewed and published in~\cite{behnke2023offloading .}
elements.sent-2411-1767: The \ac{iiot aims to connect embedded devices in industrial environments to enable remote control, mobile computing, monitoring, and collaborative problem solving.}
elements.sent-2411-1768: Examples can be found in the cyber-physical systems used in smart factories, logistics, and autonomous vehicles~\cite{bock2021performance,barzegaran_fogification_2020 , where devices often operate under real-time constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-1769: While real-time computing in automation and embedded systems is well researched and deployed, its combination with packet-based wireless IP networking presents new challenges and opportunities.
elements.sent-2411-1770: As already motivated in Chapter~\ref{cha:1_1_introduction , embedded devices typically have resource and power constraints that limit the type and amount of local computation.}
elements.sent-2411-1771: For example, resource-intensive tasks such as object recognition in video may require specialized hardware and more power than an embedded device can reasonably provide.
elements.sent-2411-1772: Another example for this can be found in close-proximity model training, where sensor data is accumulated and processed on edge servers~\cite{becker2021local .}
elements.sent-2411-1773: A common way to deal with these limitations in practice is to offload individual tasks to more powerful computers in the local area~\cite{qiu2020edge,iiot_offloading_energy,hossain_edge_2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-1774: However, for tasks with real-time requirements, this can be very challenging due to unpredictable latencies in wireless networks, especially when considering mobile devices.
elements.sent-2411-1775: Some recent works on the issue of real-time task offloading have been identified in the survey in Chapter~\ref{cha:1_3_survey .}
elements.sent-2411-1776: Scheduling real-time tasks in distributed systems has received increasing attention~\cite{roy2020contention,yi2020task,behnke2020interrupting , but related work generally assumes very reliable and predictable communication.}
elements.sent-2411-1777: However, especially with wireless connections, the exact prediction of the expected latency is difficult.
elements.sent-2411-1778: Various efforts have been made to develop methods to guarantee end-to-end delay in wireless networks, but some form of uncertainty always remains~\cite{e2e_delay_1,e2e_delay_2,e2e_delay_3,behnke2023towards .}
elements.sent-2411-1779: In addition, efficiently deploying workloads in a distributed system is difficult, especially when these workloads occur sporadically~\cite{scheduling_difficult .}
elements.sent-2411-1780: The goal of this chapter is a system design for real-time task offloading for mobile embedded systems and the development and evaluation of a scheduler for this purpose.
elements.sent-2411-1781: Figure~\ref{fig:arch_overview shows an overview of the design.}
elements.sent-2411-1782: Potentially mobile machines (clients) submit tasks with deadlines to the scheduler, which checks time feasibility and -- if accepted -- schedules the tasks to worker queues.
elements.sent-2411-1783: The workers run on the same compute cluster as the scheduler and can be preempted by it.
elements.sent-2411-1784: Each worker computes one task at a time and returns the result directly to the source client while signaling task completion to the scheduler.
elements.figure-2411-049: \includegraphics[width=.7\columnwidth]{images/3_3_offloading/sysarch.pdf} \caption{System architecture: Overview of actors and communication channels.} \label{fig:arch_overview}
elements.sent-2411-1785: The considered environments contain a scalable number of (semi-) autonomous devices that reside and potentially move in a local area spanned by an IP network.
elements.sent-2411-1786: Based on these settings, the following task and scheduler properties can be derived.
elements.sent-2411-1787: Therefore the tasks are independent and no precedence constraints need to be considered.
elements.sent-2411-1788: However, with the major difference of distributed processing entities (workers) and task dispatch via local network.
elements.sent-2411-1789: Design decisions and the operation of the scheduler are explained in detail in Section~\ref{sec:3_3_scheduler .}
elements.sent-2411-1790: The task is the central data structure that holds all the necessary information about the workload that a client wants to offload.
elements.sent-2411-1791: It is modeled after real-time operating system processes and is shared and sent between the different entities in the system.
elements.sent-2411-1792: In an IIoT environment such as a smart factory, we assume that all entities and workloads are known before the system is set up.
elements.sent-2411-1793: Therefore, clients can assume that the programs or binaries necessary to compute all the workloads that a client wants to offload are present on the workers.
elements.sent-2411-1794: This makes a task submission much like a Remote Procedure Call (RPC).
elements.sent-2411-1795: A task is generated initially by the client, who sends it to the scheduler, which in turn eventually sends it to a worker.
elements.sent-2411-1796: It is serialized and deserialized for transportation over the network, but all entities work on the same shared Task data structure.
elements.sent-2411-1797: For the purpose of this work it is characterized by the tuple \begin{equation}(C, T_d, t_r, t_cs, t_w, t_e, P)\end{equation} \begin{compactitem \item $C$ the source client.}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}(C, T_d, t_r, t_cs, t_w, t_e, P)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1798: This value, along with the connection setup time $t_\text{cs}$ measured by the client, is used by the scheduler to calculate network latency.
elements.sent-2411-1799: The duration $t_e$ is set and used by the scheduler to keep track of the execution time when a task is computed by a worker and possibly preempted.
elements.sent-2411-1800: The set of parameters $\mathcal{P}$ contains the command line arguments and payload data that the worker passes to the actual executable.
elements.sent-2411-1801: There are two sensible design choices for when to dispatch consecutively scheduled tasks to the assigned workers: \begin{compactenum \item Dispatch a task to the worker as soon as it is assigned to it, and let the worker maintain an internal task queue.}
elements.sent-2411-1802: On the other hand, the scheduler sends lower priority tasks to the worker to add to its queue while it is working on the highest priority task.
elements.sent-2411-1803: The second option interrupts the worker only when a task with a higher priority than the one the worker is currently processing arrives.
elements.sent-2411-1804: For the remainder of this work, the second approach is used to ensure minimal disruption and maximum predictability of the execution time of the running task.
elements.sent-2411-1805: We assume that the connection between the workers and the scheduler is wired and very fast (compared to the wireless connection of the clients), so that task dispatching causes little idle time for the workers.
elements.sent-2411-1806: In a scenario where tasks come with large input payloads, such as multimedia, the first approach may be more appropriate.
elements.sent-2411-1807: Tied to the task dispatch approaches is the question of what path the task payloads take.
elements.sent-2411-1808: Task payloads refer to the input parameters that the client sends with the task and the task result that is sent back to the client.
elements.sent-2411-1809: There are three possible paths for the input parameters: \begin{compactenum \item The client passes only the task metadata to the scheduler, which then communicates the assigned worker back to the client, so it can send the input parameters directly to the worker.}
elements.sent-2411-1810: In a scenario where the task input parameters consist of large amounts of data, such as multimedia, the scheduler might become a bottleneck if it has to handle the distribution of this data.
elements.sent-2411-1811: In this case, one of the other approaches might be more appropriate.
elements.sent-2411-1812: The task result payload is only relevant to the client.
elements.sent-2411-1813: Therefore, the worker sends it directly to the client.
elements.sent-2411-1814: Clients are the entities that generate tasks they want to offload.
elements.sent-2411-1815: They are designed as a software library that provides an interface for submitting tasks.
elements.sent-2411-1816: Client machines are mobile embedded devices that perform some kind of physical action and are connected to the rest of the network via a wireless link.
elements.sent-2411-1817: They implement fallback behavior in case a task is rejected by the scheduler.
elements.sent-2411-1818: The clients are responsible to set the following properties of offloaded tasks as defined in Section~\ref{sec:3_3_tasks .}
elements.sent-2411-1819: Submitted tasks are either accepted or rejected by the scheduler.
elements.sent-2411-1820: In case a task is accepted, the client will assume that the task result is available to the client before the deadline.
elements.sent-2411-1821: If it arrives after the deadline, this means a failure of the system and is to be generally avoided.
elements.sent-2411-1822: So, if the scheduler is not able to schedule the task on one of the workers without nearing the deadline by a certain uncertainty window, it rather rejects the task completely.
elements.sent-2411-1823: The client has some fallback behavior for this case, which causes less disruption than a missed deadline.
elements.sent-2411-1824: Workers compute the offloaded tasks distributed by the scheduler.
elements.sent-2411-1825: They can be thought of as distributed processing cores: A worker can process exactly one task at a time and is fully utilized with that execution.
elements.sent-2411-1826: Worker machines are edge resources that are physically close to the scheduler host and on the same local area network.
elements.sent-2411-1827: However, it listens for incoming tasks even while processing one, since tasks need to be preemptable in a setting with deadlines.
elements.sent-2411-1828: They ensure predictable task execution times.
elements.sent-2411-1829: Workers connect to the scheduler at startup and listen for tasks.
elements.sent-2411-1830: Upon receiving a task, the worker begins executing the task with the parameters provided by the client, but continues to listen for incoming tasks to allow for preemption by the scheduler.
elements.sent-2411-1831: When the worker receives a task while still processing an earlier task, the earlier task gets preempted until the new task is completed.
elements.sent-2411-1832: After the worker successfully connects to the scheduler, it enters the main loop, where it waits for tasks to be sent by the scheduler through the open connection.
elements.sent-2411-1833: When a task is received, a new thread is started to handle that task while the main loop continues to listen for messages from the scheduler.
elements.sent-2411-1834: The task handler running in the new thread then starts to prepare the task computation.
elements.sent-2411-1835: The TCP 3-way handshake takes a non-negligible amount of time when performed on a connection with a latency in the double-digit millisecond range~\cite{pei2016wifi .}
elements.sent-2411-1836: For this reason, the worker starts establishing the connection to the client immediately after receiving a task, in parallel with the actual task computation.
elements.sent-2411-1837: The worker sends the computation results of a task directly to the client.
elements.sent-2411-1838: This ensures that the task result can be sent with the shortest possible delay once the task has finished computing.
elements.sent-2411-1839: The worker computes the tasks by starting the task executable as a separate operating system process.
elements.sent-2411-1840: Once the process is started and a process id (PID) is returned by the operating system, the running task and its process ID are pushed into the internal task queue.
elements.sent-2411-1841: The thread then sleeps until the process has exited.
elements.sent-2411-1842: If there is already a task running, the worker lets the operating system suspend the process execution and marks the process as preempted in the task queue.
elements.sent-2411-1843: Only then the new process to compute the new task is started and the thread goes to sleep until the new process has exited.
elements.sent-2411-1844: Once the process has exited, the worker reads the computation result and packages it for transport to the client.
elements.sent-2411-1845: The worker also records the actual execution time (excluding the time spent being preempted).
elements.sent-2411-1846: The worker notifies the scheduler of having finished the task and sends the task result to the client.
elements.sent-2411-1847: However, this needs an established connection to the client, which was started in parallel with the task computation.
elements.sent-2411-1848: If the connection setup takes longer than the task computation, the worker must wait until the open connection is available.
elements.sent-2411-1849: Finally, if some task was preempted, the halted process is resumed.
elements.sent-2411-1850: This section explains the design choices and operation of the scheduler, which dispatches tasks submitted by clients to workers.
elements.sent-2411-1851: After parsing a task message into the corresponding task structure, the time to deadline is calculated from the absolute deadline $T_d$ of the task and the current system time $T_s$.
elements.sent-2411-1852: For each incoming task, the scheduler then derives the laxity~$= T_d - T_s - (t_w - t_e)$ and density~$= (t_w - t_e) / (T_d - T_s)$ to make a scheduling decision.
elements.sent-2411-1853: Based on the task model and the assumed environment, only dynamic priority, preempting scheduling algorithms such as Earliest Deadline First (EDF) and Least Laxity First (LLF) are suitable.
elements.sent-2411-1854: LLF has the major drawback of thrashing~\cite{llt , which is exacerbated in this scenario.}
elements.sent-2411-1855: Since context switches are expensive, the high frequency of context switches caused by the thrashing effect is already problematic in a general-purpose operating system.
elements.sent-2411-1856: In our scenario, where each preemption not only causes a context switch on the worker, but also requires communication, the effect has an even greater impact on overall system performance.
elements.sent-2411-1857: For this reason, EDF was considered a more reasonable algorithm for our scheduler.
elements.sent-2411-1858: Yet, for the sake of completeness, there also exist enhanced versions of LLT which aim to avoid thrashing.
elements.sent-2411-1859: In a multiprocessor environment, EDF works either with a global task queue (global EDF) or with partitioned queues for each worker (partitioned EDF).
elements.sent-2411-1860: In addition to the non-optimality of global EDF due to Dhall's effect~\cite{dhall , a global queue also makes task acceptance testing more difficult: When a task is added to the global task queue and given its priority, it is not immediately clear which worker will eventually pick up the task, at what time, and whether this is early enough to meet the deadline.}
elements.sent-2411-1861: It would be necessary to simulate separate run queues for each worker to find the next worker to become free when the new task eventually becomes the highest priority task in the global queue.
elements.sent-2411-1862: Since the scheduler must decide whether it can guarantee the timely execution of tasks as they arrive, partitioned scheduling is more appropriate.
elements.sent-2411-1863: Partitioned EDF requires an algorithm or heuristic to assign a task to a worker when multiple workers can meet the task deadline.
elements.sent-2411-1864: First-fit, worst-fit, and best-fit are the heuristics considered.
elements.sent-2411-1865: Which one to use will depend on the circumstances and will be further explored in the evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-1866: When a new task arrives at time $t$, the scheduler adjusts the deadline set by the client to account for network latency.
elements.sent-2411-1867: In our setting, the communication delay between a client and the scheduler is roughly the same as between a worker and the client.
elements.sent-2411-1868: Therefore, the scheduler uses the delay that occurs in the client-scheduler connection to calculate the expected delay $d_\text{exp}$ that will occur when the worker sends the task result to the client: The client provides the connection setup time $t_\text{cs}$ and the initial time to deadline $t_r$ at the time the client generated the task in the task message. \begin{equation}d_\text{exp}=t_r-T_d-t\end{equation} However, in a high latency environment with short task execution times, it may take longer to establish the connection than to complete the task computation.
elements.sent-2411-1869: In this case, the difference between $t_\text{cs}$ and $t_w$ must also be considered for the delay adjustment $d_\text{adj}$: \begin{equation}d_\text{adj} = \begin{cases} d_\text{exp}+t_\text{cs}-t_w \text{, }t_\text{cs} > t_w d_\text{exp} \text{, else} \end{cases}\end{equation} The adjusted delay is then multiplied by the scheduler's uncertainty factor $\mathcal{U}$ to account for latency jitter.
elements.sent-2411-1870: Finally, the resulting value is subtracted from the deadline. \begin{equation}T_{d\text{-new}}=T_{d\text{-old}} - \mathcal{U} \cdot d_\text{adj}\end{equation} This adjusted deadline can now be used for scheduling and assessed to see if it leaves enough time for the task result to be sent to the client and not miss the original deadline set by the client.
elements.sent-2411-1871: A first acceptance check is then performed to ensure that the time to the adjusted deadline is greater than the task execution time.
elements.sent-2411-1872: The scheduler maintains a queue for each worker according to the partitioned EDF scheme.
elements.sent-2411-1873: To make a scheduling decision, a copy of each queue is scheduled using EDF and evaluated for potential deadline misses.
elements.sent-2411-1874: Queues with predicted misses are eliminated.
elements.sent-2411-1875: In addition, the overall density of the queue is calculated from the individual task densities.
elements.sent-2411-1876: If the scheduler is configured to use the first-fit strategy to select the worker, it will select the first worker without a missed deadline.
elements.sent-2411-1877: For the other strategies, each worker is tested.
elements.sent-2411-1878: In the case of the best-fit or worst-fit strategies, the scheduler selects the worker with the highest or lowest total density, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-1879: If the new task has the highest priority, the currently running task is preempted.
elements.sent-2411-1880: The scheduler records the elapsed execution time $t_e$ of the task, and the worker saves its context.
elements.sent-2411-1881: If no worker is found for which its queue can be scheduled without predicted missed deadlines, the task is rejected.
elements.sent-2411-1882: We simulate three scenarios to investigate the impact of our architecture on offloaded real-time tasks in wireless IIoT environments.
elements.sent-2411-1883: We explore the limits at which tasks or network environments may or may not meet real-time requirements.
elements.sent-2411-1884: We implemented the proposed architecture and partitioned EDF scheduler in Rust and made the code publicly available\footnote{\url{https://github.com/dos-group/Real-Time-Offloading-Simulator-IIoT .}}
elements.sent-2411-1885: We implemented a reference scheduler for comparison.
elements.sent-2411-1886: It runs EDF on a global task queue, rejecting tasks only if the time to deadline is less than the WCET and without deadline adjustment.
elements.sent-2411-1887: We use Mininet\footnote{\url{https://mininet.org to emulate a network of clients, workers, and the scheduler.}}
elements.sent-2411-1888: The virtual network links between hosts can be configured with arbitrary latency, jitter, and bandwidth limits.
elements.sent-2411-1889: The basic network layout is the same for all test setups: Client hosts are connected wirelessly to an access point, which is connected to a switch via Ethernet.
elements.sent-2411-1890: Edge cluster hosts (running workers and the scheduler) are also connected to this switch via Ethernet.
elements.sent-2411-1891: All tasks in a test run have the same predefined execution time of 30 seconds and have been run 5 times each.
elements.sent-2411-1892: The relative deadlines, on the other hand, are only configured in terms of mean and variance, and are dynamically sampled from a normal distribution by the clients at runtime.
elements.sent-2411-1893: This results in more or less preemptions, depending on the configuration, because the deadlines of the tasks differ accordingly for the same execution time.
elements.sent-2411-1894: The task submission frequency is sampled from a Poisson distribution for which $\lambda = 1$ is configured for all clients.
elements.sent-2411-1895: However, clients only submit one task at a time, so the minimum time between task submissions is limited by the task execution time.
elements.sent-2411-1896: The worst case task execution times are set to 100ms for all experiments.
