elements.sent-2411-1897: As described in Section~\ref{sec:3_3_adjustment , the scheduler is configured with an uncertainty factor $\mathcal{U}$ and the task assignment heuristic.}
elements.sent-2411-1898: The scheduler approximates the worker-to-client latency to be considered when creating the schedule based on the client-to-scheduler latency measured during task submission.
elements.sent-2411-1899: The measured client to worker latency is multiplied by $\mathcal{U}$ to account for variations in latency.
elements.sent-2411-1900: The following test scenarios explore how much variance needs to be accounted for with which uncertainty factor, and the impact this has on overall throughput.
elements.sent-2411-1901: Each scenario is tested with $\mathcal{U}$ ranging from 0.25 to 5.0.
elements.sent-2411-1902: Table~\ref{tab:parameters lists the chosen parameters for the three scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-1903: The first scenario examines the behavior of the system under varying loads.
elements.sent-2411-1904: Ten to fifty clients each create an average of one task per second.
elements.sent-2411-1905: The different task assignment heuristics were compared to see if the tasks were distributed appropriately.
elements.sent-2411-1906: All reported results use the worst-fit allocation strategy, as first-fit and best-fit simply concentrate the workload on a single worker.
elements.sent-2411-1907: All experimental results can be seen in Figure~\ref{fig:throughput .}
elements.sent-2411-1908: We present only the most relevant results for uncertainty factors between 0.5 and 1.25.
elements.sent-2411-1909: For higher values, the acceptance rate drops without any further gain in reliability.
elements.sent-2411-1910: We present the ratio of successfully offloaded tasks, where a submission is considered successful if the scheduler accepts it and the computation result is returned before its deadline has passed.
elements.sent-2411-1911: The results show that as the load increases, the reference miss rate increases, reducing the actual throughput of successful tasks.
elements.sent-2411-1912: Depending on the uncertainty factor used, our approach minimizes the number of missed deadlines for any load.
elements.sent-2411-1913: However, for higher loads and higher uncertainty factors, the number of rejected tasks increases, leading to an expected decrease in relative throughput.
elements.sent-2411-1914: There are few to no missed deadlines in any of the experiments using our design and a success rate of up to 60\% for the highest tested submission load.
elements.sent-2411-1915: Rejected tasks are returned to clients as early as possible for fallback processing.
elements.figure-2411-051: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{images/3_3_offloading/throughput.pdf} \subfloat[\label{fig:scalability-a} Scenario 1]{ } \subfloat[\label{fig:scalability-b} Scenario 2]{ } \subfloat[\label{fig:scalability-c} Scenario 3]{ } \caption{For each scenario, we report the throughput (successfully finished tasks) and the fraction of tasks that have been accepted but missed their deadline. We compare the reference baseline vs. our latency-aware approach at $\mathcal{U}=\{0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.25\}$.} \label{fig:throughput}
elements.sent-2411-1916: Next, we test the effect of task laxity on the acceptance rate and the miss rate.
elements.sent-2411-1917: The lower the laxity, the less communication and scheduling time is available.
elements.sent-2411-1918: Experiments were conducted with laxities ranging from 180ms down to 60ms.
elements.sent-2411-1919: It is evident that task laxity plays an important role in real-time processing.
elements.sent-2411-1920: With sufficient laxity, the proposed design is able to meet all deadlines while rejecting only a small fraction of tasks, resulting in a relative throughput of 95\%.
elements.sent-2411-1921: As the laxity becomes smaller, fewer tasks can be safely accepted.
elements.sent-2411-1922: The proposed design outperforms the reference implementation for all laxities and for uncertainty factors below 1.0, meaning that some risk of missed deadlines must be accepted.
elements.sent-2411-1923: The best results could be achieved with $\mathcal{U = 0.5$, with a successful throughput difference of 5 - 20 percentage points compared to the reference scheduler.}
elements.sent-2411-1924: By choosing $\mathcal{U > 1.0$, missed deadlines can be avoided for all tested laxities.}
elements.sent-2411-1925: However, in the most extreme case of 60ms submission laxity, only 5\% of submissions are accepted to accommodate this predictability.
elements.sent-2411-1926: With $\mathcal{U = 1.0$ and submission laxities above 90ms, more tasks are successful than with the reference scheduler.}
elements.sent-2411-1927: The final scenario tests the behavior of the system under increasing network latencies.
elements.sent-2411-1928: We tested latency variances ranging from 10ms to 50ms.
elements.sent-2411-1929: The results for this scenario show the least impact of increasing the parameter.
elements.sent-2411-1930: Again, we see that higher uncertainty factors lead to higher deadline hit rates and lower task acceptance rates.
elements.sent-2411-1931: The relative throughput of our design outperforms the reference implementation with somewhat risky uncertainty factor values below 1.0.
elements.sent-2411-1932: For very tight deadlines, where the laxity is in the same range as the latency, the real-time scheduler relies heavily on correct latency estimates to generate efficient schedules.
elements.sent-2411-1933: For low-variance latencies, it has been shown that high throughput can still be achieved while preventing missed deadlines and hence gaining overall system predictability.
elements.sent-2411-1934: For pessimistic latency estimates, stronger guarantees of meeting deadlines can be provided.
elements.sent-2411-1935: However, this comes at the expense of throughput, since a correspondingly large number of tasks must be discarded.
elements.sent-2411-1936: Still, high throughput can be achieved if isolated misses are acceptable.
elements.sent-2411-1937: The choice of an acceptable uncertainty factor must be made taking into account client fallback mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-1938: The highest advantages of the proposed design can be seen under high task submission loads, where the reference design is only able to successfully compute 5\% of submitted tasks while missing the deadlines of the remaining 95\% of tasks.
elements.sent-2411-1939: Here, our design has still a relative throughput of 45\% with no deadline misses.
elements.section-2411-035: Removing the Single Point of Failure
elements.sent-2411-1940: In the realm of computing systems, one critical aspect that deserves close attention is the potential occurrence of a \ac{spof .}
elements.sent-2411-1941: An \ac{spof represents a part of a system, which, if it fails, would cause the whole system to stop functioning.}
elements.sent-2411-1942: These failures can occur in any part of the system and can be due to various reasons such as hardware failure, software bugs, network congestion, or even human error.
elements.sent-2411-1943: Addressing this vulnerability is essential, particularly in systems where continuous operation is integral to their purpose, such as real-time systems.
elements.sent-2411-1944: In real-time systems, especially those involving safety-critical operations, the implications of an \ac{spof can be severe.}
elements.sent-2411-1945: Real-time systems are often characterized by stringent timing constraints and, therefore, any component failure can lead to missed deadlines, resulting in potential system failure.
elements.sent-2411-1946: These implications can be even more pronounced in networks used for distributed real-time systems.
elements.sent-2411-1947: In our case, the centralized access and scheduling node presents an \ac{spof .}
elements.sent-2411-1948: When it becomes unavailable, task offloading requests cannot be forwarded and no answers can be returned.
elements.sent-2411-1949: In this section, we present a fault-tolerant architecture for real-time task offloading.
elements.sent-2411-1950: A self-managing network of edge nodes schedules and processes tasks in a distributed manner.
elements.sent-2411-1951: Each node hereby acts as a scheduler and worker and is connected to a wireless access point.
elements.sent-2411-1952: To provide a fault-tolerant system design, some basic architecture paradigms have to be adjusted.
elements.sent-2411-1953: In the following, we introduce the advantages of self-organized wireless networks and distributed scheduling approaches.
elements.sent-2411-1954: The goal of the distributed approach is to create an environment where each participating node is able to autonomously provide computation capabilities while cooperating with any other nodes in the same network.
elements.sent-2411-1956: In centralized networks, a single central node oversees the communication between all other nodes.
elements.sent-2411-1957: This design can create potential issues such as single points of failure and bottlenecks given the central node's crucial role.
elements.sent-2411-1958: A fundamental aspect of \acp{son is the absence of fixed infrastructure; each node operates independently and can connect freely with other nodes.}
elements.sent-2411-1959: This allows the network to adapt and reorganize itself as nodes join or leave, ensuring constant connectivity and operational continuity~\cite{peltomaki2011algorithms .}
elements.sent-2411-1960: In \acp{son , the functions of the network are distributed across various nodes.}
elements.sent-2411-1961: Each node can operate as a host for local data and services and act as a router, forwarding data from other nodes.
elements.sent-2411-1962: As a result, if a node fails or leaves the network, the remaining nodes can reconfigure themselves and route data through different paths, thereby ensuring the network continues to function seamlessly.
elements.sent-2411-1964: Distributing the load over multiple nodes also helps to avoid performance bottlenecks.
elements.sent-2411-1965: However, despite the numerous advantages, \acp{son also bring about their unique challenges.}
elements.sent-2411-1966: The dynamic and decentralised nature of \acp{son results in frequent changes in network topology.}
elements.sent-2411-1967: This necessitates an efficient routing protocol that can quickly adapt to changes and ensure data is efficiently transmitted between nodes.
elements.sent-2411-1968: Additionally, because each node has its own resources and capabilities, coordinating and synchronizing activities amongst nodes can be complex.
elements.sent-2411-1969: Another challenge lies in the fairness of resource allocation.
elements.sent-2411-1970: Without a central authority to oversee and enforce resource distribution, ensuring that all nodes contribute and consume resources fairly can be a complex task.
elements.sent-2411-1971: Consequently, a suitable consensus and coordination mechanism is required to manage resource allocation.
elements.sent-2411-1972: Unlike centralized scheduling algorithms, where a single node or scheduler takes on the responsibility of managing resources and tasks, \acp{dsa decentralize this responsibility.}
elements.sent-2411-1973: Each node in the distributed system has the ability to allocate its resources and manage its tasks with local scheduling.
elements.sent-2411-1974: Decisions are made based on localized information, although nodes may communicate to share load or information when necessary.
elements.sent-2411-1975: This decentralized approach offers several advantages: \begin{compactenum \item \acp{dsa improve system scalability by sharing the responsibility of managing tasks and resources as the network expands and more nodes are added.}}
elements.sent-2411-1976: Even if a node fails or leaves the network, the other nodes continue with their local scheduling, thereby keeping the system operational.
elements.sent-2411-1977: Each node makes its scheduling decisions, eliminating the need for constant communication with a central scheduler.
elements.sent-2411-1978: One major challenge is balancing local and global optimization.
elements.sent-2411-1979: While each node can make decisions based on its own information, these decisions may not result in the best overall solution or performance for the entire system.
elements.sent-2411-1980: This is connected to maintaining consistency among nodes.
elements.sent-2411-1981: In real-time systems, where tasks have strict timing constraints, ensuring all nodes have a synchronized and accurate perception of time is crucial.
elements.sent-2411-1982: This becomes more complex in a distributed setting and requires appropriate synchronization protocols.
elements.sent-2411-1983: Constant communication between nodes to maintain synchronized states consumes network resources and might result in increased latency variance.
elements.sent-2411-1984: Therefore, finding efficient synchronization mechanisms that minimize this overhead becomes imperative in the design of \acp{dsa , particularly in real-time systems operating within \acp{son .}}
elements.sent-2411-1985: In the following sections, we present a self-managing distributed architecture for real-time task offloading based on the centralized architecture in Section~\ref{sec:3_3_sysarch .}
elements.sent-2411-1986: In the context of the proposed system for managing offloaded real-time tasks in self-organized wireless networks, the architectural blueprint plays a critical role in tracing the underlying operational mechanisms and their interactions.
elements.sent-2411-1987: The system's architecture consists of three significant modules: Client devices, \acp{ap , and edge nodes containing a worker and a scheduler each.}
elements.sent-2411-1988: The subsequent sections will probe deeper into the specifics of the architectural elements.
elements.sent-2411-1989: The communication and interaction between the various components of the system form the architecture backbone.
elements.sent-2411-1990: The edge nodes maintain a dedicated wired network (LAN) for internal communication while being part of a broader network that comprises devices and Access Points.
elements.sent-2411-1991: The client devices in the system send offloading requests to the 'closest' node they are aware of.
elements.sent-2411-1992: This 'closeness' is determined not by physical proximity, but is based on the latency of communication.
elements.sent-2411-1993: This mechanism ensures that tasks are offloaded efficiently, taking into account the uncertainty associated with the nondeterministic communication channels between devices and nodes.
elements.sent-2411-1994: Upon receipt of an offload request from a device, the designated node, as shown in \figurename\ref{fig:communication_sequence , takes on the responsibility of distributing the task to the most suitable node for execution.}
elements.sent-2411-1995: This selection process is driven by the scheduling algorithm in use within the system.
elements.sent-2411-1996: This task distribution strategy is instrumental in ensuring that the real-time requirements of the tasks are adequately fulfilled.
elements.sent-2411-1997: When a node accepts an offload request and commences its execution, it sends a notification to the device that originated the request, indicating the initiation of the processing phase.
elements.sent-2411-1998: This interaction forms a feedback loop, keeping the devices informed about the status of their offloaded tasks.
elements.sent-2411-1999: The clients stay mostly unchanged from the original architecture.
elements.sent-2411-2000: Running on mobile devices such as plant floor robots, they change thei rlocation over time.
elements.sent-2411-2001: This mobility directly impacts the association between the devices and \acp{ap , influencing the decisions of task offloading.}
elements.sent-2411-2002: Devices maintain a record of their closest edge node via the latency of the last successful communication between the device and the respective edge node.
elements.sent-2411-2003: Each device keeps a simple array of the three closest nodes based on their communication latency.
elements.sent-2411-2004: When a new node enters the network and reports its status, or an existing node communicates with the device, the respective latency is calculated and the list of closest nodes is updated.
elements.sent-2411-2005: The device identifies the closest edge node to offload the task, based on the last known communication latency with each node.
elements.sent-2411-2006: In case there has been no prior communication with the chosen node, a new TCP socket is opened to the respective edge node.
elements.sent-2411-2007: If communication has occurred before, the existing open socket is reused, preserving network resources and time.
elements.sent-2411-2008: The device then sends the task offloading request to the selected edge node using this TCP connection.
elements.sent-2411-2009: Upon receiving the request, the edge node identifies the most appropriate node (according to the worst-fit strategy) to execute the task and forwards the offload request to it over a dedicated TCP socket.
elements.sent-2411-2010: This receiving node assesses whether it can accept the request.
elements.sent-2411-2011: If it can, it sends an ACCEPT result back to the device using a dedicated TCP socket.
elements.sent-2411-2012: If it is unable to accept the request, it forwards the offload request to the second most appropriate node, and this process continues.
elements.sent-2411-2013: If the last node in the chain is unable to accept the request, it returns a REJECT message to the device using a dedicated TCP socket.
elements.sent-2411-2014: Moreover, in case a device receives no response (either ACCEPT or REJECT) within a defined threshold period, it picks the second-closest edge node and sends the same Offload Request to it accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-2015: The \acp{ap play a vital role in the distributed scheduler model, serving as the connection hubs between the devices and the edge node network.}
elements.sent-2411-2016: In this system, APs establish wireless connections with devices.
elements.sent-2411-2017: Unlike the devices, APs are stationary entities, ensuring constant coverage and efficient utilization of the network space.
elements.sent-2411-2018: They are organized in a grid pattern that spans the physical area, facilitating wide network coverage.
elements.sent-2411-2019: Notably, APs also function as an abstraction layer for the edge node infrastructure, simplifying the offloading process for devices.
elements.sent-2411-2020: By connecting to the APs, devices can offload tasks without needing direct interaction with the complex edge node network.
elements.sent-2411-2021: It is the responsibility of the APs to handle the communication with the edge node network.
elements.sent-2411-2022: In the context of the distributed architecture, the edge nodes function as an interconnected network of computation resources.
elements.sent-2411-2023: They form a self-organized distributed environment.
elements.sent-2411-2024: Computing nodes can join and leave the network based on factors such as availability, resource allocation, and system requirements.
elements.sent-2411-2025: By leveraging socket-based communication, the node is able to receive information regarding the state of the entire network, including node densities and task overloading scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-2026: Once the connection is successfully set up, the node completes its initialization, announces itself to the network and starts to accept task offloading requests.
elements.sent-2411-2027: Every node starts its lifecycle by sending a join request to the network.
elements.sent-2411-2028: The request is then processed by all other nodes, who in turn broadcast their own densities and update their node lists to include the new member.
elements.sent-2411-2029: After a new node joins the network successfully, it sends a message to all the other nodes to signal it is ready to accept offloading requests.
elements.sent-2411-2030: This information is also sent to all active clients and they trigger their process for deciding whether the new node should be considered when creating offloading requests based on the latency of the communication.
elements.sent-2411-2031: A node's density is the queue density of the worker as defined in Section~\ref{sec:3_3_adjustment .}
elements.sent-2411-2032: Node density broadcasts are used to manage and maintain the up-to-date state of the edge worker network in the distributed scheduler.
elements.sent-2411-2033: Each worker node regularly transmits its current task density to the other nodes in the network.
elements.sent-2411-2034: Additionally to the transmission when a new node joins the network, the density information is updated and transmitted to all other nodes when a node accepts a new task.
elements.sent-2411-2035: Furthermore, when a node completes a task, it broadcasts an update, allowing the network to adjust to the node's reduced load.
elements.sent-2411-2036: These transmissions are transmitted over the LAN using a TCP socket.
elements.sent-2411-2037: Hence, they are no broadcasts in the technical sense but rather individual connections to all known nodes.
elements.sent-2411-2038: Worker nodes periodically (in the magnitude of seconds) advertise themselves to the network.
elements.sent-2411-2039: Once received by an \ac{ap , the node advertisement is forwarded to the WiFi subnet where the client machines are connected.}
elements.sent-2411-2040: This propagation ensures that all devices are kept informed about the active edge nodes in the network.
elements.sent-2411-2041: Each device, upon receiving a node advertisement, measures the communication latency to the broadcasting node.
elements.sent-2411-2042: They might consider offloading tasks to a node that has the lowest communication latency, thus reducing task completion time.
elements.sent-2411-2043: It is important to note that this measured latency is end-to-end, from the worker to the client, rather than just from the AP to the device.
elements.sent-2411-2044: When an offload request is received, the worker node determines whether it can accept the job based on its current task schedule.
elements.sent-2411-2045: If the node cannot accept the job due to time constraints, it finds the worker with the least density and forwards the request to it.
elements.sent-2411-2046: If no available node can accept the job, the original node will reject the job and inform the device.
elements.sent-2411-2047: After the completion of a task, the executing worker node reports the result back to the device that originally offloaded the task.
elements.sent-2411-2048: This communication completes the cycle of task offloading and ensures that the computation results are relayed back to the originating device.
elements.sent-2411-2049: The comprehension of the functioning and efficiency of the implemented distributed scheduler for offloaded real-time tasks in self-organized wireless networks is achieved via the conduction of five key simulation scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-2050: Each scenario varies a specific parameter and is designed for enhanced understanding of the system's performance, behaviour, and robustness under different conditions.

elements.sent-2411-2108: This attribute significantly improves network resilience by ensuring that the failure of a single entity does not compromise the entire system.

elements.sent-2411-2122: In this chapter, we investigated the trade-offs and implications of offloading real-time tasks over wireless networks to local edge resources in the context of IIoT environments.
elements.sent-2411-2123: On this basis, a system architecture was designed that integrates networking uncertainties.
elements.sent-2411-2124: Three interacting actors have been conceived: (1) The client machines, which are mobile devices offloading tasks with deadlines.
elements.sent-2411-2125: The focus here was the inclusion of an unreliable network in the creation of the schedule.
elements.sent-2411-2126: This system was implemented and extensively tested.
elements.sent-2411-2127: Given the broadness of the proposed architecture optimizations and adaptations towards specialized use-cases can be applied to the scheduler and real-time networking concepts can be incorporated in future work.
elements.sent-2411-2128: Scheduling algorithms could incorporate network bandwidth, memory consumption, and payload sizes.
elements.sent-2411-2129: Additionally, the system design was extended to be fully distributed.
elements.sent-2411-2130: Edge nodes were used to combine worker nodes and a scheduler each, where all nodes share the same system state information.
elements.sent-2411-2131: The resulting self-managing offloading network successfully demonstrates that such a distributed set-up can effectively mitigate the single point of failure problem of centralized schedulers, ensuring no tasks are lost in the event of sudden \ac{en failure.}
elements.sent-2411-2132: Furthermore, such an event does not affect the overall performance of the system or its real-time guarantees as long as the number of participating edge nodes does not introduce too much additional communication.
elements.sent-2411-2133: When scaling such a system further, the design needs to be segregated in multiple subnetworks.

elements.sent-2411-2164: Addressing the unwanted impact of interrupts is being researched since decades.
elements.sent-2411-2165: A popular example of unwanted effects is priority inversion, where the execution of a high-priority task is preempted by an \ac{isr which belongs to a lower-priority task.}
elements.sent-2411-2166: To understand today's common interrupt handling mechanisms, it is helpful to understand the evolution of those mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-2167: The first part of this section will briefly outline implementations that pioneered concepts which are relevant up until today.
elements.sent-2411-2168: Thereafter, we present scientific works which address the impact of \acp{irq on embedded real-time systems and applications.}
elements.sent-2411-2169: The LynxOS\footnote{\url{https://www.lynx.com/products/lynxos-178-do-178c-certified-posix-rtos \ac{rtos has introduced the concept of running re-entrant network protocol stacks in the priority spectrum of a receiver's process.}}}
elements.sent-2411-2170: By de-multiplexing network traffic at the \ac{nic level, low latency for processing real-time network traffic can be guaranteed.}
elements.sent-2411-2171: Simultaneously, priority inversions caused by packets directed at non-real-time processes is avoided.
elements.sent-2411-2172: LynxOS claims to be the first system built on an architecture that separates \acp{isr and \acp{ist (i.e., light weight kernel tasks)~\cite{bunnell95operating .}}}
elements.sent-2411-2173: The SUN (now Oracle) Solaris \ac{os ~\cite{oracle19oracle is another prominent example for implementing interrupt handling within the priority spectrum of regular process and thread scheduling.}}
elements.sent-2411-2174: Solaris distinguishes real-time, system, and time-sharing scheduling classes.
elements.sent-2411-2175: Processes in the real-time class have a the second-highest priority spectrum, a time quantum, and they are scheduled strictly on the basis of these parameters.
elements.sent-2411-2176: As long as an RT process is ready to run, no SYS or TS process can run.
elements.sent-2411-2177: The system class exists to schedule the execution of special system processes, such as paging or streams (i.e., the networking code).
elements.sent-2411-2178: Processes of the SYS class of processes have fixed priorities below RT.
elements.sent-2411-2179: Regular time-sharing processes are mapped into the lowest priority spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-2180: With these priority spectrums, Solaris was able to effectively isolate real-time processing from networking.
elements.sent-2411-2181: Windows Embedded Compact (Windows CE)~\cite{microsoft13history , is an \ac{os family developed for handheld consumer electronics (CE) devices.}}
elements.sent-2411-2182: Windows CE up to version 5 was based on a microkernel design and has used a slot model for memory management.
elements.sent-2411-2183: Interrupt handling is divided among the kernel and a process running all device drivers.
elements.sent-2411-2184: Starting with version 3, Windows CE has been providing 256 priority levels for thread scheduling.
elements.sent-2411-2185: From the beginning, Windows CE has separated \acp{isr and \acp{ist for I/O and network processing.}}
elements.sent-2411-2186: By running network handlers with a priority lower than the one used for real-time tasks, Windows CE effectively is able to implement isolation and call admission for incoming network activities.
elements.sent-2411-2187: These \acp{os served millions of industrial applications in practice and the influences of their concept can still be found today.}
elements.sent-2411-2188: Nevertheless, applications, their surrounding environments and their underlying hardware have changed.
elements.sent-2411-2189: Nowadays, even entry-level hardware has complex performance features, such as multi-level caching, pipelining, pre-fetching and out-of-order execution.
elements.sent-2411-2190: Manufacturing is heavily cost-optimized and the transistor sizes decrease constantly.

elements.sent-2411-2393: In this way, the amount of interrupts can be reduced while ensuring the timely arrival of critical packets to high-priority tasks.
elements.sent-2411-2394: In addition, packets that do not belong to a real-time task can be filtered before any work is generated.
elements.sent-2411-2395: In an attempt to combine the software- and hardware-based approaches, a hardware-software co-design for a custom network interface controller and corresponding driver is developed.
elements.sent-2411-2396: The central goal of the design is to address and mitigate the problems real-time systems face when connected to IP networks.
elements.sent-2411-2397: Advantageous aspects of both solutions are combined with new techniques developed in this thesis into a holistic design.
elements.sent-2411-2398: A working implementation developed in parallel with this thesis is presented and outlined, providing an example of how such a combined software/hardware approach can be realized in practice.
elements.sent-2411-2399: Furthermore, the implementation is used in an experiment to provide empirical evidence of its effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-2400: The results show that the implementation prevents priority inversion and succeeds in providing predictable resources for high-priority tasks even under high load.
elements.sent-2411-2401: Offloading is a common method to address the resource and performance limitations of networked embedded devices, which are becoming more prevalent in industrial environments.
elements.sent-2411-2402: It involves transferring computationally intensive tasks to a more powerful device on the network, often located nearby and using wireless communications.
elements.sent-2411-2403: As mentioned earlier, devices used in most industrial scenarios operate under real-time constraints.
elements.sent-2411-2404: Offloading real-time tasks over a wireless network with latency uncertainties presents challenges.
elements.sent-2411-2405: We seek to better understand these challenges by proposing a system architecture and scheduler for real-time task offloading in wireless IIoT environments.
elements.sent-2411-2406: Our design was able to prevent missed deadlines under heavy load and network uncertainties, and outperformed a reference scheduler in terms of successful task throughput.
elements.sent-2411-2407: Specifically, under high task load, where the reference scheduler had a success rate of 5\%, our design achieved a success rate of 60\%.
elements.sent-2411-2408: In addition, the design is extended to be fully distributed, eliminating any single points of failure.
elements.sent-2411-2409: Connecting devices to large networks naturally involves the risk a gateway to the outside entails.
elements.sent-2411-2410: The severity of this risk depends on the environment being considered.
elements.sent-2411-2411: For example, IoT devices connected directly to the internet are much more vulnerable to packet flooding than embedded systems used in closed factory networks.
elements.sent-2411-2412: The second axis of risk is real-time criticality.
elements.sent-2411-2413: Even if a device is located in a secured factory network, a failure of the braking routine of a moving robot is highly critical and must be prevented by decoupled safety systems.
elements.sent-2411-2414: With the contributions of this thesis, we have been able to reduce the risks to real-time properties at the embedded device level.
elements.sent-2411-2415: We have shown that real-time aware reduction of network generated interrupts and dynamic prioritization of network processing can protect time-critical processes.
elements.sent-2411-2416: At the same time, we are cautious about adding IP networking to critical real-time devices and emphasize that, in practice, mission-critical real-time applications should be designed holistically and secured with redundancies to prevent catastrophic damage.
elements.sent-2411-2417: Therefore, the survey in Chapter \ref{cha:1_3_survey is specific to manufacturing and smart factory scenarios, comparing current research (prototypes) with mature standards and practices from critical industrial applications.}
elements.sent-2411-2418: The surveyed works as well as the methods and designs presented in this thesis can only be seen as proofs of concept before intensive testing, standardization and integration.
elements.sent-2411-2419: We propose interrupt and rate limiting methods that can be subject to misconfiguration.
elements.sent-2411-2420: They perform best when configured with the most realistic network load metrics, which requires knowledge of the running processes and their communication behavior.
elements.sent-2411-2421: Future research in this area therefore includes methods for automatic and adaptive parameter configuration: On the one hand, parameter inference from application- and environment-specific expected network loads needs to be supported by an appropriate tool, and on the other hand, autonomous reconfiguration methods benefit our approaches in more dynamic environments.
elements.sent-2411-2422: In Chapter \ref{cha:3_3_offloading we present a system architecture for offloading real-time tasks to local edge nodes.}
elements.sent-2411-2423: Future work should extend this system to enable more computational layers, such as eBPF-based low-latency responses and cloud computing resources for best-effort tasks.
elements.sent-2411-2424: The management of mixed criticalities and deadline sizes is a challenge that remains to be solved.
elements.sent-2411-2425: In addition, enabling embedded real-time devices to make run-time offload decisions based on available computational resources needs more attention for better integration into distributed offloading systems.
